( 6 ) The powers, duties and liabilities of a receiver appointed under this section shall be the same as those of a receiver appointed under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
summary: A court that has issued a proclamation may, after recording reasons, order attachment of any movable or immovable property of the proclaimed person. Attachment can be made immediately if the court is satisfied the person is about to dispose of or remove the property. The section details methods of attachment for debts, movable property, immovable property, and perishable assets, and aligns the receiver’s powers with those under the Civil Procedure Code.
ingredients: Court must record reasons in writing before ordering attachment.
Attachment may be ordered if the court is satisfied (by affidavit or otherwise) that the proclaimed person is about to dispose of or remove his property.
Attachment can be authorized within the district or, with endorsement by the District Magistrate, outside the district.
Methods for attaching movable property or debt: seizure, appointment of a receiver, written prohibition of delivery, or any combination thereof.
Methods for attaching immovable property: through the Collector for revenue land, or taking possession, appointing a receiver, prohibiting rent payment, or any combination thereof.
For livestock or perishable property, the court may order immediate sale and the proceeds follow the court’s order.
Receiver’s powers, duties, and liabilities are the same as those under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 85, Page 3

description: The Court may, on the written request from a police officer not below the rank of the Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police, initiate the process of requesting assistance from a Court or an authority in the contracting State for identification, attachment and forfeiture of property belonging to a proclaimed person in accordance with the procedure provided in Chapter VIII.
summary: Upon a written request from a Superintendent of Police or higher, a court can seek assistance from a foreign court or authority to identify, attach, and forfeit property of a proclaimed person, following the procedure laid down in Chapter VIII.
ingredients: Written request must come from a police officer not below the rank of Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police.
Court initiates assistance from a court or authority in the contracting State.
Assistance sought for identification, attachment, and forfeiture of property of the proclaimed person.
Procedure to be followed as per Chapter VIII.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 86, Page 3

description: ( 1 ) If any claim is preferred to, or objection made to the attachment of, any property attached under section 85, within six months from the date of such attachment, by any person other than the proclaimed person, on the ground that the claimant or objector has an interest in such property, and that such interest is not liable to attachment under section 85, the claim or objection shall be inquired into, and may be allowed or disallowed in whole or in part: Provided that any claim preferred or objection made within the period allowed by this sub-section may, in the event of the death of the claimant or objector, be cont
summary: Any person other than the proclaimed person may, within six months of attachment, claim or object to the attachment of property if they have an interest not liable to attachment. The court will inquire into the claim and may allow or reject it, with provisions for continuation in case of the claimant’s death.
ingredients: Claim or objection must be filed within six months from the date of attachment.
Only persons other than the proclaimed person may file the claim or objection.
The claimant or objector must assert an interest in the property that is not liable to attachment under section 85.
The court shall inquire into the claim or objection and may allow or disallow it wholly or partially.
If the claimant or objector dies during the period, the claim or objection may still be continued.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 87, Page 3

title: Release, sale and restoration of attached property
description: ( 1 ) If the proclaimed person appears within the time specified in the proclamation, the Court shall make an order releasing the property from the attachment. ( 2 )  If  the  proclaimed  person  does  not  appear  within the  time  specified  in  the proclamation,  the  property  under  the  attachment  shall  be  at  the  disposal  of  the  State Identification and attachment  of property  of proclaimed person. Claims  and objections  to attachment. Release,  sale and restoration  of attached property.
summary: If the proclaimed person appears within the stipulated time, the court orders release of the attached property. If the person fails to appear, the property is disposed of by the State, subject to the provisions herein.
ingredients: Proclaimed person must appear within the time specified in the proclamation to obtain release of property.
If the proclaimed person does not appear, the property is placed at the disposal of the State.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 88, Page 3

title: Appeal from order rejecting application for restoration of attached property
description: Any person referred to in sub-section ( 3 ) of section 88, who is aggrieved by any refusal to deliver property or the proceeds of the sale thereof may appeal to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from the sentences of the first-mentioned Court.
summary: A person aggrieved by refusal to deliver attached property or its sale proceeds may appeal to the appropriate appellate court as per normal appellate jurisdiction.
ingredients: The aggrieved person must be referred to in sub‑section (3) of Section 88.
The appeal must be made to the court that normally hears appeals from the original court's sentences.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 89, Page 3

title: Power to issue warrant in lieu of, or in addition to, summons
description: A  Court  may,  in  any  case  in  which  it  is  empowered  by  this  Sanhita  to  issue  a summons for the appearance of any person, issue, after recording its reasons in writing, a warrant for his arrest— ( a ) if, either before the issue of such summons, or after the issue of the same but before the time fixed for his appearance, the Court sees reason to believe that he has absconded or will not obey the summons; or ( b ) if at such time he fails to appear and the summons is proved to have been duly served in time to admit of his appearing in accordance therewith and no reasonable excuse is offered for such failure.
summary: The court may issue an arrest warrant, either instead of or together with a summons, when it believes the person will abscond or fails to appear despite proper service of the summons and no reasonable excuse.
ingredients: Court must be empowered by the Sanhita to issue a summons for the person's appearance.
Court must record reasons in writing for issuing the warrant.
Condition (a): Court believes the person has absconded or will not obey the summons, either before or after the summons is issued but before the appearance date.
Condition (b): Person fails to appear after a duly served summons and offers no reasonable excuse.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 90, Page 4

title: Power to take bond for appearance
description: This Clause provides any person for whose appearance or arrest the officer presiding in any Court is empowered to issue a summons or warrant, is present in such Court, such officer may require such person to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for his appearance in such Court, or any other Court to which the case may be transferred for trial.
summary: When a person appears before a court that can issue a summons or warrant, the presiding officer may require that person to execute a bond—either with or without sureties—to ensure appearance in that court or any other court where the case is transferred.
ingredients: Person is present in the Court where the officer can issue a summons or warrant
Officer may require the person to execute a bond
Bond may be with or without sureties
Bond is for appearance in that Court or any other Court to which the case may be transferred
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 91, Page 4

title: Arrest on breach of bond for appearance
description: When any person who is bound by any bond taken under this Sanhita to appear before a Court, does not appear, the officer presiding in such Court may issue a warrant directing that such person be arrested and produced before him.
summary: If a person who has executed a bond for appearance fails to appear, the presiding officer may issue a warrant for his arrest and production before the court.
ingredients: The person must be bound by a bond taken under the Sanhita to appear before a court.
The person fails to appear as required.
The presiding officer may issue a warrant for arrest and production before the court.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 92, Page 4

description: Hiring, employing or engaging a child to commit an offence. imprisonment of either description or fine provided for that offence as if the offence has been committed by such person himself According as offence committed is cognizable or
summary: Using a child to commit an offence: liable to the same punishment as if the child had committed it, depending on whether the offence is cognizable. Cognizable status depends on the underlying offence.
ingredients: All summonses and arrest warrants issued under the BNSS are subject to the provisions of this chapter regarding issue, service, and execution.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 93, Page 4

title: Summons to produce document or other thing
description: ( 1 ) Whenever any Court or any officer in charge of a police station considers that the  production  of  any  document,  electronic  communication,  including  communication devices which is likely to contain digital evidence or other thing is necessary or desirable for the purposes of any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, by or before such Court or officer, such Court or officer may, by a written order, either in physical form or in electronic form, require the person in whose possession or power such document or thing is believed to be, to attend and produce it, or to produce it, at the time and place stated in the summons or order. ( 2 ) Any person required under this section merely to produce a document, or other thing shall be deemed to have complied with the requisition if he causes such document or thing to be produced instead of attending personally to produce the same. ( 3 ) Nothing in this section shall be deemed— ( a ) to affect sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 or the Bankers' Books Evidence Act, 1891; or ( b ) to apply to a letter, postcard, or other document or any parcel or thing in the custody  of  the  postal  authority.
summary: A court or police officer may issue a written summons ordering a person to produce a document, electronic communication, or device needed for investigation or trial. Production can be by personal appearance or by causing the item to be produced. Certain evidentiary statutes and postal items are excluded.
ingredients: Court or police officer must consider the production of a document/electronic communication/device necessary or desirable for investigation, inquiry, trial, or other proceeding.
A written order (physical or electronic) must be issued requiring the person to attend and produce the item, or to cause its production, at the specified time and place.
A person is deemed to have complied if the item is produced without personal attendance.
The section does not affect sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 or the Bankers' Books Evidence Act, 1891.
The section does not apply to letters, postcards, or any parcel or thing in the custody of the postal authority.
exceptions: Nothing in this section shall be deemed— ( a ) to affect sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 or the Bankers' Books Evidence Act, 1891; or ( b ) to apply to a letter, postcard, or other document or any parcel or thing in the custody of the postal authority.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 94, Page 4

title: Production of documents, parcels or things in custody of postal authority
description: ( 1 ) If any document, parcel or thing in the custody of a postal authority is, in the opinion of the District Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate, Court of Session or High Court wanted for the purpose of any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, such Magistrate or Court may require the postal authority to deliver the document, parcel or thing to such person as the Magistrate or Court directs. ( 2 ) If any such document, parcel or thing is, in the opinion of any other Magistrate, whether Executive or Judicial, or of any Commissioner of Police or District Superintendent of Police, wanted for any such purpose, he may require the postal authority to cause search to be made for and to detain such document, parcel or thing pending the order of a District Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate or Court under sub-section ( 1 ).
summary: Higher courts may direct the postal authority to deliver documents, parcels, or items needed for investigation or trial. Other magistrates or police officials may order the postal authority to search for and detain such items pending direction from a higher court.
ingredients: If a document, parcel or thing in postal custody is needed for investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding, a District Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate, Court of Session or High Court may direct the postal authority to deliver it.

Any other magistrate (executive or judicial) or Commissioner of Police/District Superintendent of Police may order the postal authority to search for and detain the item pending an order from a District Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate or Court under sub‑section (1).
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 95, Page 4

title: Search‑warrants
description: ( 1 ) ( a ) Where any Court has reason to believe that a person to whom a summons order  under  section  94  or  a  requisition  under  sub-section  ( 1 )  of  section  95  has  been,  or might be, addressed, will not or would not produce the document or thing as required by such summons or requisition; or ( b ) where such document or thing is not known to the Court to be in the possession of any person; or ( c ) where the Court considers that the purposes of any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under  this  Sanhita  will  be  served  by  a  general  search  or  inspection,  it  may  issue  a search-warrant; and the person to whom such warrant is directed, may search or inspect in accordance therewith and t
summary: A court may issue a search‑warrant if it believes a person will not produce a required document, if the document's location is unknown, or if a general search is needed for an inquiry or trial.
ingredients: Court must have reason to believe the person addressed by a summons under Section 94 or requisition under Section 95 will not produce the required document or thing.
Court may issue a warrant when the document or thing is not known to be in anyone's possession.
Court may issue a warrant when a general search or inspection is deemed necessary for the purposes of an inquiry, trial, or other proceeding.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 96, Page 4

description: ( 1 ) If a District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate or Magistrate of the first class, upon information and after such inquiry as he thinks necessary, has reason to believe that any place is used for the deposit or sale of stolen property, or for the deposit, sale or production  of  any  objectionable  article  to  which  this  section  applies,  or  that  any  such  objectionable  article  is  deposited  in  any  place,  he  may  by  warrant  authorise  any police officer above the rank of a constable— ( a ) to enter, with such assistance as may be required, such place; ( b ) to search the same in the manner specified in the warrant; ( c ) to take possession of any property or article therein found which he reasonably suspects to be stolen property or objectionable article to which this section applies; ( d ) to convey such property or article before a Magistrate, or to guard the same on the spot until the offender is taken before a Magistrate, or otherwise to dispose of it in some place of safety; ( e ) to take into custody and carry before a Magistrate every person found in such place who appears to have been privy to the deposit, sale or production of any such property or article knowing or having reasonable cause to suspect it to be stolen property or, as the case may be, objectionable article to which this section applies. ( 2 ) The objectionable articles to which this section applies are— ( a ) counterfeit coin; ( b ) pieces of metal made in contravention of the Coinage Act, 2011, or brought into India in contravention of any notification for the time being in force issued under section 11 of the Customs Act, 1962; ( c ) counterfeit currency note; counterfeit stamps; ( d ) forged documents; ( e ) false seals; ( f ) obscene objects referred to in section 292 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; ( g ) instruments or materials used for the production of any of the articles mentioned in clauses ( a ) to ( f ).
summary: A magistrate may issue a search warrant for places suspected of holding stolen property or specified objectionable articles. The warrant authorises police to enter, search, seize, convey, and detain persons and items related to the offence.
ingredients: Magistrate must have reason to believe the place is used for deposit or sale of stolen property or objectionable articles.
Magistrate must act upon information and after any inquiry he deems necessary.

Warrant must specify powers to: (a) enter the place with assistance, (b) search as per warrant, (c) take possession of suspected stolen or objectionable articles, (d) convey or guard the property until a magistrate takes over, (e) take into custody persons suspected of involvement.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 97, Page 4

description: ( 1 ) Where— ( a ) any newspaper, or book; or ( b ) any document, wherever printed, appears to the State Government to contain any matter the publication of which is punishable under section 150 or section 194 or section 195 or section 292 or section 293 or section 297 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the State Government may, by notification, stating the grounds of its opinion, declare every copy of the issue of the newspaper containing such matter, and every copy of such book or other document to be forfeited to Government, and thereupon any police officer may seize the same wherever found in India and any Magistrate may by warrant authorise any police officer not below the rank of sub-inspector to enter upon and search for the same in any premises where any copy of such issue, or any such book or other document may be or may be reasonably suspected to be. ( 2 ) In this section and in section 99,— ( a ) "newspaper" and "book" have the same meaning as in the Press and Registration of Books Act, 1867; ( b ) "document" includes any painting, drawing or photograph, or other visible representation. ( 3 ) No order passed or action taken under this section shall be called in question in any Court otherwise than in accordance with the provisions of section 99.
summary: The State Government can declare newspapers, books, or documents containing punishable matter as forfeited, allowing police to seize them and magistrates to issue search warrants for their recovery.
ingredients: State Government must be satisfied that the publication contains matter punishable under specified sections of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.
State Government issues a notification declaring the copies forfeited.
Police may seize the declared copies wherever found.
A magistrate may, by warrant, authorize police to search premises where the copies may be located.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 98, Page 4

description:
( 1 ) Any person having any interest in any newspaper, book or other document, in respect of which a declaration of forfeiture has been made under section 98, may, within two months from the date of publication in the Official Gazette of such declaration, apply to the High Court to set aside such declaration on the ground that the issue of the newspaper, or Power  to declare certain publications forfeited  and to  issue search- warrants  for same. 11  of  2011. 52  of  1962. 25  of  1867. Application  to High  Court  to set  aside declaration  of forfeiture. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45
29 the book or other document, in respect of which the declaration was made, did not contain any such matter as is referred to in sub-section ( 1 ) of section 98. ( 2 )  Every  such  application  shall,  where  the  High  Court  consists  of  three  or  more Judges, be heard and determined by a Special Bench of the High Court composed of three Judges and where the High Court consists of less than three Judges, such Special Bench shall be composed of all the Judges of that High Court. ( 3 ) On the hearing of any such application with reference to any newspaper, any copy of such newspaper may be given in evidence in aid of the proof of the nature or tendency of the words, signs or visible representations contained in such newspaper, in respect of which the declaration of forfeiture was made. ( 4 ) The High Court shall, if it is not satisfied that the issue of the newspaper, or the book or other document, in respect of which the application has been made, contained any such matter as is referred to in sub-section ( 1 ) of section 98, set aside the declaration of forfeiture. ( 5 )  Where  there  is  a  difference  of  opinion  among  the  Judges  forming  the  Special Bench, the decision shall be in accordance with the opinion of the majority of those Judges.
summary: Any interested person can apply to the High Court within two months to set aside a forfeiture declaration of a newspaper, book, or document, with the matter heard by a special bench.
ingredients: Application must be filed within two months from the Gazette notification of forfeiture.
Application is made to the High Court on the ground that the publication does not contain punishable matter.
If the High Court has three or more judges, a special bench of three judges hears the case; otherwise, all judges of the High Court form the bench.
During hearing, copies of the newspaper may be produced as evidence.
High Court may set aside the forfeiture if not satisfied that the publication contains punishable matter.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 99, Page 4

description: If any District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate or Magistrate of the first class has reason to believe that any person is confined under such circumstances that the confinement  amounts  to  an  offence,  he  may  issue  a search-warrant,  and  the  person  to whom  such  warrant  is  directed  may  search  for  the  person so  confined;  and  such  search shall be made in accordance therewith, and the person, if found, shall be immediately taken before a Magistrate, who shall make such order as in the circumstances of the case seems proper.
summary: A magistrate may issue a search warrant to locate a person confined in circumstances constituting an offence, and the found person must be presented before the magistrate for appropriate orders.
ingredients: Magistrate must have reason to believe the confinement amounts to an offence.
Magistrate issues a search warrant directed at the place of confinement.
Police execute the warrant to search for the confined person.
If the person is found, he/she is taken immediately before the magistrate for further orders.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 100, Page 4

description: Upon complaint made on oath of the abduction or unlawful detention of a woman, or a female child under the age of eighteen years for any unlawful purpose, a District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate or Magistrate of the first class may make an order for the immediate restoration of such woman to her liberty, or of such female child to her husband, parent, guardian or other person having the lawful charge of such child, and may compel compliance with such order, using such force as may be necessary.
summary: When a complaint under oath alleges abduction or unlawful detention of a woman or minor girl, a magistrate can order immediate restoration of liberty and enforce the order with necessary force.
ingredients: Complaint must be made on oath alleging abduction or unlawful detention of a woman or a female child under 18.
Magistrate may order immediate restoration of the woman or child to lawful custody.
Magistrate may use necessary force to compel compliance with the restoration order.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 101, Page 4

description: The provisions of sections 32, 72, 74, 76, 79, 80 and 81 shall, so far as may be, apply to all search-warrants issued under section 96, section 97, section 98 or section 100.
summary: Specific procedural provisions from sections 32, 72, 74, 76, 79, 80, and 81 are extended to search warrants issued under sections 96, 97, 98, and 100.
ingredients: Apply provisions of sections 32, 72, 74, 76, 79, 80, and 81 to search warrants under sections 96, 97, 98, and 100.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 102, Page 4

description: ( 5 ) Where a statement in writing purporting to be signed by the managing director of the corporation or by any person duly authorised by him (by whatever name called) having, or being one of the persons having the management of the affairs of the corporation to the effect that the person named in the statement has been appointed as the representative of the corporation for the purposes of this section, is filed, the Court shall, unless the contrary is proved, presume that such person has been so appointed. ( 6 ) If a question arises as to whether any person, appearing as the representative of a corporation in an inquiry or trial before a Court is or is not such representative, the question shall be determined by the Court.
summary: A written statement signed by the managing director or an authorized person creates a presumption that the named individual is the corporation's representative, unless disproved. Any doubt about a representative's status is to be decided by the court.
ingredients: Court presumes appointment of representative if a written statement signed by managing director or authorized person is filed
Presumption stands unless the contrary is proved
If there is a dispute about a person's status as representative, the court determines the issue
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 103, Page 4

description: 104. When, in the execution of a search-warrant at any place beyond the local jurisdiction of the Court which issued the same, any of the things for which search is made, are found, such things, together with the list of the same prepared under the provisions hereinafter contained, shall be immediately taken before the Court issuing the warrant, unless such place is nearer to the Magistrate having jurisdiction therein than to such Court, in which case the list and things shall be immediately taken before such Magistrate; and, unless there be good cause to the contrary, such Magistrate shall make an order authorising them to be taken to such Court.
summary: When a search-warrant is executed outside the issuing court's jurisdiction, seized items and their inventory must be taken to the issuing court, unless a nearer magistrate is more appropriate, in which case they go to that magistrate. The magistrate may order the items to be transferred to the original court unless good cause exists to the contrary.
ingredients: Execution of a search-warrant beyond the local jurisdiction of the issuing Court.
Seized items and the prepared list must be taken immediately before the Court that issued the warrant.
If the place is nearer to a Magistrate with jurisdiction, the items and list are taken before that Magistrate instead.
The Magistrate must, unless good cause exists, order the items to be taken to the issuing Court.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 104, Page 4

description: 105. The process of conducting search of a place or taking possession of any property, article or thing under this Chapter or under section 185, including preparation of the list of all things seized in the course of such search and seizure and signing of such list by witnesses, shall be recorded through any audio-video electronic means preferably cell phone and the police officer shall without delay forward such recording to the District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate of the first class.
summary: All searches or seizures under this Chapter (or section 185) must be audio‑video recorded, with the list of seized items signed by witnesses, and the recording must be promptly sent to the appropriate magistrate.
ingredients: Conducting a search or taking possession of property under this Chapter or section 185.
Preparation of a list of all seized items and signing by witnesses.
Recording the entire process through audio‑video means, preferably a cell phone.
Forwarding the recording without delay to the District Magistrate, Sub‑divisional Magistrate, or Judicial Magistrate of the first class.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 105, Page 4

description: 106. (1) Any police officer may seize any property which may be alleged or suspected to have been stolen, or which may be found under circumstances which create suspicion of the commission of any offence. (2) Such police officer, if subordinate to the officer in charge of a police station, shall forthwith report the seizure to that officer. (3) Every police officer acting under sub-section (1) shall forthwith report the seizure to the Magistrate having jurisdiction and where the property seized is such that it cannot be conveniently transported to the Court, or where there is difficulty in securing proper accommodation for the custody of such property, or where the continued retention of the property in police custody may not be considered necessary for the purpose of investigation, he may give custody thereof to any person on his executing a bond undertaking to produce the property before the Court as and when required and to give effect to the further orders of the Court as to the disposal of the same: Provided that where the property seized under sub-section (1) is subject to speedy and natural decay and if the person entitled to the possession of such property is unknown or absent and the value of such property is less than five hundred rupees, it may forthwith be sold by auction under the orders of the Superintendent of Police and the provisions of sections 505 and 506 shall, as nearly as may be practicable, apply to the net proceeds of such sale.
summary: A police officer may seize property suspected to be stolen or linked to an offence, must report the seizure to the supervising officer and the magistrate, and may entrust custody to a bonded person if transport or storage is problematic. If the seized property is perishable, owner unknown, and valued under five hundred rupees, it can be auctioned with proceeds governed by sections 505 and 506.
ingredients: Police officer may seize property alleged or suspected to be stolen or linked to an offence.
If the officer is subordinate, he must immediately report the seizure to the officer in charge of the police station.
The officer must immediately report the seizure to the Magistrate having jurisdiction.

If the property cannot be conveniently transported, stored, or retained, custody may be given to any person on execution of a bond to produce the property before the Court and comply with court orders.

Provided that if the seized property is perishable, the owner is unknown or absent, and its value is less than five hundred rupees, it may be auctioned immediately under the Superintendent of Police's orders, with sections 505 and 506 applying to the proceeds.
exceptions: Provided that where the property seized under sub-section (1) is subject to speedy and natural decay and if the person entitled to the possession of such property is unknown or absent and the value of such property is less than five hundred rupees, it may forthwith be sold by auction under the orders of the Superintendent of Police and the provisions of sections 505 and 506 shall, as nearly as may be practicable, apply to the net proceeds of such sale.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 106, Page 4

description: 107. (1) Where a police officer making an investigation has reason to believe that any property is derived or obtained, directly or indirectly, as a result of a criminal activity or from the commission of any offence, he may, with the approval of the Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police, make an application to the Court or the Judicial Magistrate exercising Disposal of things found in search beyond jurisdiction. Recording of search and seizure through audio- video electronic means. Power of police officer to seize certain property. Attachment, forfeiture or restoration of property. (2) If the Court or the Judicial Magistrate has reasons to believe, whether before or after taking evidence, that all or any of such properties are proceeds of crime, the Court or the Magistrate may issue a notice upon such person calling upon him to show cause within a period of fourteen days as to why an order of attachment shall not be made. (3) Where the notice issued to any person under sub-section (2) specifies any property as being held by any other person on behalf of such person, a copy of the notice shall also be served upon such other person. (4) The Court or the Judicial Magistrate may, after considering the explanation, if any, to the show-cause notice issued under sub-section (2) and the material fact available before such Court or Magistrate and after giving a reasonable opportunity of being heard to such person or persons, may pass an order of attachment, in respect of those properties which are found to be the proceeds of crime: Provided that if such person does not appear before the Court or the Magistrate or represent his case before the Court or Judicial Magistrate within a period of fourteen days specified in the show-cause notice, the Court or the Judicial Magistrate may proceed to pass the ex-parte order. (5) Notwithstanding anything contained in sub-section (2), if the Court or the Judicial Magistrate is of the opinion that issuance of notice under the said sub-section would defeat the object of attachment or seizure, the Court or Judicial Magistrate may by an interim order passed ex-parte direct attachment or seizure of such property, and such order shall remain in force till an order under sub-section (6) is passed. (6) If the Court or the Judicial Magistrate finds the attached or seized properties to be the proceeds of crime, the Court or the Judicial Magistrate shall by order direct the District Magistrate to rateably distribute such proceeds of crime to the persons who are affected by such crime. (7) On receipt of an order passed under sub-section (6), the District Magistrate shall, within a period of sixty days distribute the proceeds of crime either by himself or authorise any officer subordinate to him to effect such distribution. (8) If there are no claimants to receive such proceeds or no claimant is ascertainable or there is any surplus after satisfying the claimants, such proceeds of
summary: A police officer may apply to a court, with senior police approval, to dispose of property suspected to be proceeds of crime. The court can issue a 14‑day show‑cause notice, attach the property after hearing, or make an ex‑parte attachment if the person does not appear. The court then directs distribution of the proceeds by the District Magistrate within sixty days, with provisions for surplus or unclaimed proceeds.
ingredients: Police officer, with approval of Superintendent or Commissioner, may apply to the Court or Judicial Magistrate for disposal of property suspected to be proceeds of crime.
Court or Judicial Magistrate may issue a notice calling upon the person to show cause within fourteen days why attachment should not be made.
If the notice mentions property held by another person, a copy of the notice must be served on that other person.
After hearing and considering any explanation, the Court may order attachment of the property found to be proceeds of crime.
Provided that if the person does not appear or present his case within the fourteen‑day period, the Court may pass an ex‑parte attachment order.
If issuing a notice would defeat the purpose of attachment, the Court may make an interim ex‑parte attachment order, remaining in force until a final order is passed.
Upon finding the property to be proceeds of crime, the Court directs the District Magistrate to distribute the proceeds to affected persons.
The District Magistrate must distribute the proceeds within sixty days, either personally or through a subordinate officer.
If there are no claimants or a surplus remains after satisfying claimants, the surplus is dealt with as per the provisions (text truncated).
exceptions: Provided that if such person does not appear before the Court or the Magistrate or represent his case before the Court or Judicial Magistrate within a period of fourteen days specified in the show-cause notice, the Court or the Judicial Magistrate may proceed to pass the ex-parte order.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 107, Page 4

description: (5) Where a trial is related to a person under this section, the deposition and examination of the witness, may, as far as practicable, be recorded by audio-video electronic means preferably mobile phone and such recording shall be kept in such manner as the Court may direct. (6) In prosecution for offences under this Sanhita, voluntary absence of accused after the trial has commenced under sub-section (1) shall not prevent continuing the trial including the pronouncement of the judgment even if he is arrested and produced or appears at the conclusion of such trial. (7) No appeal shall lie against the judgment under this section unless the proclaimed offender presents himself before the Court of appeal: Provided that no appeal against conviction shall lie after the expiry of three years from the date of the judgment. (8) The State may, by notification, extend the provisions of this section to any absconder mentioned in sub-section (1) of section 84 of this Sanhita.
summary: During a trial covered by this section, witness testimony may be recorded by audio‑video means. The accused's voluntary absence after trial commencement does not halt the trial or judgment. Appeals are barred unless the proclaimed offender appears before the appellate court, and no appeal may be filed after three years from the judgment. The State can extend these provisions to other absconders via notification.
ingredients: Witness deposition and examination may be recorded by audio‑video means
Voluntary absence of accused after trial commencement does not stop trial or judgment
No appeal unless proclaimed offender appears before appellate court
No appeal against conviction after three years from judgment
State may extend provisions to other absconders by notification
exceptions: Provided that no appeal against conviction shall lie after the expiry of three years from the date of the judgment.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 108, Page 4

title: Power of Court to impound documents or things
description: Any Court may, if it thinks fit, impound any document or thing produced before it under this Sanhita.
summary: A court has discretionary authority to seize and retain any document or object presented before it under the BNSS.
ingredients: Court must deem it appropriate ('if it thinks fit')
Document or thing must have been produced before the court
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 109, Page 4

title: Reciprocal arrangements for service and execution of summons, warrants and search-warrants
description: ( 1 ) Where a Court in the territories to which this Sanhita extends (hereafter in this section referred to as the said territories) desires that— ( a ) a summons to an accused person; or ( b ) a warrant for the arrest of an accused person; or ( c ) a summons to any person requiring him to attend and produce a document or other thing, or to produce it; or Magistrate may direct search in his presence. Power to impound document, etc., produced. Reciprocal arrangements regarding processes. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 32 ( d ) a search-warrant, issued by it shall be served or executed at any place,— ( i ) within the local jurisdiction of a Court in any State or area in India outside the said territories, it may send such summons or warrant in duplicate by post or otherwise, to the presiding officer of that Court to be served or executed; and where any summons referred to in clause ( a ) or clause ( c ) has been so served, the provisions of section 70 shall apply in relation to such summons as if the presiding officer of the Court to whom it is sent were a Magistrate in the said territories; ( ii ) in any country or place outside India in respect of which arrangements have been made by the Central Government with the Government of such country or place for service or execution of summons or warrant in relation to criminal matters (hereafter in this section referred to as the contracting State), it may send such summons or warrant in duplicate in such form, directed to such Court, Judge or Magistrate, and send to such authority for transmission, as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf. ( 2 ) Where a Court in the said territories has received for service or execution— ( a ) a summons to an accused person; or ( b ) a warrant for the arrest of an accused person; or ( c ) a summons to any person requiring him to attend and produce a document or other thing, or to produce it; or ( d ) a search-warrant, issued by— ( I ) a Court in any State or area in India outside the said territories; ( II ) a Court, Judge or Magistrate in a contracting State, it shall cause the same to be served or executed as if it were a summons or warrant received by it from another Court in the said territories for service or execution within its local jurisdiction; and where— ( i ) a warrant of arrest has been executed, the person arrested shall, so far as possible, be dealt with in accordance with the procedure specified by sections 82 and 83; ( ii ) a search-warrant has been executed, the things found in the search shall, as far as possible, be dealt with in accordance with the procedure specified by section 104: Provided that in a case where a summons or search-warrant received from a contracting State has been executed, the documents or things produced or things found in the search shall be forwarded to the Court issuing the summons or search-warrant through such authority as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf.
summary: Section 110 outlines how courts in the BNSS territories may serve and execute summons, arrest warrants and search‑warrants both within India and in foreign contracting states, and prescribes the procedures to be followed upon receipt of such documents.
ingredients: Court in the territories may desire issuance of summons, arrest warrant, or search‑warrant
For service within India, the court may send duplicate summons or warrant to the presiding officer of the receiving court
If served, Section 70 provisions apply as if the receiving officer were a magistrate in the territories
For service in a contracting State, the court may send duplicate summons or warrant as per Central Government notification
Upon receipt, the court must treat the document as if received from another court in the territories
If a warrant of arrest is executed, the arrested person is dealt with per sections 82 and 83
If a search‑warrant is executed, the seized items are dealt with per section 104

Provided that, when a summons or search‑warrant from a contracting State is executed, the produced documents or items must be forwarded to the issuing court via a Central Government‑specified authority
exceptions: Provided that in a case where a summons or search-warrant received from a contracting State has been executed, the documents or things produced or things found in the search shall be forwarded to the Court issuing the summons or search-warrant through such authority as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 110, Page 4

title: Definitions for Chapter on attachment and forfeiture of property
description: ( a ) "contracting State" means any country or place outside India in respect of which arrangements have been made by the Central Government with the Government of such country through a treaty or otherwise; ( b ) "identifying" includes establishment of a proof that the property was derived from, or used in, the commission of an offence; ( c ) "proceeds of crime" means any property derived or obtained directly or indirectly, by any person as a result of criminal activity (including crime involving currency transfers) or the value of any such property; ( d ) "property" means property and assets of every description whether corporeal or incorporeal, movable or immovable, tangible or intangible and deeds and instruments evidencing title to, or interest in, such property or assets derived or used in the commission of an offence and includes property obtained through proceeds of crime; ( e ) "tracing" means determining the nature, source, disposition, movement, title or ownership of property.
summary: Section 111 provides key definitions—contracting State, identifying, proceeds of crime, property, and tracing—used throughout the chapter dealing with attachment and forfeiture of property.
ingredients: Definition of "contracting State"
Definition of "identifying"
Definition of "proceeds of crime"
Definition of "property"
Definition of "tracing"
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 111, Page 4

title: Letter of request to foreign courts or authorities
description: ( 1 ) If, in the course of an investigation into an offence, an application is made by the investigating officer or any officer superior in rank to the investigating officer that evidence may be available in a country or place outside India, any Criminal Court may issue a letter of request to a Court or an authority in that country or place competent to deal with such request to examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case and to record his statement made in the course of such examination and also to require such person or any other person to produce any document or thing which may be in his possession pertaining to the case and to forward all the evidence so taken or collected or the authenticated copies thereof or the thing so collected to the Court issuing such letter. ( 2 ) The letter of request shall be transmitted in such manner as the Central Government may specify in this behalf. ( 3 ) Every statement recorded or document or thing received under sub-section ( 1 ) shall be deemed to be the evidence collected during the course of investigation under this Chapter.
summary: Section 112 empowers a criminal court to issue a letter of request to a foreign court or authority for examination of persons, production of documents, and forwarding of evidence, when such evidence may exist abroad.
ingredients: Application by investigating officer or superior that evidence may exist abroad
Criminal Court may issue a letter of request to foreign competent court/authority
Letter may request oral examination and recording of statements
Letter may require production of documents or things in possession of the person
Evidence collected must be forwarded to the issuing Court
Transmission method as specified by Central Government
Evidence received is deemed collected during investigation
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 112, Page 4

title: Procedure upon receipt of a foreign letter of request
description: ( 1 ) Upon receipt of a letter of request from a Court or an authority in a country or place outside India competent to issue such letter in that country or place for the examination of any person or production of any document or thing in relation to an offence under investigation in that country or place, the Central Government may, if it thinks fit— ( i ) forward the same to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate as he may appoint in this behalf, who shall thereupon summon the person before him and record his statement or cause the document or thing to be produced; or ( ii ) send the letter to any police officer for investigation, who shall thereupon investigate into the offence in the same manner, as if the offence had been committed within India. ( 2 ) All the evidence taken or collected under sub-section ( 1 ), or authenticated copies thereof or the thing so collected, shall be forwarded by the Magistrate or police officer, as the case may be, to the Central Government for transmission to the Court or the authority issuing the letter of request, in such manner as the Central Government may deem fit.
summary: Section 113 details how the Central Government may act on a foreign letter of request—either by directing a magistrate to summon and record statements or by assigning a police officer to investigate—as well as the forwarding of collected evidence back to the requesting foreign authority.
ingredients: Receipt of foreign letter of request for examination or production of evidence
Central Government may forward the letter to a Chief Judicial Magistrate/Judicial Magistrate
Magistrate may summon person, record statement, or cause production of documents/things
Alternatively, Central Government may send the letter to a police officer to investigate as if offence occurred in India
All evidence or authenticated copies must be forwarded by the magistrate or police officer to Central Government
Central Government transmits evidence to the foreign court or authority as it deems fit
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 113, Page 4

title: Execution of arrest warrant in a contracting State
description: ( 1 ) Where a Court in India, in relation to a criminal matter, desires that a warrant for arrest of any person to attend or produce a document or other thing issued by it shall be executed in any place in a contracting State, it shall send such warrant in duplicate in such form to such Court, Judge or Magistrate through such authority, as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf and that Court, Judge or Magistrate, as the case may be, shall cause the same to be executed. ( 2 ) If , in the course of an investigation or any inquiry into an offence, an application is made by the investigating officer or any officer superior in rank to the investigating officer that the attendance of a person who is in any place in a contracting State is requ
summary: Section 114 provides that a Indian court may issue an arrest warrant to be executed in a foreign contracting State by sending the warrant through a Central Government‑specified channel, and outlines the procedure for seeking the attendance of a person located in such a state.
ingredients: Court in India may desire execution of its arrest warrant in a contracting State
Court must send the warrant in duplicate to the appropriate foreign Court, Judge or Magistrate
Transmission must be through an authority specified by Central Government notification
The foreign Court, Judge or Magistrate must cause the warrant to be executed
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 114, Page 4

description: ( 1 ) Where a Court in India has reasonable grounds to believe that any property obtained by any person is derived or obtained, directly or indirectly, by such person from the commission of an offence, it may make an order of attachment or forfeiture of such property, as it may deem fit under the provisions of sections 116 to 122 (both inclusive). ( 2 ) Where the Court has made an order for attachment or forfeiture of any property under sub-section ( 1 ), and such property is suspected to be in a contracting State, the Court may issue a letter of request to a Court or an authority in the contracting State for execution of such order. ( 3 ) Where a letter of request is received by the Central Government from a Court or an authority in a contracting State requesting attachment or forfeiture of the property in India, derived or obtained, directly or indirectly, by any person from the commission of an offence committed in that contracting State, the Central Government may forward such letter of request to the Court, as it thinks fit, for execution in accordance with the provisions of sections 116 to 122 (both inclusive) or, as the case may be, any other law for the time being in force.
summary: The court can order attachment or forfeiture of property believed to be proceeds of an offence and can seek cooperation from foreign courts for execution. The Central Government may forward foreign requests for attachment or forfeiture to the appropriate Indian court.
ingredients: Court must have reasonable grounds to believe property is derived from an offence.
Court may order attachment or forfeiture under sections 116‑122.
If property is in a contracting State, court may issue a letter of request to that State.
Central Government may forward foreign letters of request to the court for execution.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 115, Page 4

description: ( 1 ) The Court shall, under sub-section ( 1 ), or on receipt of a letter of request under sub-section ( 3 ) of section 115, direct any police officer not below the rank of Sub-Inspector of Police to take all steps necessary for tracing and identifying such property. ( 2 ) The steps referred to in sub-section ( 1 ) may include any inquiry, investigation or survey in respect of any person, place, property, assets, documents, books of account in any bank or public financial institutions or any other relevant matters. ( 3 ) Any inquiry, investigation or survey referred to in sub-section ( 2 ) shall be carried out by an officer mentioned in sub-section ( 1 ) in accordance with such directions issued by the said Court in this behalf.
summary: The court orders a police officer of at least Sub‑Inspector rank to trace and identify property linked to an offence, authorising inquiries, investigations or surveys as directed.
ingredients: Court directs a police officer (rank ≥ Sub‑Inspector) to trace and identify the property.
Steps may include inquiry, investigation, or survey of persons, places, assets, documents, bank records, etc.
Officer must act according to directions issued by the court.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 116, Page 4

description: ( 1 ) Where any officer conducting an inquiry or investigation under section 116 has a reason to believe that any property in relation to which such inquiry or investigation is being conducted is likely to be concealed transferred or dealt with in any manner which will result in disposal of such property, he may make an order for seizing such property and where it is not practicable to seize such property, he may make an order of attachment directing that such property shall not be transferred or otherwise dealt with, except with the prior permission of the officer making such order, and a copy of such order shall be served on the person concerned. ( 2 ) Any order made under sub-section ( 1 ) shall have no effect unless the said order is confirmed by an order of the said Court, within a period of thirty days of its being made.
summary: An officer investigating under Section 116 can order seizure or attachment of property suspected to be concealed or disposed of, but the order must be confirmed by the court within thirty days to be effective.
ingredients: Officer must have reason to believe property may be concealed, transferred, or disposed of.
Officer may order seizure of the property.
If seizure is not practicable, officer may order attachment preventing transfer without prior permission.
Order must be served on the concerned person.
Court must confirm the order within thirty days for it to have effect.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 117, Page 4

description: ( 1 ) The Court may appoint the District Magistrate of the area where the property is situated, or any other officer that may be nominated by the District Magistrate, to perform the functions of an Administrator of such property. ( 2 ) The Administrator appointed under sub-section ( 1 ) shall receive and manage the property in relation to which the order has been made under sub-section ( 1 ) of section 117 or under section 120 in such manner and subject to such conditions as may be specified by the Central Government. ( 3 ) The Administrator shall also take such measures, as the Central Government may direct, to dispose of the property which is forfeited to the Central Government.
summary: The court can designate a District Magistrate or nominated officer as Administrator to receive, manage, and possibly dispose of property ordered for attachment or forfeiture, following conditions set by the Central Government.
ingredients: Court appoints District Magistrate or nominated officer as Administrator.
Administrator receives and manages the attached/forfeited property.
Management is subject to conditions specified by the Central Government.
Administrator may dispose of the property as directed by the Central Government.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 118, Page 5

description: ( 1 ) If as a result of the inquiry, investigation or survey under section 116, the Court has reason to believe that all or any of such properties are proceeds of crime, it may serve a notice upon such person (hereinafter referred to as the person affected) calling upon him within a period of thirty days specified in the notice to indicate the source of income, earnings or assets, out of which or by means of which he has acquired such property, the evidence on which he relies and other relevant information and particulars, and to show cause why all or any of such properties, as the case may be, should not be declared to be proceeds of crime and forfeited to the Central Government. ( 2 ) Where a notice under sub-section ( 1 ) to any person specifies any property as being held on behalf of such person by any other person, a copy of the notice shall also be served upon such other person.
summary: When the court believes property may be proceeds of crime, it issues a show‑cause notice to the affected person (and any holder) to disclose source of income and justify why the property should not be forfeited.
ingredients: Court must have reason to believe property is proceeds of crime.
Court serves a notice to the person affected, giving thirty days to disclose source of income, assets, evidence, and to show cause.
If the notice identifies property held by another person, a copy of the notice must also be served on that other person.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 119, Page 5

description: ( 1 ) The Court may, after considering the explanation, if any, to the show-cause notice issued under section 119 and the material available before it and after giving to the person affected (and in a case where the person affected holds any property specified in the notice through any other person, to such other person also) a reasonable opportunity of being heard, by order, record a finding whether all or any of the properties in question are proceeds of crime: Provided that if the person affected (and in a case where the person affected holds any property specified in the notice through any other person such other person also) does not appear before the Court or represent his case before it within a period of thirty days specified in the show-cause notice, the Court may proceed to record a finding under this sub-section ex parte on the basis of evidence available before it. ( 2 ) Where the Court is satisfied that some of the properties referred to in the show-cause notice are proceeds of crime but it is not possible to identify specifically such properties, then, it shall be lawful for the Court to specify the properties which, to the best of its judegment, are proceeds of crime and record a finding accordingly under sub-section ( 1 ). ( 3 ) Where the Court records a finding under this section to the effect that any property is proceeds of crime, such property shall stand forfeited to the Central Government free from all encumbrances. ( 4 ) Where any shares in a compa
summary: The court evaluates explanations to the show‑cause notice and may, after hearing, declare property as proceeds of crime, forfeiting it to the Central Government; it can also decide ex parte if the person fails to appear.
ingredients: Court considers any explanation to the show‑cause notice and material before it.
Court gives the affected person (and any other holder) a reasonable opportunity to be heard.
If the person does not appear within thirty days, the court may record an ex parte finding.
If the court cannot identify specific properties, it may specify those it believes are proceeds of crime.
Properties declared as proceeds of crime are forfeited to the Central Government free of encumbrances.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 120, Page 5

description: (1) Where the Court makes a declaration that any property stands forfeited to the Central Government under section 120 and it is a case where the source of only a part of such property has not been proved to the satisfaction of the Court, it shall make an order giving an option to the person affected to pay, in lieu of forfeiture, a fine equal to the market value of such part. (2) Before making an order imposing a fine under sub-section (1), the person affected shall be given a reasonable opportunity of being heard. Management of properties seized or forfeited under this Chapter. Notice of forfeiture of property. Forfeiture of property in certain cases. Fine in lieu of forfeiture. 18 of 2013. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45
summary: If a court declares property forfeited but the source of part of it is unproven, the court may allow the person to pay a fine equal to the market value of that part, after giving a reasonable hearing.
ingredients: Court makes a declaration of forfeiture under section 120.
Source of only a part of the property is not proved to the court's satisfaction.
Court may order the person to pay a fine equal to the market value of that part in lieu of forfeiture.
Before imposing the fine, the person must be given a reasonable opportunity to be heard.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 121, Page 5

title: Certain transfers to be null and void
description: This Clause seeks to provide after the making of an order under sub-section ( 1 ) of section 117 or the issue of a notice under section 119, any property referred to in the said order or notice is transferred by any mode whatsoever such transfers shall, for the purposes of the proceedings under this Chapter, be ignored and if such property is subsequently forfeited to the Central Government under section 120, then, the transfer of such property shall be deemed to be null and void.
summary: Any transfer of property after an order under Section 117(1) or a notice under Section 119 is ignored for the chapter's proceedings and is considered null and void if the property is later forfeited under Section 120.
ingredients: Order under sub‑section (1) of Section 117 or notice under Section 119
Transfer of the property by any mode thereafter
Transfers are ignored for the purposes of the chapter's proceedings
If the property is later forfeited under Section 120, the transfer is deemed null and void
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 122, Page 5

title: Procedure in respect of letter of request
description: This Clause seeks to provide every letter of request, summons or warrant, received by the Central Government from, and every letter of request, summons or warrant, to be transmitted to a contracting State under this Chapter shall be transmitted to a contracting State or, as the case may be, sent to the concerned Court in India in such form and in such manner as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf.
summary: All letters of request, summons, or warrants received by the Central Government for transmission to a contracting State must be sent to that State or the appropriate Indian court in the form and manner prescribed by a Central Government notification.
ingredients: Receipt of a letter of request, summons or warrant by the Central Government
Requirement to transmit the document to a contracting State or the concerned Indian court
Transmission must follow the form and manner specified by a Central Government notification
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 123, Page 5

description: Ditto.
summary: Repeats the description for causing hurt by rash and negligent act endangering human life.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 123(a)

description: Ditto.
summary: Repeats the description for causing grievous hurt by rash and negligent act endangering human life.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 123(b)

title: Power of the Central Government to issue notification with regards to the application of Chapter VIII with the contracting State
description: This Clause seeks to provide the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, direct that the application of this Chapter in relation to a contracting State with which reciprocal arrangements have been made, shall be subject to such conditions, exceptions or qualifications as are specified in the said notification.
summary: The Central Government can, via Official Gazette notification, set conditions, exceptions, or qualifications for applying Chapter VIII with any contracting State that has reciprocal arrangements.
ingredients: Central Government's authority to issue a notification in the Official Gazette
Direction that the application of the Chapter to a contracting State is subject to conditions, exceptions, or qualifications
Applicability only where reciprocal arrangements exist with the contracting State
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 124, Page 5

description: (1) When a Court of Session or Court of a Magistrate of the first class convicts a person of any of the offences specified in sub-section (2) or of abetting any such offence and is of opinion that it is necessary to take security from such person for keeping the peace, the Court may, at the time of passing sentence on such person, order him to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for keeping the peace for such period, not exceeding three years, as it thinks fit. (2) The offences referred to in sub-section (1) are— (a) any offence punishable under Chapter VIII of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, other than an offence punishable under section 191 or section 194 or section 195 thereof; (b) any offence which consists of, or includes, assault or using criminal force or committing mischief; (c) any offence of criminal intimidation; (d) any other offence which caused, or was intended or known to be likely to cause, a breach of the peace. (3) If the conviction is set aside on appeal or otherwise, the bond so executed shall become void. (4) An order under this section may also be made by an Appellate Court or by a Court when exercising its powers of revision.
summary: A Court may require a convicted person of specified offences to execute a bond for keeping the peace for up to three years. The bond becomes void if the conviction is later set aside, and appellate or revisional courts may also issue such orders.
ingredients: Court of Session or first‑class Magistrate convicts a person of offences listed in sub‑section (2) or of abetting such offences.
Court is of the opinion that security for keeping the peace is necessary.
Court may order the person to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for a period not exceeding three years.
If the conviction is set aside on appeal or otherwise, the bond becomes void.
An Appellate Court or a Court exercising revision powers may also make an order under this section.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 125, Page 5

description: (1) When an Executive Magistrate receives information that any person is likely to commit a breach of the peace or disturb the public tranquility or to do any wrongful act that may probably occasion a breach of the peace or disturb the public tranquility and is of opinion that there is sufficient ground for proceeding, he may, in the manner hereinafter provided, require such person to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bond with or without sureties, for keeping the peace for such period, not exceeding one year, as the Magistrate thinks fit. (2) Proceedings under this section may be taken before any Executive Magistrate when either the place where the breach of the peace or disturbance is apprehended is within his local jurisdiction or there is within such jurisdiction a person who is likely to commit a breach of the peace or disturb the public tranquility or to do any wrongful act as aforesaid beyond such jurisdiction.
summary: An Executive Magistrate, upon receiving information of a likely breach of peace, may require the person to show cause why a bond for up to one year should not be executed. Proceedings can be initiated when the anticipated breach falls within or beyond the magistrate's jurisdiction.
ingredients: Executive Magistrate receives information that a person is likely to commit a breach of peace or disturb public tranquility.
Magistrate is of the opinion that there is sufficient ground for proceeding.
Magistrate may require the person to show cause why a bond (with or without sureties) for keeping the peace should not be executed.
Bond period may not exceed one year.

Proceedings may be taken when the anticipated breach is within the magistrate's local jurisdiction or when a person likely to commit such breach is within the jurisdiction but the breach may occur beyond it.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 126, Page 5

description: (1) When an Executive Magistrate receives information that there is within his local jurisdiction any person who, within or without such jurisdiction,— (i) either orally or in writing or in any other manner, intentionally disseminates or attempts to disseminate or abets the dissemination of,— (a) any matter the publication of which is punishable under section 150 or section 194 or section 195 or section 297 of the Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, or (b) any matter concerning a Judge acting or purporting to act in the discharge of his official duties which amounts to criminal intimidation or defamation under the Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, (ii) makes, produces, publishes or keeps for sale, imports, exports, conveys, sells, lets to hire, distributes, publicly exhibits or in any other manner puts into circulation any obscene matter such as is referred to in section 292 of the Bhartiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, and the Magistrate is of opinion that there is sufficient ground for proceeding, the Magistrate may, in the manner hereinafter provided, require such person to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for his good behaviour for such period, not exceeding one year, as the Magistrate thinks fit. (2) No proceedings shall be taken under this section against the editor, proprietor, printer or publisher of any publication registered under, and edited, printed and published in conformity with, the rules laid down in the Press and Registration of Periodicals Act, 2023 with reference to any matter contained in such publication except by the order or under the authority of the State Government or some officer empowered by the State Government in this behalf.
summary: If an Executive Magistrate learns of a person disseminating prohibited material or obscene content, and deems there is sufficient ground, he may require the person to show cause why a bond for good behaviour (up to one year) should not be executed. The editor, proprietor, printer or publisher of a lawfully registered publication is exempt unless ordered by the State Government.
ingredients: Executive Magistrate receives information of a person within his jurisdiction who intentionally disseminates prohibited matter (as per sections 150, 194, 195, 297) or obscene matter (section 292).
Magistrate is of the opinion that there is sufficient ground for proceeding.
Magistrate may require the person to show cause why a bond for good behaviour, not exceeding one year, should not be executed.

No proceedings under this section against editor, proprietor, printer or publisher of a publication registered under the Press and Registration of Periodicals Act, 2023, unless ordered by the State Government.
exceptions: No proceedings shall be taken under this section against the editor, proprietor, printer or publisher of any publication registered under, and edited, printed and published in conformity with, the rules laid down in the Press and Registration of Periodicals Act, 2023 with reference to any matter contained in such publication except by the order or under the authority of the State Government or some officer empowered by the State Government in this behalf.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 127, Page 5

title: Security for good behaviour from suspected persons
description: This Clause seeks to provide an Executive Magistrate receives information that there is within his local jurisdiction a person taking precautions to conceal his presence and that there is reason to believe that he is doing so with a view to committing a cognizable offence, the Magistrate may, in the manner hereinafter provided, require such person to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for his good behaviour for such period, not exceeding one year, as the Magistrate thinks fit.
