( d )  A  is  accused  of  obstructing  B,  a  public  servant,  in  the  discharge  of  his  public functions at a given time and place. The charge must set out the manner in which A obstructed B in the discharge of his functions. ( e ) A is accused  of  the murder of B at a given time and place. The charge need not state the manner in which A murdered B. ( f )  A  is  accused  of  disobeying  a  direction  of the law  with  intent  to  save  B  from punishment. The charge must set out the disobedience charged and the law infringed.
summary: Section 236 requires that when the details in sections 234 and 235 are insufficient, the charge must also describe the manner of committing the offence, with specific requirements varying by type of offence.
ingredients: If particulars in sections 234 and 235 are insufficient, include details of the manner of the offence.
For theft, the manner need not be stated.
For cheating, the manner of cheating must be set out.
For false evidence, the specific false portion must be set out.
For obstruction of a public servant, the manner of obstruction must be set out.
For murder, the manner of murder need not be stated.
For disobedience of a law to save another from punishment, the disobedience and the law infringed must be set out.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 236, Page 8

description: In every charge words used in describing an offence shall be deemed to have been used in the sense attached to them respectively by the law under which such offence is  punishable. Particulars  as to  time,  place and  person. When  manner of  committing offence  must be  stated. Words  in  charge taken  in sense  of law  under  which offence  is punishable.
summary: Section 237 clarifies that words in a charge are to be interpreted according to their legal meaning, and reiterates that time, place, person, and manner must be stated where required.
ingredients: Words describing an offence are deemed to have the meaning given by the law under which the offence is punishable.
Particulars of time, place, and person must be included where required.
Manner of committing the offence must be stated where required.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 237, Page 8

description: No error in stating either the offence or the particulars required to be stated in the charge, and no omission to state the offence or those particulars, shall be regarded at any stage of the case as material, unless the accused was in fact misled by such error or omission, and it has occasioned a failure of justice. Illustrations. ( a ) A is charged under section 178 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, with "having been in possession of counterfeit coin, having known at the time when he became possessed thereof that such coin was counterfeit," the word "fraudulently" being omitted in the charge. Unless it appears that A was in fact misled by this omission, the error shall not be regarded as material. ( b ) A is charged with cheating B, and the manner in which he cheated B is not set out in the charge or is set out incorrectly. A defends himself, calls witnesses and gives his own account of the transaction. The Court may infer from this that the omission to set out the manner of the cheating is not material. ( c ) A is charged with cheating B, and the manner in which he cheated B is not set out in  the  charge.  There   were  many  transactions  between  A  and  B,  and  A  had  no  means  of knowing to which of them the charge referred, and offered no defence. The Court may infer from such facts that the omission to set out the manner of the cheating was, in the case, a material error. ( d ) A is charged with the murder of Khoda Baksh on the 21st January, 2023. In fact, the murdered person's name was Haidar Baksh, and the date of the murder was the 20th January, 2023. A was never charged with any murder but one, and had heard the inquiry before the Magistrate, which referred exclusively to the case of Haidar Baksh. The Court may infer from these facts that A was not misled, and the error in the charge was immaterial. ( e ) A was charged with murdering Haidar Baksh on the 20th January, 2023, and Khoda Baksh (who tried to arrest him for that murder) on the 21st January, 2023. When charged for the murder of Haidar Baksh, he was tried for the murder of Khoda Baksh. The witnesses present in his defence were witnesses in the case of Haidar Baksh. The Court may infer from this that A was misled, and that the error was material.
summary: Section 238 states that errors or omissions in a charge are not material unless they mislead the accused and cause a failure of justice, illustrated with various scenarios.
ingredients: An error or omission in stating the offence or required particulars is not material unless the accused is misled.
The error must have caused a failure of justice to be considered material.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 238, Page 8

description: ( 1 ) Any Court may alter or add to any charge at any time before judgment is pronounced. ( 2 ) Every such alteration or addition shall be read and explained to the accused. ( 3 ) If the alteration or addition to a charge is such that proceeding immediately with the trial is not likely, in the opinion of the Court, to prejudice the accused in his defence or the  prosecutor  in  the  conduct  of  the  case,  the  Court  may,  in  its  discretion,  after  such alteration or addition has been made, proceed with the trial as if the altered or added charge had been the original charge. ( 4 ) If the alteration or addition is such that proceeding immediately with the trial is likely, in the opinion of the Court, to  prejudice the accused or the prosecutor as aforesaid, the Court may either direct a new trial or adjourn the trial for such period as may be necessary. ( 5 ) If the offence stated in the altered or added charge is one for the prosecution of which  previous  sanction  is  necessary,  the  case  shall  not  be  proceeded  with  until  such sanction  is  obtained,  unless  sanction  has  been  already  obtained  for  a  prosecution  on  the same facts as those on which the altered or added charge is founded.
summary: Section 239 empowers courts to modify charges before judgment, requiring the accused to be informed, and sets guidelines on proceeding with trial or ordering a new trial, especially where prior sanction is required.
ingredients: Court may alter or add to any charge before judgment.
Alteration/addition must be read and explained to the accused.
If the change is unlikely to prejudice either side, the trial may continue as if the new charge were original.
If the change is likely to prejudice either side, the court may order a new trial or adjourn the trial.
If the altered charge involves an offence requiring prior sanction, the trial cannot proceed until such sanction is obtained, unless already obtained for the same facts.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 239, Page 8

description: Whenever a charge is altered or added to by the Court after the commencement of the trial, the prosecutor and the accused shall be allowed— ( a ) to recall or re-summon, and examine with reference to such alteration or addition, any witness who may have been examined, unless the Court, for reasons to be recorded in writing, considers that the prosecutor or the accused, as the case may be, desires to recall or re-examine such witness for the purpose of vexation or delay or for defeating the ends of justice; Effect  of errors. Court  may alter  charge. Recall  of witnesses when  charge altered. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45 50 ( b ) also to call any further witness whom the Court may think to be material.
summary: Section 240 provides that after a charge is altered during trial, both parties may recall or re‑examine witnesses, subject to court approval, and may also call additional material witnesses.
ingredients: After a charge is altered or added post‑trial commencement, parties may recall or re‑summon any previously examined witness.
Recall or re‑examination is permitted unless the court records in writing that the request is for vexation, delay, or to defeat justice.
Parties may also call any further witness the court deems material.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 240, Page 8

title: Separate charges for distinct offences
description: Clause 241 of the Bill relates to Separate charges for distinct offences. This Clause provides for every distinct offence of which any person is accused there shall be a separate charge, and every such charge shall be tried separately, subject to certain conditions.
summary: Each distinct offence alleged against a person must be charged separately and tried separately, unless specific conditions allow otherwise. This ensures clarity of charges and prevents amalgamation of unrelated offences.
ingredients: Every distinct offence must have its own charge.
Each charge must be tried separately.
Subject to certain conditions (not specified in this clause).
exceptions: Provided that where
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 241, Page 8

description: 242. ( 1 ) When a person is accused of more offences than one of the same kind committed within the space of twelve months from the first to the last of such offences, whether in respect of the same person or not, he may be charged with, and tried at one trial for, any number of them not exceeding five. ( 2 ) Offences are of the same kind when they are punishable with the same amount of punishment under the same section of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or of any special or local law: Provided that, for the purposes of this section, an offence punishable under section 301 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 shall be deemed to be an offence of the same kind as an offence punishable under section 303 of the said Sanhita, and that an offence punishable under any section of the said Sanhita, or of any special or local law, shall be deemed to be an offence of the same kind as an attempt to commit such offence, when such an attempt is an offence.
summary: When a person commits multiple offences of the same kind within a twelve‑month period, they may be charged and tried together, but not for more than five offences. "Same kind" means offences punishable by the same amount under the same provision, with specific inclusions for sections 301, 303 and attempts.
ingredients: Offences must be of the same kind (punishable with the same amount of punishment under the same section).
Offences must be committed within a twelve‑month period from the first to the last offence.
A maximum of five offences may be charged and tried together.
Offences punishable under section 301 are treated as the same kind as those under section 303.
Any offence and its attempt are deemed to be of the same kind.
exceptions: Provided that, for the purposes of this section, an offence punishable under section 301 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 shall be deemed to be an offence of the same kind as an offence punishable under section 303 of the said Sanhita, and that an offence punishable under any section of the said Sanhita, or of any special or local law, shall be deemed to be an offence of the same kind as an attempt to commit such offence, when such an attempt is an offence.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 242, Page 8

description: 243. ( 1 ) If, in one series of acts so connected together as to form the same transaction, more offences than one are committed by the same person, he may be charged with, and tried at one trial for, every such offence. ( 2 ) When a person charged with one or more offences of criminal breach of trust or dishonest misappropriation of property as provided in sub-section ( 2 ) of section 235 or in sub-section ( 1 ) of section 242, is accused of committing, for the purpose of facilitating or concealing the commission of that offence or those offences, one or more offences of falsification of accounts, he may be charged with, and tried at one trial for, every such offence. ( 3 ) If the acts alleged constitute an offence falling within two or more separate definitions of any law in force for the time being by which offences are defined or punished, the person accused of them may be charged with, and tried at one trial for, each of such offences. ( 4 )  If several acts, of which one or more than one would by itself or themselves constitute an offence, constitute when combined a different offence, the person accused of them may be charged with, and tried at one trial for the offence constituted by such acts when combined, and for any offence constituted by any one, or more, of such acts. ( 5 ) Nothing contained in this section shall affect section 12 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
summary: Section 243 allows a person who commits multiple offences in a single transaction to be charged and tried together. It also covers cases where offences are linked to breach of trust, falsification of accounts, multiple legal definitions, or combined acts creating a different offence, while preserving the effect of section 12.
ingredients: Multiple offences committed in a single series of acts forming the same transaction may be charged and tried together.
If offences of criminal breach of trust or dishonest misappropriation are linked with falsification of accounts, all such offences may be charged together.
When alleged acts fall under two or more separate legal definitions, each offence may be charged and tried together.
If several acts combine to form a different offence, the combined offence and each individual offence may be charged and tried together.
The provisions do not affect section 12 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
exceptions: Nothing contained in this section shall affect section 12 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 243, Page 8

description: 244. ( 1 ) If a single act or series of acts is of such a nature that it is doubtful which of several offences the facts which can be proved will constitute, the accused may be charged with having committed all or any of such offences, and any number of such charges may be tried at once; or he may be charged in the alternative with having committed some one of the said offences. Where it is doubtful what offence has been committed. ( 2 )  If  in  such  a  case  the  accused  is  charged  with  one  offence,  and  it  appears  in evidence that he committed a different offence for which he might have been charged under the provisions of sub-section ( 1 ), he may be convicted of the offence which he is shown to have committed, although he was not charge
summary: When the facts are ambiguous and it is unclear which offence(s) apply, the accused can be charged with all possible offences and tried together, or alternatively with any one of them. If evidence later shows a different offence, the court may convict the accused of that offence even if it was not originally charged.
ingredients: If doubt exists about which offence(s) the proved facts constitute, the accused may be charged with all or any of the possible offences.
Any number of such charges may be tried together.
The accused may also be charged in the alternative with a single offence.
If evidence later shows the accused committed a different offence covered by sub‑section (1), conviction may be for that offence even if it was not originally charged.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 244, Page 8

description: (1) When a person is charged with an offence consisting of several particulars, a combination of some only of which constitutes a complete minor offence, and such combination is proved, but the remaining particulars are not proved, he may be convicted of the minor offence, though he was not charged with it. (2) When a person is charged with an offence and facts are proved which reduce it to a minor offence, he may be convicted of the minor offence, although he is not charged with it. (3) When a person is charged with an offence, he may be convicted of an attempt to commit such offence although the attempt is not separately charged. (4) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to authorise a conviction of any minor offence where the conditions requisite for the initiation of proceedings in respect of that minor offence have not been satisfied.
summary: Section 245 allows conviction of a minor offence even if not charged, when the facts support it, and also permits conviction for an attempt. However, it bars conviction if the procedural requirements for initiating the minor offence are not met.
ingredients: If a person is charged with an offence having several particulars and a combination of some particulars proves a complete minor offence, conviction for the minor offence is permitted.
If facts proved reduce the charged offence to a minor offence, conviction for the minor offence is permitted.
A person may be convicted of an attempt to commit the offence even if the attempt is not separately charged.
No conviction of a minor offence is authorised if the conditions required to initiate proceedings for that minor offence are not satisfied.
exceptions: Nothing in this section shall be deemed to authorise a conviction of any minor offence where the conditions requisite for the initiation of proceedings in respect of that minor offence have not been satisfied.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 245, Page 9

description: The following persons may be charged and tried together, namely:— (a) persons accused of the same offence committed in the course of the same transaction; (b) persons accused of an offence and persons accused of abetment of, or attempt to commit, such offence; (c) persons accused of more than one offence of the same kind, within the meaning of section 242 committed by them jointly within the period of twelve months; (d) persons accused of different offences committed in the course of the same transaction; (e) persons accused of an offence which includes theft, extortion, cheating, or criminal misappropriation, and persons accused of receiving or retaining, or assisting in the disposal or concealment of, property possession of which is alleged to have been transferred by any such offence committed by the first‑named persons, or of abetment of or attempting to commit any such last‑named offence; (f) persons accused of offences under section 315 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or either of those sections in respect of stolen property the possession of which has been transferred by one offence; (g) persons accused of any offence under Chapter XII of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 relating to counterfeit coin and persons accused of any other offence under the said Chapter relating to the same coin, or of abetment of or attempting to commit any such offence; and the provisions contained in the former part of this Chapter shall, so far as may be, apply to all such charges: Provided that where a number of persons are charged with separate offences and such persons do not fall within any of the categories specified in this section, the Magistrate or Court of Session may, if such persons by an application in writing, so desire, and if he or it is satisfied that such persons would not be prejudicially affected thereby, and it is expedient so to do, try all such persons together.
summary: Section 246 enumerates categories of persons who may be jointly charged and tried, covering same offences, abetments, multiple offences of the same kind, and related offences. It also allows courts to try separate offences together upon application and satisfaction of non‑prejudicial impact.
ingredients: Persons accused of the same offence committed in the same transaction may be tried together.
Persons accused of an offence and persons accused of its abetment or attempt may be tried together.
Persons accused of more than one offence of the same kind committed jointly within twelve months may be tried together.
Persons accused of different offences committed in the same transaction may be tried together.

Persons accused of offences involving theft, extortion, cheating, or criminal misappropriation and persons accused of receiving, retaining, or assisting in disposal of property transferred by such offences may be tried together.
Persons accused of offences under section 315 relating to stolen property transferred by one offence may be tried together.
Persons accused of offences under Chapter XII relating to counterfeit coin and related offences may be tried together.
If persons charged with separate offences do not fall within the above categories, the court may, upon written application and satisfaction that it will not prejudice them, try them together.
exceptions: Provided that where a number of persons are charged with separate offences and such persons do not fall within any of the categories specified in this section, the Magistrate or Court of Session may, if such persons by an application in writing, so desire, and if he or it is satisfied that such persons would not be prejudicially affected thereby, and it is expedient so to do, try all such persons together.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 246, Page 9

title: Withdrawal of remaining charges on conviction on one of several charges
description: Clause 247 of the Bill relates to Withdrawal of remaining charges on conviction on one of several charges. This Clause provides that a charge containing more heads than one is framed against the same person, and when a conviction has been had on one or more of them, the complainant, or the officer conducting the prosecution, may, with the consent of the Court, withdraw the remaining charge or charges, or the Court of its own accord may stay the inquiry into, or trial of, such charge or charges and such withdrawal shall have the effect of an acquittal on such charge or charges, unless the conviction be set aside, in which case the said Court (subject to the order of the Court setting aside the conviction) may proceed with the inquiry into, or trial of, the charge or charges so withdrawn.
summary: If a person is convicted on one part of a multi‑head charge, the remaining parts may be withdrawn with court consent, resulting in acquittal of those parts unless the conviction is later set aside.
ingredients: A charge may contain multiple heads.
If conviction occurs on one or more heads, the remaining heads may be withdrawn with court consent.
The court may also stay the trial of the remaining heads on its own motion.
Withdrawal results in acquittal of the withdrawn heads unless the conviction is set aside.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 247, Page 9

description: Taking gift, etc., to screen an offender from punishment if the offence be capital. Imprisonment for 7 years and fine. Ditto Ditto Ditto. If punishable with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment for 10 years. Imprisonment for 3 years and fine. Ditto Ditto Ditto. If punishable with imprisonment for less than 10 years. Imprisonment for a quarter of the longest term provided for the offence, or fine, or both. Ditto Ditto Ditto.
summary: Section 248 criminalises offering gifts to shield offenders in capital cases, with imprisonment ranging from a quarter term to seven years depending on the offence's punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment for 7 years and fine.
ingredients: In every trial before a Court of Session, the prosecution must be conducted by a Public Prosecutor.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 248, Page 9

description: Offering gift or restoration of property in consideration of screening offender if the offence be capital. Imprisonment for 7 years and fine. Non-cognizable Ditto Ditto. If punishable with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment for 10 years. Imprisonment for 3 years and fine. Ditto Ditto Ditto. If punishable with imprisonment for less than 10 years. Imprisonment for a quarter of the longest term, provided for the offence, or fine, or both. Ditto Ditto Ditto.
summary: Section 249 penalises offering gifts or property restoration to conceal offenders in capital cases, with imprisonment up to seven years, varying by the underlying offence's severity.
punishment: Imprisonment for 7 years and fine.
ingredients: Accused must appear or be brought before the Court.
Prosecutor must describe the charge.
Prosecutor must state the evidence he intends to rely upon.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 249, Page 9

title: Discharge
description: Clause 250 of the Bill relates to Discharge. This Clause provides that the accused may prefer an application for discharge within a period of sixty days from the date committal under Clause 232 and if, upon consideration of the record of the case and the documents submitted therewith, and after hearing the submissions of the accused and the prosecution in this behalf, the Judge considers that there is not sufficient ground for proceeding against the accused, he shall discharge the accused and record his reasons for so doing.
summary: An accused can apply for discharge within 60 days of committal; if the judge finds insufficient grounds to proceed after reviewing the record and hearing both sides, the accused is discharged with reasons recorded.
ingredients: Application for discharge must be filed within 60 days of committal.
Judge must consider the case record and documents.
Judge must hear submissions from both accused and prosecution.
If insufficient grounds exist, the judge discharges the accused and records reasons.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 250, Page 9

description: (1) If, after such consideration and hearing as aforesaid, the Judge is of opinion that there is ground for presuming that the accused has committed an offence which— (a) is not exclusively triable by the Court of Session, he may, frame a charge against the accused and, by order, transfer the case for trial to the Chief Judicial Magistrate, or any other Judicial Magistrate of the first class and direct the accused to appear before the Chief Judicial Magistrate, or the Judicial Magistrate of the first class, on such date as he deems fit, and thereupon such Magistrate shall try the offence in accordance with the procedure for the trial of warrant‑cases instituted on a police report; (b) is exclusively triable by the Court, he shall frame in writing a charge against the accused within a period of sixty days from the date of first hearing on charge. (2) Where the Judge frames any charge under clause (b) of sub‑section (1), the charge shall be read and explained to the accused present either physically or through electronic means and the accused shall be asked whether he pleads guilty of the offence charged or claims to be tried.
summary: Section 251 directs the judge to either transfer a case to a magistrate when the offence is not exclusively triable by the Court of Session, or to frame a charge within 60 days when it is exclusively triable. It also requires the charge to be read to the accused and the accused to be asked to plead.
ingredients: If the offence is not exclusively triable by the Court of Session, the judge may frame a charge and transfer the case to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or any Judicial Magistrate of the first class.
The transferred magistrate shall try the offence following the procedure for warrant‑cases instituted on a police report.
If the offence is exclusively triable by the Court of Session, the judge must frame a written charge within 60 days from the first hearing on charge.
When a charge is framed under clause (b), it must be read and explained to the accused, either physically or electronically.
The accused must be asked whether he pleads guilty or claims to be tried.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 251, Page 9

title: Conviction on plea of guilty
description: Clause 252 of the Bill relates to Conviction on plea of guilty. This Clause provides that the accused pleads guilty, the Judge shall record the plea and may, in his discretion, convict him thereon.
summary: If the accused pleads guilty, the judge records the plea and may, at his discretion, convict the accused on that basis.
ingredients: Accused must plead guilty.
Judge records the plea.
Judge may, at discretion, convict the accused.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 252, Page 9

description: Public servant disobeying a direction of law with intent to save person from punishment, or property from forfeiture. Imprisonment for 2 years, or fine, or both. Non-cognizable Ditto Any Magistrate.
summary: Section 253 penalises public servants who defy legal directions to protect a person or property from punishment or forfeiture, with up to two years imprisonment or a fine.
punishment: Imprisonment for 2 years, or fine, or both.
ingredients: If the accused refuses to plead or does not plead, the procedure to be followed is prescribed (text incomplete).
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 253, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) On the date so fixed, the Judge shall proceed to take all such evidence as may be produced in support of the prosecution: Provided that evidence of a witness under this sub-section may be recorded by audio-video electronic means. ( 2 ) The deposition of evidence of any police officer or public servant may be taken through audio-video electronic means. ( 3 ) The Judge may, in his discretion, permit the cross-examination of any witness to be deferred until any other witness or witnesses have been examined or recall any witness for further cross-examination.
summary: The Judge records prosecution evidence on the fixed date, may use audio‑video recording for witnesses and police officers, and can defer or recall cross‑examination of witnesses at his discretion.
ingredients: Judge fixes a date for taking prosecution evidence
Evidence may be recorded by audio‑video means
Deposition of police officers/public servants may be recorded by audio‑video means
Judge may defer cross‑examination of a witness or recall a witness for further cross‑examination
exceptions: evidence of a witness under this sub-section may be recorded by audio-video electronic means
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 254, Page 9

title: Acquittal
description: Clause 255 of the Bill relates to Acquittal. This Clause provides that after taking the evidence for the prosecution, examining the accused and hearing the prosecution and the defence on the point, the Judge considers that there is no evidence that the accused committed the offence, the Judge shall record an order of acquittal.
summary: When, after hearing both sides, the judge finds no evidence proving the accused's guilt, an order of acquittal must be recorded.
ingredients: Judge takes prosecution evidence.
Judge examines the accused.
Judge hears both prosecution and defence.
If no evidence of guilt is found, judge records acquittal.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 255, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) Where the accused is not acquitted under section 255, he shall be called upon to enter on his defence and adduce any evidence he may have in support thereof. ( 2 ) If the accused puts in any written statement, the Judge shall file it with the record. ( 3 ) If the accused applies for the issue of any process for compelling the attendance of any witness or the production of any document or thing, the Judge shall issue such process unless he considers, for reasons to be recorded, that such application should be refused on the ground that it is made for the purpose of vexation or delay or for defeating the ends of justice.
summary: If not acquitted, the accused must present his defence and may submit written statements. He may also seek court process to compel witnesses or documents, unless the Judge records that the request is vexatious, intended to cause delay, or to defeat justice.
ingredients: Accused enters defence and adduces evidence
Written statements by the accused are filed with the record
Accused may apply for process to compel witness attendance or document production
Judge may refuse such application if it is for vexation, delay, or defeating justice, with reasons recorded
exceptions: that such application should be refused on the ground that it is made for the purpose of vexation or delay or for defeating the ends of justice
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 256, Page 9

description: Intentional omission to apprehend on the part of a public servant bound by law to apprehend an offender, if the offence be capital. Imprisonment for 7 years, with or without fine. According as the offence in relation to which such omission has been made is cognizable or non- cognizable. Ditto Ditto. If punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for 10 years. Imprisonment for 3 years, with or without fine. Cognizable Ditto Ditto. If punishable with imprisonment for less than 10 years. Imprisonment for 2 years, with or without fine. Ditto Ditto Ditto.
summary: Section 257 addresses willful failure by public servants to apprehend offenders in capital cases, with imprisonment ranging from two to seven years based on the offence's severity.
punishment: Imprisonment for 7 years, with or without fine.
ingredients: Defence witness examination completed
Prosecutor sums up the case
Accused or pleader may reply
If a point of law is raised, prosecution may submit on that point with Judge’s permission
exceptions: where any point of law is raised by the accused or his pleader, the prosecution may, with the permission of the Judge, make his submissions with regard to such point of law
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 257, Page 9

description: Intentional omission to apprehend on the part of a public servant bound by law to apprehend person under sentence of a Court of Justice if under sentence of death. Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for 14 years, with or without fine. Ditto Non- bailable Court of Session. If under sentence of imprisonment for life or imprisonment for 10 years, or upwards. Imprisonment for 7 years, with or without fine. Ditto Ditto Magistrate of the first class. If under sentence of imprisonment for less than 10 years
summary: Section 258 penalises deliberate failure by public servants to apprehend persons sentenced to death, with life imprisonment or up to 14 years, and specifies higher penalties for other sentencing categories.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for 14 years, with or without fine.
ingredients: Judge hears arguments and points of law
Judge delivers judgment within 30 days, extendable to 60 days for specific reasons
If accused is convicted, Judge hears sentencing questions unless section 401 applies
Judge passes sentence according to law
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 258, Page 9

description: In a case where a previous conviction is charged under the provisions of sub-section ( 7 ) of section 234, and the accused does not admit that he has been previously convicted as alleged in the charge, the Judge may, after he has convicted the said accused under section 252 or section 258, take evidence in respect of the alleged previous conviction, and shall record a finding thereon: Conviction on plea of guilty. Date for prosecution evidence. Evidence for prosecution. Acquittal. Entering upon defence. Arguments. Judgment of acquittal or conviction. Previous conviction.
summary: When a prior conviction is alleged but not admitted, after convicting the accused under section 252 or 258, the Judge may take evidence about the prior conviction and record a finding.
ingredients: Alleged prior conviction under sub‑section (7) of section 234
Accused does not admit prior conviction
After conviction under section 252 or 258, Judge may take evidence of prior conviction
Judge records finding on the prior conviction
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 259, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) A Court of Session taking cognizance of an offence under sub-section ( 1 ) of section 222 shall try the case in accordance with the procedure for the trial of warrant-cases instituted otherwise than on a police report before a Court of Magistrate: Provided that the person against whom the offence is alleged to have been committed shall, unless the Court of Session, for reasons to be recorded, otherwise directs, be examined as a witness for the prosecution. ( 2 ) Every trial under this section shall be held in camera if either party thereto so desires or if the Court thinks fit so to do. ( 3 ) If, in any such case, the Court discharges or acquits all or any of the accused and is of opinion that there was no reasonable cause for making the accusation against them or any of them, it may, by its order of discharge or acquittal, direct the person against whom the offence was alleged to have been committed (other than the President, Vice-President or the Governor of a State or the Administrator of a Union territory) to show cause why he should not pay compensation to such accused or to each or any of such accused, when there are more than one. ( 4 ) The Court shall record and consider any cause which may be shown by the person so directed, and if it is satisfied that there was no reasonable cause for making the accusation, it may, for reasons to be recorded, make an order that compensation to such amount not exceeding five thousand rupees, as it may determine, be paid by such person to the accused or to each or any of them. ( 5 ) Compensation awarded under sub-section ( 4 ) shall be recovered as if it were a fine imposed by a Magistrate. ( 6 ) No person who has been directed to pay compensation under sub-section ( 4 ) shall, by reason of such order, be exempted from any civil or criminal liability in respect of the complaint made under this section: Provided that any amount paid to an accused person under this section shall be taken into account in awarding compensation to such person in any subsequent civil suit relating to the same matter. ( 7 ) The person who has been ordered under sub-section ( 4 ) to pay compensation may appeal from the order, in so far as it relates to the payment of compensation, to the High Court. ( 8 ) When an order for payment of compensation to an accused person is made, the compensation shall not be paid to him before the period allowed for the presentation of the appeal has elapsed, or, if an appeal is presented, before the appeal has been decided.
summary: A Court of Session trying a warrant‑case not based on a police report follows magistrate procedures, may hold the trial in camera, and can order compensation from the alleged offender if the accusation is found unreasonable. Compensation is capped at Rs 5,000, is recoverable as a fine, and may be appealed to the High Court.
ingredients: Court of Session takes cognizance of offence under sub‑section (1) of section 222
Trial follows magistrate procedure for warrant‑cases not instituted on police report
Person alleged to have committed offence may be examined as prosecution witness unless directed otherwise
Trial may be held in camera on request of a party or court’s discretion
If court finds accusation unreasonable, it may direct the alleged offender (excluding certain high offices) to show cause for compensation
Compensation order limited to Rs 5,000 and treated as a fine
Compensation payer remains liable for other civil/criminal liabilities
Compensation payer may appeal to High Court; payment stays pending appeal outcome
exceptions: the person against whom the offence is alleged to have been committed shall, unless the Court of Session, for reasons to be recorded, otherwise directs, be examined as a witness for the prosecution
any amount paid to an accused person under this section shall be taken into account in awarding compensation to such person in any subsequent civil suit relating to the same matter
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 260, Page 9

description: When, in any warrant-case instituted on a police report, the accused appears or is brought before a Judicial Magistrate at the commencement of the trial, the Judicial Magistrate shall satisfy himself that he has complied with the provisions of section 230.
summary: At the start of a warrant‑case trial based on a police report, the Judicial Magistrate must verify compliance with section 230 before proceeding.
ingredients: Accused appears before Judicial Magistrate at trial commencement
Judicial Magistrate checks compliance with section 230
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 261, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) The accused may prefer an application for discharge within a period of sixty days from the date of framing of charges. ( 2 ) If, upon considering the police report and the documents sent with it under section 293 and making such examination, if any, of the accused as the Magistrate thinks necessary and after giving the prosecution and the accused an opportunity of being heard, the Magistrate considers the charge against the accused to be groundless, he shall discharge the accused, and record his reasons for so doing.
summary: The accused can apply for discharge within 60 days of charge framing. If the Magistrate, after reviewing the police report, documents, and hearing both sides, finds the charge groundless, he must discharge the accused and record reasons.
ingredients: Accused may file discharge application within 60 days of charge framing
Magistrate reviews police report, accompanying documents, and may examine the accused
Both prosecution and accused are given a chance to be heard
If charge is deemed groundless, Magistrate discharges accused and records reasons
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 262, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) If, upon such consideration, examination, if any, and hearing, the Magistrate is of opinion that there is ground for presuming that the accused has committed an offence triable under this Chapter, which such Magistrate is competent to try and which, in his opinion, could be adequately punished by him, he shall frame in writing a charge against the accused within a period of sixty days from the date of first hearing on charge. ( 2 ) The charge shall then be read and explained to the accused, and he shall be asked whether he pleads guilty of the offence charged or claims to be tried.
summary: If the Magistrate, after consideration, believes there is sufficient basis to presume the accused committed a triable offence, he must frame a written charge within 60 days of the first charge hearing, read it to the accused, and ask for a plea of guilty or trial.
ingredients: Magistrate considers evidence, examination, and hearing
Magistrate forms opinion that accused may have committed a triable offence
Magistrate frames written charge within 60 days of first charge hearing
Charge is read and explained to accused
Accused is asked to plead guilty or claim trial
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 263, Page 9

title: Fixing date when accused refuses to plead
description: Clause 264 This Clause provides that the accused refuses to plead, or does not plead, or claims to be tried or is not convicted under Clause 252, the Judge shall fix a date for the examination of witnesses, and may, on the application of the prosecution, issue any process for compelling the attendance of any witness or the production of any document or other thing.
summary: If the accused does not plead or refuses to plead, the judge must fix a date for witness examination and may issue processes to compel attendance of witnesses or production of documents upon prosecution's application.
ingredients: Accused refuses or fails to plead.
Judge fixes a date for examination of witnesses.
Judge may, on prosecution's application, issue process to compel witness attendance or document production.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 264, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) If the accused refuses to plead or does not plead, or claims to be tried or the Magistrate does not convict the accused under section 264, the Magistrate shall fix a date for the examination of witnesses: Provided that the Magistrate shall supply in advance to the accused, the statement of witnesses recorded during investigation by the police. ( 2 ) The Magistrate may, on the application of the prosecution, issue a summons to any of its witnesses directing him to attend or to produce any document or other thing. ( 3 ) On the date so fixed, the Magistrate shall proceed to take all such evidence as may be produced in support of the prosecution: Provided that the Magistrate may permit the cross-examination of any witness to be deferred until any other witness or witnesses have been examined or recall any witness for further cross-examination: Provided further that evidence of a witness under this sub-section may be recorded by audio-video electronic means.
summary: The Magistrate fixes a date for witness examination, supplies witness statements to the accused, may summon witnesses on the prosecution's request, and can record evidence by audio‑video means. Cross‑examination may be deferred or witnesses recalled as needed.
ingredients: Fix a date for examination of witnesses if accused refuses or does not plead
Supply to the accused, in advance, the statement of witnesses recorded during police investigation
Issue a summons to witnesses on prosecution's application
Take all prosecution evidence on the fixed date
Permit deferment of cross‑examination or recall of witnesses for further cross‑examination
Allow evidence of a witness to be recorded by audio‑video electronic means
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 265, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) The accused shall then be called upon to enter upon his defence and produce his evidence; and if the accused puts in any written statement, the Magistrate shall file it with the record. ( 2 ) If the accused, after he has entered upon his defence, applies to the Magistrate to issue any process for compelling the attendance of any witness for the purpose of examination or  cross-examination,  or  the  production  of  any  document  or  other  thing,  the  Magistrate shall  issue  such  process  unless  he  considers  that  such  application  should  be  refused  on the ground that it is made for the purpose of vexation or delay or for defeating the ends of justice and such ground shall be recorded by him in writing: Provided  that  when  the  accused  has  cross-examined  or  had  the  opportunity  of cross-examining any witness before entering on his defence, the attendance of such witness shall  not  be  compelled  under  this  section,  unless  the  Magistrate  is  satisfied  that  it  is necessary for the ends of justice. ( 3 )  The  Magistrate  may,  before  summoning  any  witness  on  an  application  under sub-section ( 2 ), require that the reasonable expenses incurred by the witness in attending for the purposes of the trial be deposited in Court.
summary: The accused must present his defence and any written statement. If he seeks a court process to compel a witness or document, the Magistrate must grant it unless it is for vexation, delay, or defeating justice, and may require the witness to deposit expenses.
ingredients: Call the accused to enter upon his defence and produce evidence
File any written statement of the accused with the record
Issue process to compel attendance of a witness or production of a document unless the application is for vexation, delay, or defeating the ends of justice
Record any refusal ground in writing
If the accused has already cross‑examined a witness before entering defence, attendance of that witness cannot be compelled unless necessary for the ends of justice
Require the witness to deposit reasonable expenses before being summoned
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 266, Page 9

description: B.— Cases instituted otherwise than on police report 267.  ( 1 ) When, in any warrant-case instituted otherwise than on a police report, the accused appears or is brought before a Magistrate, the Magistrate shall proceed to hear the prosecution and take all such evidence as may be produced in support of the prosecution. ( 2 ) The Magistrate may, on the application of the prosecution, issue a summons to any of its witnesses directing him to attend or to produce any document or other thing.
summary: In warrant‑cases not started by a police report, the Magistrate must hear the prosecution and take all prosecution evidence, and may summon witnesses on the prosecution's request.
ingredients: Proceed to hear the prosecution in warrant‑cases instituted without a police report
Take all prosecution evidence that may be produced
Issue summons to witnesses on the prosecution's application
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 267, Page 9

description: ( 1 )  If,  upon  taking  all  the  evidence  referred  to  in  section  267,  the  Magistrate considers, for reasons to be recorded, that no case against the accused has been made out which, if unrebutted, would warrant his conviction, the Magistrate shall discharge him. ( 2 ) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to prevent a Magistrate from discharging the  accused  at  any  previous  stage  of  the  case  if,  for  reasons  to  be  recorded by such Magistrate, he considers the charge to be groundless.
summary: If, after taking all evidence, the Magistrate finds no case that would justify conviction, he must discharge the accused, and may also discharge at earlier stages if the charge is groundless, recording reasons each time.
ingredients: Discharge the accused if, after taking all evidence under section 267, no case exists that would warrant conviction
Record reasons for discharge in writing
Allow discharge at any earlier stage if the charge is considered groundless, with reasons recorded
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 268, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) If, when such evidence has been taken, or at any previous stage of the case, the Magistrate is of opinion that there is ground for presuming that the accused has committed an offence triable under this Chapter, which such Magistrate is competent to try and which, in his opinion, could be adequately punished by him, he shall frame in writing a charge against the accused. ( 2 ) The charge shall then be read and explained to the accused, and he shall be asked whether he pleads guilty or has any defence to make. ( 3 ) If the accused pleads guilty, the Magistrate shall record the plea, and may, in his discretion, convict him thereon. ( 4 ) If the accused refuses to plead, or does not plead or claims to be tried or if the accused is not convicted under sub-section ( 3 ), he shall be required to state, at the commencement of the next hearing of the case, or, if the Magistrate for reasons to be recorded in writing so thinks fit, forthwith, whether he wishes to cross-examine any, and, if so, which, of the witnesses for the prosecution whose evidence has been taken. ( 5 ) If he says he does so wish, the witnesses named by him shall be recalled and after cross-examination and re-examination (if any), they shall be discharged. ( 6 ) The evidence of any remaining witnesses for the prosecution shall next be taken, and after cross-examination and re-examination (if any), they shall also be discharged. ( 7 ) Where, despite giving opportunity to the prosecution and after taking all reasonable measures under this Sanhita, if the attendance of the prosecution witnesses under sub-sections ( 5 ) and ( 6 ) cannot be secured for cross examination, it shall be deemed that such witness has not been examined for not being available, and the Magistrate may close the prosecution evidence for reasons to be recorded in writing and proceed with the case on the basis of the materials on record.
summary: The Magistrate frames a charge when evidence suggests the accused committed a triable offence, reads it to the accused, and determines plea. If the accused does not plead guilty, he may request cross‑examination of prosecution witnesses; the Magistrate can recall witnesses, and if witnesses are unavailable, may close prosecution evidence and proceed on the record.
ingredients: Frame a written charge when evidence supports presumption of commission of a triable offence
Read and explain the charge to the accused
Ask the accused whether he pleads guilty or has a defence
Record a guilty plea and optionally convict
If no plea or not convicted, require the accused to state at the next hearing whether he wishes to cross‑examine any prosecution witnesses
Recall and cross‑examine named witnesses, then discharge them after re‑examination
Take evidence of remaining prosecution witnesses and discharge after cross‑examination
If prosecution witnesses cannot be secured for cross‑examination despite reasonable measures, deem them not examined and close prosecution evidence, recording reasons
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 269, Page 9

title: Evidence for defence
description: Clause 270 of the Bill relates to Evidence for defence. This Clause provides that accused shall then be called upon to enter upon his defence and produce his evidence; and the provisions of section 266
summary: After the prosecution's case, the accused is called to present his defence and produce evidence, in accordance with the provisions of section 266.
ingredients: Accused is called to enter upon his defence.
Accused must produce his evidence.
The process follows the provisions of section 266.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 270, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) If, in any case under this Chapter in which a charge has been framed, the Magistrate finds the accused not guilty, he shall record an order of acquittal. ( 2 ) Where, in any case under this Chapter, the Magistrate finds the accused guilty, but does not proceed in accordance with the provisions of section 364 or section 401, he shall, after hearing the accused on the question of sentence, pass sentence upon him according to law. ( 3 ) Where, in any case under this Chapter, a previous conviction is charged under the provisions of sub-section ( 7 ) of section 234 and the accused does not admit that he has been previously convicted as alleged in the charge, the Magistrate may, after he has convicted the said accused, take evidence in respect of the alleged previous conviction, and shall record a finding thereon: Provided that no such charge shall be read out by the Magistrate nor shall the accused be asked to plead thereto nor shall the previous conviction be referred to by the prosecution or in any evidence adduced by it, unless and until the accused has been convicted under sub-section ( 2 ).
summary: If the Magistrate finds the accused not guilty, an acquittal order is recorded. If guilty but sentencing provisions of sections 364 or 401 are not followed, the Magistrate must hear the accused on sentencing and pass sentence per law. Evidence of a prior conviction may be taken only after conviction, with strict restrictions on how it is presented.
ingredients: Record an order of acquittal when the accused is found not guilty
If the accused is found guilty and sentencing provisions of sections 364 or 401 are not applied, hear the accused on sentencing and pass sentence according to law
When a previous conviction is alleged under section 234(7) and the accused does not admit it, take evidence of that conviction after the accused is convicted
Record a finding on the previous conviction
Do not read out the previous conviction charge, ask the accused to plead to it, or refer to it in prosecution evidence until after conviction under sub‑section (2)
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 271, Page 9

title: Absence of complainant
description: This Clause provides that the proceedings have been instituted upon complaint, and on any day fixed for the hearing of the case, the complainant is absent, and the offence may be lawfully compounded or is not a cognizable offence, the Magistrate may after giving thirty days' time to the complainant to be present, in his discretion, notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained, at any time before the charge has been framed, discharge the accused.
summary: If the complainant is absent on the hearing day and the offence is compoundable or non‑cognizable, the magistrate may, after granting a 30‑day period for the complainant to appear, discharge the accused before charge framing.
ingredients: Identify that proceedings are instituted upon complaint
Note the complainant's absence on the scheduled hearing day
Determine that the offence is lawfully compoundable or non‑cognizable
Give the complainant thirty days' time to appear
Discharge the accused at the Magistrate's discretion before charge framing
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 272, Page 9

description: ( 1 ) If, in any case instituted upon complaint or upon information given to a police officer or to a Magistrate, one or more persons is or are accused before a Magistrate of any offence triable by a Magistrate, and the Magistrate by whom the case is heard discharges or acquits all or any of the accused, and is of opinion that there was no reasonable ground for making the accusation against them or any of them, the Magistrate may, by his order of discharge or acquittal, if the person upon wh
summary: The provision (as presented) allows a Magistrate, after discharging or acquitting accused in a complaint‑initiated case, to order discharge/acquittal if he believes there was no reasonable ground for the accusation, subject to further conditions not shown in the excerpt.
ingredients: Applicable to cases instituted upon complaint or information to a police officer or Magistrate
If the Magistrate discharges or acquits accused and believes there was no reasonable ground for the accusation, he may order discharge or acquittal
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 273, Page 9

description:
274.  When  in  a  summons-case  the  accused  appears  or  is  brought  before  the Magistrate, the particulars of the offence of which he is accused shall be stated to him, and he shall be asked whether he pleads guilty or has any defence to make, but it shall not be necessary to frame a formal charge: Absence  of complainant. Compensation for  accusation without reasonable cause. Substance  of accusation  to be  stated.
Provided that if the Magistrate considers the accusation as groundless, he shall, after recording reasons in writing, release the accused and such release shall have the effect of discharge.
summary: When an accused appears in a summons case, the magistrate must inform him of the offence details and ask for a plea, without needing a formal charge. If the accusation is found groundless, the magistrate may release the accused after recording reasons.
ingredients: Magistrate must state the particulars of the offence to the accused.
Magistrate must ask the accused whether he pleads guilty or has any defence.
It is not necessary to frame a formal charge.
If the magistrate finds the accusation groundless, he may release the accused after recording reasons in writing.
exceptions: Provided that if the Magistrate considers the accusation as groundless, he shall, after recording reasons in writing, release the accused and such release shall have the effect of discharge.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 274, Page 10

title: Conviction on plea of guilty
description: This Clause provides that the accused pleads guilty, the Magistrate shall record the plea as nearly as possible in the words used by the accused and may, in his discretion, convict him thereon.
summary: When an accused pleads guilty, the magistrate records the plea verbatim and may, at his discretion, convict the accused on that basis.
ingredients: Magistrate must record the guilty plea as nearly as possible in the accused's own words.
Magistrate may, at his discretion, convict the accused based on the plea.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 275, Page 10

description: ( 1 )  Where  a  summons  has  been  issued  under  section  229  and  the  accused desires  to  plead  guilty  to  the  charge  without  appearing  before  the  Magistrate,  he  shall transmit to the Magistrate, by post or by messenger, a letter containing his plea and also the amount of fine specified in the summons. ( 2 ) The Magistrate may, in his discretion, convict the accused in his absence, on his plea of guilty and sentence him to pay the fine specified in the summons, and the amount transmitted by the accused shall be adjusted towards that fine, or where a pleader authorised by the accused in this behalf pleads guilty on behalf of the accused, the Magistrate shall record  the  plea  as  nearly  as possible  in  the  words used  by  the  pleader  and  may,  in his discretion, convict the accused on such plea and sentence him as aforesaid.
summary: An accused can plead guilty without appearing by sending a written plea and the fine amount. The magistrate may convict in the accused's absence, adjusting the transmitted amount towards the fine, or may accept a plea made by an authorized pleader.
ingredients: Accused may send a written plea and the fine amount by post or messenger.
Magistrate may convict the accused in his absence and adjust the transmitted amount towards the fine.
If an authorized pleader pleads on behalf of the accused, the magistrate records the plea in the pleader's words and may convict accordingly.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 276, Page 10

description: ( 1 )  Where  the  Magistrate  does  not  convict  the  accused  under  section  275  or section 276, the Magistrate shall proceed to hear the prosecution and take all such evidence as may be produced in support of the prosecution, and also to hear the accused and take all such evidence as he produces in his defence. ( 2 ) The Magistrate may, if he thinks fit, on the application of the prosecution or the accused, issue a summons to any witness directing him to attend or to produce any document or other thing. ( 3 ) The Magistrate may, before summoning any witness on such application, require that the reasonable expenses of the witness incurred in attending for the purposes of the trial be deposited in Court.
summary: If the magistrate does not convict under sections 275 or 276, he must hear both prosecution and defence evidence. He may issue summons to witnesses on application and can require witnesses to deposit reasonable expenses before attendance.
ingredients: Magistrate must hear prosecution evidence and defence evidence if no conviction under 275/276.
Magistrate may issue a summons to any witness on application of prosecution or accused.
Magistrate may require the witness to deposit reasonable expenses before being summoned.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 277, Page 10

description: ( 1 ) If the Magistrate, upon taking the evidence referred to in section 277 and such further evidence, if any, as he may, of his own motion, cause to be produced, finds the accused not guilty, he shall record an order of acquittal. ( 2 )  Where  the  Magistrate  does  not  proceed  in  accordance  with  the  provisions  of section 364 or section 401, he shall, if he finds the accused guilty, pass sentence upon him according to law. ( 3 ) A Magistrate may, under section 275 or section 278, convict the accused of any offence triable under this Chapter, which from the facts admitted or proved he appears to have committed, whatever may be the nature of the complaint or summons, if the Magistrate is satisfied that the accused would not be prejudiced thereby.
summary: After hearing evidence, the magistrate records an acquittal if the accused is found not guilty. If he proceeds without following sections 364 or 401 and finds the accused guilty, he must pass sentence as per law. He may also convict of any triable offence if satisfied no prejudice will result.
ingredients: If after taking evidence the magistrate finds the accused not guilty, he records an acquittal.
If the magistrate does not follow sections 364 or 401 and finds the accused guilty, he must pass sentence according to law.
Magistrate may convict of any offence triable under the chapter if facts admitted or proved show commission and no prejudice to the accused.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 278, Page 10

description: ( 1 ) If the summons has been issued on complaint, and on the day appointed for the appearance of the accused, or any day subsequent thereto to which the hearing may be adjourned, the complainant does not appear, the Magistrate shall, notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained, acquit the accused, unless for some reason he thinks it proper to adjourn the hearing of the case to some other day: Provided  that  where  the  complainant  is  represented  by  a  pleader  or  by  the  officer conducting  the  prosecution  or  where  the  Magistrate  is  of  opinion  that  the  personal attendance  of  the complainant  is  not  necessary,  the  Magistrate  may,  dispense  with  his attendance and proceed with the case. ( 2 )  The  provisions  of  sub-section  ( 1 )  shall,  so  far  as  may  be,  apply  also  to  cases where the  non-appearance of the complainant is due to his death.
summary: If a complainant fails to appear on the scheduled day in a summons issued on complaint, the magistrate must acquit the accused unless he adjourns the hearing. The magistrate may proceed without the complainant if represented or attendance is unnecessary. The same rule applies if the complainant's non‑appearance is due to death.
ingredients: If the complainant does not appear on the appointed day, the magistrate shall acquit the accused unless he adjourns the hearing.
Provided that if the complainant is represented by a pleader or the prosecuting officer, or if personal attendance is not necessary, the magistrate may proceed without him.
The same provisions apply when the complainant's non‑appearance is due to death.
exceptions: Provided that where the complainant is represented by a pleader or by the officer conducting the prosecution or where the Magistrate is of opinion that the personal attendance of the complainant is not necessary, the Magistrate may, dispense with his attendance and proceed with the case.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 279, Page 10

title: Withdrawal of complaint
description: This Clause provides that a complainant, at any time before a final order is passed in any case under this Chapter, satisfies the Magistrate that there are sufficient grounds for permitting him to withdraw his complaint against the accused, or if there be more than one accused, against all or any of them, the Magistrate may permit him to withdraw the same, and shall thereupon acquit the accused against whom the complaint is so withdrawn.
summary: A complainant may seek to withdraw the complaint before a final order; if the magistrate is satisfied, the complaint is withdrawn and the accused is acquitted.
ingredients: Complainant may seek to withdraw the complaint before a final order if sufficient grounds exist.
Magistrate may permit the withdrawal.
Upon withdrawal, the magistrate shall acquit the accused against whom the complaint is withdrawn.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 280, Page 10

title: Power to stop proceedings in certain cases
description: This Clause provides that any summons-case instituted otherwise than upon complaint, a Magistrate of the first class or, with the previous sanction of the Chief Judicial Magistrate, any other Judicial Magistrate, may, for reasons to be recorded by him, stop the proceedings at any stage without pronouncing any judgment and where such stoppage of proceedings is made after the evidence of the principal witnesses has been recorded, pronounce a judgment of acquittal, and in any other case, release the accused, and such release shall have the effect of discharge.
summary: A magistrate, with appropriate sanction, can halt a summons case at any stage without judgment; if halted after principal witness evidence, a judgment of acquittal may be pronounced, otherwise the accused is released and discharged.
ingredients: Magistrate of first class, or any magistrate with prior sanction of the Chief Judicial Magistrate, may stop proceedings at any stage.
Reasons for stoppage must be recorded.
If stoppage occurs after principal witness evidence is recorded, magistrate may pronounce acquittal.
In other cases, magistrate may release the accused, which has the effect of discharge.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 281, Page 10

title: Power of Court to convert summons-cases into warrant-cases
description: This Clause provides that the course of the trial of a summons-case relating to an offence punishable with imprisonment for a term exceeding six months, it appears to the Magistrate that in the interests of justice, the offence should be tried in accordance with the procedure for the trial of warrant-cases, such Magistrate may proceed to re-hear the case in the manner provided by this Sanhita for the trial of warrant-cases and may re-call any witness who may have been examined.
summary: If a summons case involves an offence with imprisonment over six months and justice requires, the magistrate may convert it to a warrant case, rehear it, and recall witnesses as per warrant‑case procedures.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 282, Page 10

description: ( 1 ) Notwithstanding anything contained in this Sanhita— ( a ) any Chief Judicial Magistrate; ( b ) Magistrate of the first class, shall try in a summary way all or any of the following offences:— ( i ) theft, under section 301, section 303 or section 304 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 where the value of the property stolen does not exceed twenty  thousand  rupees; ( ii )  receiving  or  retaining  stolen  property,  under  section  315  of  the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, where the value of the property does not exceed twenty  thousand  rupees; ( iii )  assisting  in  the  concealment  or  disposal  of  stolen  property  under section  315  of  the  Bharatiya  Nyaya  Sanhita,  2023,  where  the  value  of  such property  does  not  exceed  twenty  thousand  rupees; ( iv ) offences under section 330 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; ( v ) insult with intent to provoke a breach of the peace, under section 350, and criminal intimidation, under section 349 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; ( vi ) abetment of any of the foregoing offences; ( vii )  an  attempt  to  commit  any  of  the  foregoing  offences,  when  such attempt is an offence; ( viii ) any offence constituted by an act in respect of which a complaint may be made under section 20 of the Cattle-trespass Act, 1871. ( 2 ) The Magistrate may, after giving the accused a reasonable opportunity of being heard, for reasons to be recorded in writing, try in a summary way all or any of the offences not punishable with death or imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term exceeding three  years. ( 3 ) When, in the course of a summary trial it appears to the Magistrate that the nature of the case is such that it is undesirable to try it summarily, the Magistrate shall re-call any witnesses  who  may  have  been  examined  and  proceed  to  re-hear  the  case  in  the  manner provided by this Sanhita.
summary: Section 283 empowers Chief Judicial Magistrates and First Class Magistrates to conduct summary trials for specified offences, primarily low‑value theft‑related crimes and other minor offences. It also allows summary trial of any offence not punishable with death, life imprisonment or more than three years, provided the accused is heard. If during a summary trial the magistrate deems a full trial necessary, he must recall witnesses and proceed to a regular trial.
ingredients: Chief Judicial Magistrate or Magistrate of the first class may try summary trials of listed offences.
Listed offences include theft under sections 301, 303, 304 where value ≤ 20,000 rupees.
Receiving or retaining stolen property under section 315 where value ≤ 20,000 rupees.
Assisting concealment or disposal of stolen property under section 315 where value ≤ 20,000 rupees.
Offences under section 330 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.
Insult with intent to provoke breach of peace under section 350 and criminal intimidation under section 349.
Abetment of any of the foregoing offences.
Attempt to commit any of the foregoing offences, when such attempt is an offence.
Any offence where a complaint may be made under section 20 of the Cattle‑trespass Act, 1871.
Magistrate may, after hearing the accused, try summary any offence not punishable with death, life imprisonment or term > 3 years.
If during summary trial the case appears unsuitable for summary disposition, magistrate must recall witnesses and rehear the case as a regular trial.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 283, Page 10

title: Summary trial by Magistrate of the second class
description: This Clause provides that the High Court may confer on any Magistrate invested with the powers of a Magistrate of the second class power to try summarily any offence which is punishable only with fine or with imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months with or without fine, and any abetment of or attempt to commit any such offence.
summary: The High Court can empower a second‑class magistrate to conduct summary trials for offences punishable only by fine or imprisonment up to six months, including attempts or abetments.
ingredients: High Court may confer power on Second Class Magistrates.
Empowered magistrate can try summary any offence punishable only with fine or imprisonment ≤ 6 months, with or without fine.
Power also extends to abetment or attempt of such offences.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 284, Page 10

title: Procedure for summary trials
description: This Clause provides that trials under this Chapter XXIII, the procedure specified in this Sanhita for the trial of summons-case shall be followed except as hereinafter mentioned and No sentence of imprisonment for a term exceeding three months shall be passed in the case of any conviction under this Chapter.
summary: Summary trials follow the summons‑case procedure, except as specified, and no imprisonment exceeding three months may be imposed under this chapter.
ingredients: Trials under this chapter follow the summons‑case procedure, except as later modified.
Imprisonment sentences exceeding three months cannot be imposed for convictions under this chapter.
