sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 285, Page 10

description: In every case tried summarily, the Magistrate shall enter, in such form as the State Government may direct, the following particulars, namely:— ( a ) the serial number of the case; ( b ) the date of the commission of the offence; ( c ) the date of the report or complaint; ( d ) the name of the complainant (if any); ( e ) the name, parentage and residence of the accused; ( f )  the  offence  complained  of  and  the  offence  (if  any)  proved,  and  in  cases coming under clause ( ii ), clause ( iii ) or clause ( iv ) of sub-section ( 1 ) of section 283, the value of the property in respect of which the offence has been committed; ( g ) the plea of the accused and his examination (if any); ( h ) the finding; ( i ) the sentence or other final order; ( j ) the date on which proceedings terminated.
summary: Section 286 mandates that magistrates record specific details for every summary trial, including case identifiers, dates, parties, offence particulars, pleas, findings, and final orders, in a format prescribed by the State Government.
ingredients: Record serial number of the case.
Record date of commission of the offence.
Record date of report or complaint.
Record name of complainant, if any.
Record name, parentage and residence of the accused.
Record offence complained of and offence proved; for offences under sub‑section (1) of section 283, also record value of property.
Record plea of the accused and his examination, if any.
Record the finding.
Record the sentence or other final order.
Record the date on which proceedings terminated.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 286, Page 10

title: Judgment in cases tried summarily
description: This Clause provides that every case tried summarily in which the accused does not plead guilty, the Magistrate shall record the substance of the evidence and a judgment containing a brief statement of the reasons for the finding.
summary: For summary trials where the accused does not plead guilty, the magistrate must record evidence substance and deliver a concise judgment stating the reasons for the decision.
ingredients: If accused does not plead guilty, magistrate must record the substance of the evidence.
Magistrate must issue a judgment containing a brief statement of reasons for the finding.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 287, Page 10

title: Language of record and judgment
description: This Clause provides that Every such record and judgment shall be written in the language of the Court and The High Court may authorise any Magistrate empowered to try offences summarily to prepare the aforesaid record or judgment or both by means of an officer appointed in this behalf by the Chief Judicial Magistrate, and the record or judgment so prepared shall be signed by such Magistrate.
summary: Records and judgments in summary trials must be in the court's language; the High Court may authorize a magistrate to have an appointed officer prepare them, with the magistrate's signature required.
ingredients: Record and judgment must be written in the language of the Court.
High Court may authorize a magistrate to have an officer prepare the record or judgment.
The prepared record or judgment must be signed by the magistrate.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 288, Page 10

description: ( 1 ) This Chapter shall apply in respect of an accused against whom— ( a ) the report has been forwarded by the officer in charge of the police station under section 193 alleging therein that an offence appears to have been committed by him other than an offence for which the punishment of death or of imprisonment for life or of imprisonment for a term exceeding seven years has been provided under the law for the time being in force; or ( b ) a Magistrate has taken cognizance of an offence on complaint, other than an offence for which the punishment of death or of imprisonment for life or of imprisonment for a term exceeding seven years, has been provided under the law for the time being in force, and after examining complainant and witnesses under section 223, issued the process under section 227, Summary  trial by  Magistrate of  second class. Procedure  for summary trials. Record  in summary trials. Judgement  in cases  tried summarily. Language  of record  and judgment. Application  of Chapter. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40
summary: Section 289 defines the scope of the chapter, applying it to accused whose cases are referred under section 193 or taken cognizance of by a magistrate, provided the offence does not carry death, life imprisonment, or a term exceeding seven years. It excludes offences affecting the socio‑economic condition of the country or those against women or children under fourteen.
ingredients: Chapter applies when a report under section 193 is forwarded alleging an offence not punishable with death, life imprisonment, or term > 7 years.

Chapter applies when a magistrate takes cognizance of a complaint offence not punishable with death, life imprisonment, or term > 7 years, after examining complainant and witnesses under section 223 and issuing process under section 227.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 289, Page 10

description: ( 1 ) A person accused of an offence may file an application for plea bargaining within a period of thirty days from the date of framing of charge in the Court in which such offence is pending for trial. ( 2 ) The application under sub-section ( 1 ) shall contain a brief description of the case relating to which the application is filed including the offence to which the case relates and shall  be  accompanied  by  an  affidavit  sworn  by  the accused  stating  therein  that  he  has voluntarily  preferred,  after  understanding  the  nature  and  extent  of  punishment  provided under the law for the offence, the plea bargaining in his case and that he has not previously been convicted by a Court in a similar case. ( 3 ) After receiving the application under sub-section ( 1 ), the Court shall issue notice to the Public Prosecutor or the complainant of the case and to the accused to appear on the date fixed for the case. ( 4 )  When  the  Public  Prosecutor  or  the  complainant  of  the  case  and  the  accused appear  on  the date  fixed  under  sub-section  ( 3 ),  the  Court  shall  examine  the accused  in camera,  where  the  other  party  in  the  case  shall  not  be present,  to  satisfy  itself  that  the accused has filed the application voluntarily and where— ( a )  the  Court  is  satisfied  that  the  application  has  been  filed  by  the  accused voluntarily, it shall provide time, not exceeding sixty days, to the Public Prosecutor or the  complainant  of  the  case  and  the  accused  to work  out  a  mutually  satisfactory disposition of the case which may include giving to the victim by the accused the compensation  and  other  expenses  during  the  case  and  thereafter  fix  the date  for further hearing of the case; ( b )  the  Court  finds  that  the  application  has  been  filed  involuntarily  by  the  accused or he has previously been convicted by
summary: Section 290 outlines the procedure for filing a plea‑bargaining application: it must be filed within 30 days of charge framing, include a detailed description and a voluntary affidavit, and the court must notify the prosecutor and examine the accused in camera. If the application is voluntary, the court gives up to 60 days for parties to reach a settlement; if involuntary, different measures apply (text truncated).
ingredients: Application for plea bargaining must be filed within 30 days of framing of charge.
Application must contain brief case description and be accompanied by an affidavit stating voluntary preference and no prior similar conviction.
Court must issue notice to Public Prosecutor or complainant and to the accused to appear on a fixed date.
Court examines the accused in camera to ensure the application is voluntary.
If voluntary, court provides up to 60 days for parties to work out a mutually satisfactory disposition, possibly including compensation to the victim.
If the court finds the application involuntary or the accused has prior conviction, different procedure applies (text incomplete).
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 290, Page 10

description: 291. In working out a mutually satisfactory disposition under clause (a) of sub-section (4) of section 290, the Court shall follow the following procedure, namely:— (a) in a case instituted on a police report, the Court shall issue notice to the Public Prosecutor, the police officer who has investigated the case, the accused and the victim of the case to participate in the meeting to work out a satisfactory disposition of the case: Provided that throughout such process of working out a satisfactory disposition of the case, it shall be the duty of the Court to ensure that the entire process is completed voluntarily by the parties participating in the meeting: Provided further that the accused, if he so desires, participate in such meeting with his pleader, if any, engaged in the case; (b) in a case instituted otherwise than on police report, the Court shall issue notice to the accused and the victim of the case to participate in a meeting to work out a satisfactory disposition of the case: Provided that it shall be the duty of the Court to ensure, throughout such process of working out a satisfactory disposition of the case, that it is completed voluntarily by the parties participating in the meeting:
summary: The Court must follow a prescribed procedure to reach a mutually satisfactory disposition of a case, issuing notices to relevant parties and ensuring the process is voluntary. Different notice requirements apply depending on whether the case was instituted on a police report.
ingredients: Issue notice to Public Prosecutor, investigating police officer, accused and victim when case is instituted on a police report
Accused may be represented by a pleader if he desires
Issue notice to accused and victim when case is not instituted on a police report
Court must ensure the entire process is completed voluntarily by all parties
exceptions: Provided that throughout such process of working out a satisfactory disposition of the case, it shall be the duty of the Court to ensure that the entire process is completed voluntarily by the parties participating in the meeting
Provided further that the accused, if he so desires, participate in such meeting with his pleader, if any, engaged in the case
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 291, Page 10

description: 292. Where in a meeting under section 291, a satisfactory disposition of the case has been worked out, the Court shall prepare a report of such disposition which shall be signed by the presiding officer of the Court and all other persons who participated in the meeting and if no such disposition has been worked out, the Court shall record such observation and proceed further in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita from the stage the application under sub-section (1) of section 290 has been filed in such case.
summary: If a satisfactory disposition is reached, the Court must prepare and sign a report; if not, it records the observation and continues the proceedings from the appropriate stage.
ingredients: Prepare a report of the disposition signed by the presiding officer and all participants
If no disposition is reached, record the observation and continue the case from the stage of the application under sub-section (1) of section 290
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 292, Page 10

description: 293. Where a satisfactory disposition of the case has been worked out under section 292, the Court shall dispose of the case in the following manner, namely:— (a) the Court shall award the compensation to the victim in accordance with the disposition under section 292 and hear the parties on the quantum of the punishment, releasing of the accused on probation of good conduct or after admonition under section 401 or for dealing with the accused under the provisions of the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958 or any other law for the time being in force and follow the procedure specified in the succeeding clauses for imposing the punishment on the accused; (b) after hearing the parties under clause (a), if the Court is of the view that section 401 or the provisions of the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958 or any other law for the time being in force are attracted in the case of the accused, it may release the accused on probation or provide the benefit of any such law; (c) after hearing the parties under clause (b), if the Court finds that minimum punishment has been provided under the law for the offence committed by the accused, it may sentence the accused to half of such minimum punishment, and where the accused is a first-time offender and has not been convicted of any offence in the past, it may sentence the accused to one-fourth of such minimum punishment; (d) in case after hearing the parties under clause (b), the Court finds that the offence committed by the accused is not covered under clause (b) or clause (c), then, it may sentence the accused to one-fourth of the punishment provided or extendable, as the case may be, for such offence and where the accused is a first-time offender and has not been convicted of any offence in the past, it may sentence the accused to one-sixth of the punishment provided or extendable, as the case may be, for such offence.
summary: The Court disposes of a case with a satisfactory disposition by awarding compensation, considering probation, and sentencing based on minimum punishments, with reduced sentences for first‑time offenders.
ingredients: Award compensation to the victim as per the disposition
Hear parties on the quantum of punishment
Consider release on probation or admonition under section 401 or the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958
If minimum punishment exists, may sentence to half of it, or one‑fourth for first‑time offenders
If not covered by previous clauses, may sentence to one‑fourth of the punishment, or one‑sixth for first‑time offenders
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 293, Page 10

title: Judgment of the Court
description: This Clause provides that the Court shall deliver its judgment in terms of section 293 in the open Court and the same shall be signed by the presiding officer of the Court.
summary: The court must deliver its judgment openly as per Section 293 and the presiding officer must sign it.
ingredients: Deliver judgment in open Court
Signature of the presiding officer required
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 294, Page 10

title: Finality of the judgment
description: This Clause provides that the judgment delivered by the Court under this section shall be final and no appeal (except the special leave petition under article 136 and writ petition under articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution) shall lie in any Court against such judgment.
summary: Judgments under this section are final; appeals are barred except for special leave petitions under Article 136 and writ petitions under Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution.
ingredients: Judgment is final
No appeal allowed except special leave petition (Article 136) and writ petitions (Articles 226, 227)
exceptions: except the special leave petition under article 136 and writ petition under articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 295, Page 10

title: Power of the Court in plea bargaining
description: This Clause provides that Court shall have, for the purposes of discharging its functions under this Chapter, all the powers vested in respect of bail, trial of offences and other matters relating to the disposal of a case in such Court under this Sanhita.
summary: The court, for purposes of this chapter, possesses all powers related to bail, trial of offences, and other matters concerning case disposal.
ingredients: Court possesses all powers related to bail
Court possesses all powers related to trial of offences
Court possesses all powers for disposal of a case
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 296, Page 10

title: Period of detention undergone by the accused to be set off against the sentence of imprisonment
description: This Clause provides that the provisions of section 469 shall apply, for setting off the period of detention undergone by the accused against the sentence of imprisonment imposed under this Chapter, in the same manner as they apply in respect of the imprisonment under other provisions of this Sanhita.
summary: Section 469's provisions apply to set off time already spent in detention against any imprisonment sentence imposed under this chapter, just as they do for other provisions.
ingredients: Apply section 469 for setting off detention period against imprisonment sentence
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 297, Page 10

title: Savings
description: This Clause provides that the provisions of Chapter XXIV shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other provisions of this Sanhita and nothing in such other provisions shall be construed to constrain the meaning of any provision of this Chapter.
summary: Chapter XXIV's provisions remain effective even if inconsistent with other parts of the BNSS; other provisions cannot limit this chapter's meaning.
ingredients: Chapter provisions override inconsistent provisions in the Sanhita
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 298, Page 10

title: Statements of accused not to be used
description: This Clause provides that notwithstanding anything contained in any law for the time being in force, the statements or facts stated by an accused in an application for plea bargaining filed under section 290 shall not be used for any other purpose except for the purpose of this Chapter.
summary: Any statements made by an accused in a plea‑bargaining application under Section 290 cannot be used for any purpose other than those contemplated in this chapter, irrespective of other laws.
ingredients: Statements in plea‑bargaining application are limited to use within this Chapter only
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 299, Page 10

title: Non-application of the Chapter
description: It provides that Nothing in this Chapter shall apply to any juvenile or child as defined in section 2 of the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015.
summary: The provisions of this chapter do not apply to juveniles or children as defined under the Juvenile Justice Act, 2015.
ingredients: Excludes juveniles/children from application of this Chapter
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 300, Page 10

description: 301. In this Chapter,— (a) "detained" includes detained under any law providing for preventive detention; (b) "prison" includes,— (i) any place which has been declared by the State Government, by general or special order, to be a subsidiary jail; (ii) any reformatory, Borstal institution or institution of a like nature.
summary: Defines "detained" to include preventive detention and expands the meaning of "prison" to include subsidiary jails, reformatories, Borstal institutions, and similar establishments.
ingredients: Definition of "detained" includes preventive detention
Definition of "prison" includes subsidiary jails, reformatories, Borstal institutions, and similar institutions
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 301, Page 10

description: 302. (1) Whenever, in the course of an inquiry, trial or proceeding under this Sanhita, it appears to a Criminal Court,— (a) that a person confined or detained in a prison should be brought before the Court for answering to a charge of an offence, or for the purpose of any proceedings against him; or (b) that it is necessary for the ends of justice to examine such person as a witness, the Court may make an order requiring the officer in charge of the prison to produce such person before the Court answering to the charge or for the purpose of such proceeding or for giving evidence. (2) Where an order under sub-section (1) is made by a Magistrate of the second class, it shall not be forwarded to, or acted upon by, the officer in charge of the prison unless it is countersigned by the Chief Judicial Magistrate, to whom such Magistrate is subordinate. (3) Every order submitted for countersigning under sub-section (2) shall be accompanied by a statement of the facts which, in the opinion of the Magistrate, render the order
summary: The Court may order the production of a detained person for trial or as a witness. Orders by a second‑class magistrate require countersignature by the Chief Judicial Magistrate, and must be accompanied by a factual statement.
ingredients: Court may order production of a detained person for answering a charge or as a witness
If order is made by a second‑class magistrate, it must be countersigned by the Chief Judicial Magistrate
Countersigned orders must include a statement of facts justifying the order
exceptions: it shall not be forwarded to, or acted upon by, the officer in charge of the prison unless it is countersigned by the Chief Judicial Magistrate, to whom such Magistrate is subordinate
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 302, Page 10

description: (1) The State Government or the Central Government, as the case may be, may, at any time, having regard to the matters specified in sub-section (2), by general or special order, direct that any person or class of persons shall not be removed from the prison in which he or they may be confined or detained, and thereupon, so long as the order remains in force, no order made under section 302, whether before or after the order of the State Government, shall have effect in respect of such person or class of persons. (2) Before making an order under sub-section (1), the State Government or the Central Government in the cases instituted by its central agency, as the case may be, shall have regard to the following matters, namely:— (a) the nature of the offence for which, or the grounds on which, the person or class of persons has been ordered to be confined or detained in prison; (b) the likelihood of the disturbance of public order if the person or class of persons is allowed to be removed from the prison; (c) the public interest, generally.
summary: The State or Central Government may issue an order preventing the removal of a prisoner or class of prisoners, overriding any order under section 302 while the order remains in force. Before doing so, they must consider the nature of the offence, potential public disorder, and the general public interest.
ingredients: State or Central Government may direct that any person or class shall not be removed from prison.
The order remains effective as long as it is in force.
No order under section 302 shall have effect for those persons while the order stands.
Before issuing the order, the government must consider: (a) nature of the offence or grounds for confinement, (b) likelihood of public order disturbance, (c) general public interest.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 303, Page 10

description: Where the person in respect of whom an order is made under section 302— (a) is by reason of sickness or infirmity unfit to be removed from the prison; or (b) is under committal for trial or under remand pending trial or pending a preliminary investigation; or (c) is in custody for a period which would expire before the expiration of the time required for complying with the order and for taking him back to the prison in which he is confined or detained; or (d) is a person to whom an order made by the State Government under section 303 applies, the officer in charge of the prison shall abstain from carrying out the Court's order and shall send to the Court a statement of reasons for so abstaining: Provided that where the attendance of such person is required for giving evidence at a place not more than twenty-five kilometres distance from the prison, the officer in charge of the prison shall not so abstain for the reason mentioned in clause (b).
summary: The prison officer must refuse to execute a court order to produce a prisoner if the prisoner is sick, under trial/remand, would be released before the order can be complied with, or is protected by a section 303 order, and must explain the reasons. However, if the prisoner is needed to give evidence within 25 km of the prison, the officer cannot refuse on the basis of clause (b).
ingredients: If the prisoner is unfit due to sickness or infirmity, the officer must abstain.
If the prisoner is under committal for trial, remand pending trial, or pending preliminary investigation, the officer must abstain.
If the prisoner’s custody period would expire before the order can be complied with, the officer must abstain.
If a section 303 order applies to the prisoner, the officer must abstain.
The officer must send a statement of reasons to the Court for abstaining.
Exception: If the prisoner’s attendance is required for evidence within 25 km of the prison, the officer cannot abstain on the ground of clause (b).
exceptions: Provided that where the attendance of such person is required for giving evidence at a place not more than twenty-five kilometres distance from the prison, the officer in charge of the prison shall not so abstain for the reason mentioned in clause (b).
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 304, Page 10

description: Subject to the provisions of section 304, the officer in charge of the prison shall, upon delivery of an order made under sub-section (1) of section 302 and duly countersigned, where necessary, under sub-section (2) thereof, cause the person named in the order to be taken to the Court in which his attendance is required, so as to be present there at the time mentioned in the order, and shall cause him to be kept in custody in or near the Court until he has been examined or until the Court authorises him to be taken back to the prison in which he was confined or detained.
summary: Except where section 304 applies, the prison officer must transport the named prisoner to the designated court as ordered, keep him in custody near the court until examination or court authorization for return, and ensure the order is properly countersigned.
ingredients: Officer must act upon delivery of an order under section 302(1) that is duly countersigned as required.
Transport the named person to the Court where attendance is required.
Ensure the person is present at the specified time.
Keep the person in custody in or near the Court until examined or until the Court authorizes return to prison.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 305, Page 11

description: The provisions of this Chapter shall be without prejudice to the power of the Court to issue, under section 319, a commission for the examination, as a witness, of any person confined or detained in a prison; and the provisions of Part B of Chapter XXVI shall apply in relation to the examination on commission of any such person in the prison as they apply in relation to the examination on commission of any other person.
summary: The Court retains the authority, under section 319, to issue a commission to examine any prisoner as a witness, and the procedural rules of Part B of Chapter XXVI apply equally to such examinations.
ingredients: Court may issue a commission under section 319 to examine any confined person as a witness.
Part B of Chapter XXVI governs the examination on commission of prisoners in the same way as for any other person.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 306, Page 11

description: The State Government may determine what shall be, for purposes of this Sanhita, the language of each Court within the State other than the High Court.
summary: The State Government is empowered to decide the official language to be used by each Court in the State, except for the High Court.
ingredients: State Government may designate the language for each Court in the State, excluding the High Court.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 307, Page 11

description: Except as otherwise expressly provided, all evidence taken in the course of the trial or other proceeding shall be taken in the presence of the accused, or, when his personal attendance is dispensed with, in the presence of his pleader: Provided that where the evidence of a woman below the age of eighteen years who is alleged to have been subjected to rape or any other sexual offence, is to be recorded, the court may take appropriate measures to ensure that such woman is not confronted by the accused while at the same time ensuring the right of cross-examination of the accused. Explanation.—In this section, "accused" includes a person in relation to whom any proceeding under Chapter IX has been commenced under this Sanhita.
summary: Evidence must generally be recorded in the presence of the accused, or if the accused is absent, in the presence of his pleader. For a female victim under 18 in sexual offence cases, the court may protect her from confrontation while preserving the accused’s right to cross‑examine. The term "accused" also covers persons against whom any Chapter IX proceeding has begun.
ingredients: Evidence must be taken in the presence of the accused.
If the accused’s personal attendance is dispensed with, evidence must be taken in the presence of his pleader.
For a woman under 18 alleged to be a victim of rape or other sexual offence, the court may ensure she is not confronted by the accused while preserving cross‑examination rights.
explanation: In this section, "accused" includes a person in relation to whom any proceeding under Chapter IX has been commenced under this Sanhita.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 308, Page 11

description: (1) In all summons-cases tried before a Magistrate, in all inquiries under sections 165 to 168 (both inclusive), and in all proceedings under section 493 otherwise than in the course of a trial, the Magistrate shall, as the examination of each witness proceeds, make a memorandum of the substance of the evidence in the language of the Court: Provided that if the Magistrate is unable to make such memorandum himself, he shall, after recording the reason of his inability, cause such memorandum to be made in writing or from his dictation in open Court. (2) Such memorandum shall be signed by the Magistrate and shall form part of the record.
summary: During summons cases, specified inquiries, and certain non‑trial proceedings, the Magistrate must prepare a memorandum of the evidence in the Court’s language. If he cannot do it personally, he must record the reason and have it prepared in writing or by dictation, and the memorandum must be signed and become part of the official record.
ingredients: Magistrate must make a memorandum of the substance of evidence in the language of the Court as each witness is examined.
Applies to summons‑cases, inquiries under sections 165‑168, and proceedings under section 493 not in trial.
If the Magistrate cannot make the memorandum himself, he must record the reason and cause it to be made in writing or by dictation in open Court.
The memorandum must be signed by the Magistrate and form part of the record.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 309, Page 11

description: (1) In all warrant-cases tried before a Magistrate, the evidence of each witness shall, as his examination proceeds, be taken down in writing either by the Magistrate himself or by his dictation in open Court or, where he is unable to do so owing to a physical or other incapacity, under his direction and superintendence, by an officer of the Court appointed by him in this behalf: Provided that evidence of a witness under this sub-section may also be recorded by audio-video electronic means in the presence of the advocate of the person accused of the offence. (2) Where the Magistrate causes the evidence to be taken down, he shall record a certificate that the evidence could not be taken down by himself for the reasons referred to in sub-section (1). (3) Such evidence shall ordinarily be taken down in the form of a narrative; but the Magistrate may, in his discretion take down, or cause to be taken down, any part of such evidence in the form of question and answer. (4) The evidence so taken down shall be signed by the Magistrate and shall form part of the record.
summary: In warrant cases before a Magistrate, witness evidence must be recorded in writing by the Magistrate, by dictation, or by an appointed officer if the Magistrate is incapacitated; audio‑video recording is permitted in the presence of the accused’s advocate. The Magistrate must certify any incapacity, may record evidence as narrative or Q&A, and the record must be signed and become part of the official file.
ingredients: Evidence must be taken down in writing by the Magistrate, by dictation, or by an appointed officer if the Magistrate is physically or otherwise incapacitated.
Audio‑video electronic recording is allowed if the accused’s advocate is present.
If the Magistrate cannot take down the evidence himself, he must record a certificate stating the reasons.
Evidence is ordinarily recorded as a narrative, but may be recorded in question‑and‑answer form at the Magistrate’s discretion.
The written evidence must be signed by the Magistrate and form part of the record.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 310, Page 11

description: (1) In all trials before a Court of Session, the evidence of each witness shall, as his examination proceeds, be taken down in writing either by the presiding Judge himself or by his dictation in open Court, or under his direction and superintendence, by an officer of the Court appointed by him in this behalf. (2) Such evidence shall ordinarily be taken down in the form of narrative, but the presiding Judge may, in his discretion, take down, or cause to be taken down, any part of such evidence in the form of question and answer. (3) The evidence so taken down shall be signed by the presiding Judge and shall form part of the record.
summary: During trials before a Court of Session, the presiding Judge must ensure witness evidence is recorded in writing, either personally, by dictation, or by an appointed officer; the evidence is normally narrative but may be recorded as Q&A at the Judge’s discretion, and must be signed to become part of the official record.
ingredients: Evidence must be taken down in writing by the presiding Judge, by dictation, or by an appointed officer.
Evidence is ordinarily recorded as a narrative, but may be recorded in question‑and‑answer form at the Judge’s discretion.
The written evidence must be signed by the presiding Judge and form part of the record.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 311, Page 11

description: In every case where evidence is taken down under sections 310 or 311,— (a) if the witness gives evidence in the language of the Court, it shall be taken down in that language; (b) if he gives evidence in any other language, it may, if practicable, be taken down in that language, and if it is not practicable to do so, a true translation of the evidence in the language of the Court shall be prepared as the examination of the witness proceeds, signed by the Magistrate or presiding Judge, and shall form part of the record; (c) where under clause (b) evidence is taken down in a language other than the language of the Court, a true translation thereof in the language of th
summary: When evidence is recorded under sections 310 or 311, it must be taken down in the Court’s language if the witness speaks that language; otherwise, it may be recorded in the witness’s language if feasible, or a true translation into the Court’s language must be prepared and signed, becoming part of the record.
ingredients: If the witness gives evidence in the Court’s language, the evidence is taken down in that language.
If the witness gives evidence in another language, the evidence may be taken down in that language if practicable.
If it is not practicable to take down the evidence in the witness’s language, a true translation into the Court’s language must be prepared during the examination.
The translation must be signed by the Magistrate or presiding Judge and become part of the record.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 312, Page 11

title: Record in warrant-cases. Record in trial before Court of Session. Language of record of evidence. Procedure in regard to such evidence when completed.
description: (1) As the evidence of each witness taken under section 310 or section 311 is completed, it shall be read over to him in the presence of the accused, if in attendance, or of his pleader, if he appears by pleader, and shall, if necessary, be corrected. (2) If the witness denies the correctness of any part of the evidence when the same is read over to him, the Magistrate or presiding Judge may, instead of correcting the evidence, make a memorandum thereon of the objection made to it by the witness and shall add such remarks as he thinks necessary. (3) If the record of the evidence is in a language different from that in which it has been given and the witness does not understand that language, the record shall be interpreted to him in the language in which it was given, or in a language which he understands.
summary: Section 313 mandates that after a witness's evidence is recorded, it must be read back to the accused or his pleader, allowing corrections. If the witness objects to any part, the magistrate can note the objection. When the record is in a language the witness does not understand, it must be interpreted for them.
ingredients: Read over the recorded evidence to the accused (if present) or to his pleader.
If the witness objects to any part of the evidence, the magistrate may note a memorandum of the objection and add remarks.
If the record is in a language the witness does not understand, it must be interpreted to the witness in a language he understands.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 313, Page 11

title: Interpretation of evidence to accused or his pleader.
description: (1) Whenever any evidence is given in a language not understood by the accused, and he is present in Court in person, it shall be interpreted to him in open Court in a language understood by him. (2) If he appears by pleader and the evidence is given in a language other than the language of the Court, and not understood by the pleader, it shall be interpreted to such pleader in that language. (3) When documents are put for the purpose of formal proof, it shall be in the discretion of the Court to interpret as much thereof as appears necessary.
summary: Section 314 requires that evidence given in a language the accused does not understand be interpreted for him in open court. The same applies to a pleader who does not understand the language. Courts may also interpret documents as needed for formal proof.
ingredients: Interpret evidence to the accused in a language he understands when he is present in person.
Interpret evidence to the pleader in the language of the evidence if the pleader does not understand it.
Court may interpret documents as necessary for formal proof.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 314, Page 11

title: Remarks respecting demeanour of witness.
description: When a presiding Judge or Magistrate has recorded the evidence of a witness, he shall also record such remarks (if any) as he thinks material respecting the demeanour of such witness whilst under examination.
summary: Section 315 allows the presiding judge or magistrate to note any observations about a witness's demeanour that are considered material during the recording of evidence.
ingredients: Judge or magistrate may record remarks on the witness's demeanour if deemed material.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 315, Page 11

title: Record of examination of accused.
description: (1) Whenever the accused is examined by any Magistrate, or by a Court of Session, the whole of such examination, including every question put to him and every answer given by him, shall be recorded in full by the presiding Judge or Magistrate himself or where he is unable to do so owing to a physical or other incapacity, under his direction and superintendence by an officer of the Court appointed by him in this behalf. (2) The record shall, if practicable, be in the language in which the accused is examined or, if that is not practicable, in the language of the Court. (3) The record shall be shown or read to the accused, or, if he does not understand the language in which it is written, shall be interpreted to him in a language which he understands, and he shall be at liberty to explain or add to his answers. (4) It shall thereafter be signed by the accused and by the Magistrate or presiding Judge, who shall certify under his own hand that the examination was taken in his presence and hearing and that the record contains a full and true account of the statement made by the accused: Provided that where the accused is in custody and is examined through electronic communication, his signature shall be taken within seventy-two hours of such examination. (5) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to apply to the examination of an accused person in the course of a summary trial.
summary: Section 316 requires a full, verbatim record of an accused's examination by a magistrate or session court, to be made in the language of examination if possible. The record must be shown to the accused, interpreted if needed, and signed by both parties. An exception applies to summary trials.
ingredients: Full recording of all questions and answers during the accused's examination.
Record in the language of examination if practicable; otherwise, in the language of the Court.
Show or read the record to the accused; interpret if the accused does not understand the language.
Accused and magistrate/judge must sign the record, certifying its accuracy.
If examined electronically while in custody, the accused's signature must be taken within 72 hours.
Section does not apply to examinations in summary trials.
exceptions: Nothing in this section shall be deemed to apply to the examination of an accused person in the course of a summary trial.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 316, Page 11

title: Interpreter to be bound to interpret truthfully.
description: When the services of an interpreter are required by any Criminal Court for the interpretation of any evidence or statement, he shall be bound to state the true interpretation of such evidence or statement.
summary: Section 317 obliges any interpreter employed by a criminal court to provide a true and accurate interpretation of evidence or statements.
ingredients: Interpreter must give a true interpretation of evidence or statements.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 317, Page 11

title: High Court rules on evidence taking.
description: Every High Court may, by general rule, prescribe the manner in which the evidence of witnesses and the examination of the accused shall be taken down in cases coming before it, and such evidence and examination shall be taken down in accordance with such rule.
summary: Section 318 empowers each High Court to issue general rules governing how witness evidence and accused examinations are recorded in cases before it.
ingredients: High Court may prescribe rules for recording witness evidence and accused examinations.
All evidence and examinations must follow the prescribed rules.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 318, Page 11

title: Commission for examination of witness.
description: (1) Whenever, in the course of any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, it appears to a Court or Magistrate that the examination of a witness is necessary for the ends of justice, and that the attendance of such witness cannot be procured without an amount of delay, expense or inconvenience which, under the circumstances of the case, would be unreasonable, the Court or Magistrate may dispense with such attendance and may issue a commission for the examination of the witness in accordance with the provisions of this Chapter: Provided that where the examination of the President or the Vice-President of India or the Governor of a State or the Administrator of a Union territory as a witness is necessary for the ends of Justice, a commission shall be issued for the examination of such a witness. (2) The Court may, when issuing a commission for the examination of a witness for the prosecution, direct that such amount as the Court considers reasonable to meet the expenses of the accused, including the pleader's fees, be paid by the prosecution.
summary: Section 319 allows a court or magistrate to issue a commission to examine a witness when personal attendance is impracticable, with a special provision for high constitutional offices. It also permits the court to order the prosecution to cover reasonable expenses of the accused when the commission is for the prosecution's witness.
ingredients: Court/magistrate may issue a commission to examine a witness if attendance is unreasonable.
Commission must follow provisions of the Chapter.
If the witness is the President, Vice-President, Governor, or Administrator, a commission is mandatory.
Court may order prosecution to pay reasonable expenses of the accused, including pleader's fees, when the commission is for a prosecution witness.
exceptions: Provided that where the examination of the President or the Vice-President of India or the Governor of a State or the Administrator of a Union territory as a witness is necessary for the ends of Justice, a commission shall be issued for the examination of such a witness.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 319, Page 11

title: Direction of commission to appropriate authority.
description: (1) If the witness is within the territories to which this Sanhita extends, the commission shall be directed to the Chief Judicial Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction the witness is to be found. (2) If the witness is in India, but in a State or an area to which this Sanhita does not extend, the commission shall be directed to such Court or officer as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf. (3) If the witness is in a country or place outside India and arrangements have been made by the Central Government with the Government of such country or place for taking the evidence of witnesses in relation to criminal matters, the commission shall be issued in such form, directed to such Court or officer, and sent to such authority for transmission as the Central Government may, by notification, prescribed in this behalf.
summary: Section 320 specifies to which authority a commission for witness examination must be directed, depending on whether the witness is within the jurisdiction of the Sanhita, in a non‑jurisdictional Indian state, or abroad.
ingredients: If witness is within the Sanhita's territorial extent, direct commission to the Chief Judicial Magistrate of the local jurisdiction.
If witness is in India but outside the Sanhita's extent, direct commission to the Court or officer specified by Central Government notification.
If witness is abroad, issue commission in the form prescribed by Central Government and send to the designated authority for transmission.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 320, Page 11

title: Summoning and examination of witness under commission.
description: Upon receipt of the commission, the Chief Judicial Magistrate or Judicial Magistrate as he may appoint in this behalf, shall summon the witness before him or proceed to the place where the witness is, and shall take down his evidence in the same manner, and may for this purpose exercise the same powers, as in trials or warrant-cases under this Sanhita.
summary: Section 321 empowers the magistrate receiving a commission to summon the witness, travel to the witness's location if necessary, and record the witness's evidence using the same powers as in regular trials.
ingredients: Magistrate summons the witness or proceeds to the witness's location.
Magistrate records the witness's evidence in the same manner as in trials or warrant‑cases.
Magistrate may exercise all powers applicable in trials or warrant‑cases for this purpose.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 321, Page 11

title: Interrogatories and examination of witness under commission.
description: (1) The parties to any proceeding under this Sanhita in which a commission is issued may respectively forward any interrogatories in writing which the Court or Magistrate directing the commission may think relevant to the issue, and it shall be lawful for the Magistrate, Court or officer to whom the commission, is directed, or to whom the duty of executing it is delegated, to examine the witness upon such interrogatories. (2) Any such party may appear before such magistrate, Court or Officer by pleader, or if not in custody, in person, and may examine, cross-examine and re-examine the said witness.
summary: Section 322 allows parties to submit written interrogatories for a commissioned witness, and authorizes the magistrate or officer executing the commission to examine the witness based on those interrogatories. Parties may appear and conduct examination, cross‑examination, and re‑examination.
ingredients: Parties may forward written interrogatories to the magistrate or court directing the commission.
Magistrate, court, or officer executing the commission may examine the witness based on the interrogatories.
Parties may appear before the magistrate, court, or officer by pleader or in person (if not in custody) to examine, cross‑examine, and re‑examine the witness.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 322, Page 11

title: Return of commission and deposition.
description: (1) After any commission issued under section 310 has been duly executed, it shall be returned, together with the deposition of the witness examined thereunder, to the Court or Magistrate issuing the commission; and the commission, the return thereto and the deposition shall be open at all reasonable times to inspection of the parties, and may, subject to all just exceptions, be read in evidence in the case by either party, and shall form part of the record. (2) Any deposition so taken, if it satisfies the conditions specified by s
summary: Section 323 requires that once a commission is executed, it and the witness's deposition be returned to the issuing authority, made available for inspection, and may be read as evidence subject to justified exceptions.
ingredients: Return the executed commission and witness deposition to the issuing court or magistrate.
Make the commission, return, and deposition open for inspection by parties at reasonable times.
Allow the deposition to be read in evidence by either party, subject to justified exceptions.
Deposition must satisfy conditions specified (text truncated).
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 323, Page 11

description: In every case in which a commission is issued under section 319, the inquiry, trial or other proceeding may be adjourned for a specified time reasonably sufficient for the execution and return of the commission.
summary: If a commission is issued under section 319, the court may adjourn the proceeding for a reasonable period to allow the commission to be executed and returned.
ingredients: Adjournment of inquiry, trial or other proceeding
Specified time reasonably sufficient for execution and return of the commission
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 324, Page 11

description: The person to whom the loss or damage is caused.
summary: Identifies the victim in mischief by injury to works of irrigation.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 324(a)

description:
( 1 ) The provisions of section 321 and so much of section 322 and section 323 as relate to the execution of a commission and its return shall apply in respect of commissions issued by any of the Courts, Judges or Magistrates hereinafter mentioned as they apply to commissions issued under section 319. Commission to whom to be issued. Execution of commissions. Parties may examine witnesses. Return of commission. Adjournment of proceeding. Execution of foreign commissions. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45

98 ( 2 ) The Courts, Judges and Magistrates referred to in sub-section ( 1 ) are— ( a ) any such Court, Judge or Magistrate exercising jurisdiction within an area in India to which this Sanhita does not extend, as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf; ( b ) any Court, Judge or Magistrate exercising jurisdiction in any such country or place outside India, as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf, and having authority, under the law in force in that country or place, to issue commissions for the examination of witnesses in relation to criminal matters.
summary: Section 321, and the relevant parts of sections 322 and 323, apply to commissions issued by any court, judge or magistrate as they do to commissions under section 319. It also defines which courts, judges and magistrates can issue such commissions, both within India and abroad, as notified by the Central Government.
ingredients: Application of provisions of section 321, and relevant parts of sections 322 and 323, to commissions issued by any court, judge or magistrate
Identification of courts, judges and magistrates within India where the Sanhita does not extend
Identification of courts, judges and magistrates outside India with authority under local law to issue commissions
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 325, Page 11

description: ( 1 ) The deposition of civil surgeon or other medical witness, taken and attested by a Magistrate in the presence of the accused, or taken on commission under this Chapter, may be given in evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, although the deponent is not called as a witness. ( 2 ) The Court may, if it thinks fit, and shall, on the application of the prosecution or the accused, summon and examine any such deponent as to the subject-matter of his deposition.
summary: Medical witness depositions taken by a magistrate, even if the deponent is not present as a witness, can be admitted as evidence. The court may also summon and examine the deponent on application by either party.
ingredients: Deposition taken and attested by a Magistrate in presence of the accused
Deposition may be admitted as evidence without calling the deponent as a witness
Court may summon and examine the deponent on application of prosecution or accused
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 326, Page 11

description: ( 1 ) Any document purporting to be a report of identification under the hand of an Executive Magistrate in respect of a person or property may be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, although such Magistrate is not called as a witness: Provided that where such report contains a statement of any suspect or witness to which the provisions of section 19, section 26, section 27, section 158 or section 160 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, apply, such statement shall not be used under this sub-section except in accordance with the provisions of those sections. ( 2 ) The Court may, if it thinks fit, and shall, on the application of the prosecution or of the accused, summon and examine such Magistrate as to the subject-matter of the said report.
summary: Identification reports prepared by an Executive Magistrate can be admitted as evidence without the magistrate being called as a witness, except for statements covered by specific evidentiary provisions. The court may summon the magistrate to explain the report.
ingredients: Document is a report of identification by an Executive Magistrate
Report may be used as evidence without calling the magistrate as a witness
Exception: statements of suspect or witness covered by sections 19, 26, 27, 158 or 160 of the Evidence Act must follow those provisions
Court may summon and examine the magistrate on application of either party
exceptions: Provided that where such report contains a statement of any suspect or witness to which the provisions of section 19, section 26, section 27, section 158 or section 160 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, apply, such statement shall not be used under this sub-section except in accordance with the provisions of those sections.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 327, Page 11

description:
( 1 ) Any document purporting to be a report under the hand of any such officer of any Mint or of any Note Printing Press or of any Security Printing Press (including the officer of the Controller of Stamps and Stationery) or of any Forensic Department or Division of Forensic Science Laboratory or any Government Examiner of Questioned Documents or any State Examiner of Questioned Documents as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf, upon any matter or thing duly submitted to him for examination and report in the course of any proceeding under this Sanhita, may be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, although such officer is not called as a witness. ( 2 ) The Court may, if it thinks fit, summon and examine any such officer as to the subject-matter of his report: Provided that no such officer shall be summoned to produce any records on which the report is based. ( 3 ) Without prejudice to the provisions of sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, no such officer shall, except with the permission of the General Manager or any officer in charge of any Mint or of any Note Printing Press or of any Security Printing Press or of any Forensic Department or any officer in charge of the Forensic Science Laboratory or of the Government Examiner of Questioned Documents Organisation or of the State Examiner of Questioned Documents Organisation, as the case may be, be permitted— ( a ) to give any evidence derived from any unpublished official records on which the report is based; or Deposition of medical witness. Identification report of Magistrate. Evidence of officers of the Mint. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45

99 ( b ) to disclose the nature or particulars of any test applied by him in the course of the examination of the matter or thing.
summary: Reports prepared by officers of mints, printing presses, forensic labs, or similar authorities can be admitted as evidence without the officer being called as a witness. The court may summon the officer to explain the report but cannot compel production of underlying records. Permission is required for the officer to disclose unpublished records or details of tests.
ingredients: Document is a report by an officer of a Mint, Note Printing Press, Security Printing Press, Forensic Department, etc.
Report may be used as evidence without calling the officer as a witness
Court may summon and examine the officer on the subject-matter of the report
Officer shall not be summoned to produce records on which the report is based
Permission required from appropriate senior officer to give evidence from unpublished records
Permission required to disclose nature or particulars of any test applied
exceptions: Provided that no such officer shall be summoned to produce any records on which the report is based.

Without prejudice to the provisions of sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, no such officer shall, except with the permission of the General Manager or any officer in charge of any Mint or of any Note Printing Press or of any Security Printing Press or of any Forensic Department or any officer in charge of the Forensic Science Laboratory or of the Government Examiner of Questioned Documents Organisation or of the State Examiner of Questioned Documents Organisation, as the case may be, be permitted— ( a ) to give any evidence derived from any unpublished official records on which the report is based; or
99 ( b ) to disclose the nature or particulars of any test applied by him in the course of the examination of the matter or thing.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 328, Page 11

description: ( 1 ) Any document purporting to be a report under the hand of a Government scientific expert to whom this section applies, upon any matter or thing duly submitted to him for examination or analysis and report in the course of any proceeding under this Sanhita, may be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita. ( 2 ) The Court may, if it thinks fit, summon and examine any such expert as to the subject-matter of his report. ( 3 ) Where any such expert is summoned by a Court, and he is unable to attend personally, he may, unless the Court has expressly directed him to appear personally, depute any responsible officer working with him to attend the Court, if such officer is conversant with the facts of the case and can satisfactorily depose in Court on his behalf. ( 4 ) This section applies to the following Government scientific experts, namely:— ( a ) any Chemical Examiner or Assistant Chemical Examiner to Government; ( b ) the Chief Controller of Explosives; ( c ) the Director of the Finger Print Bureau; ( d ) the Director, Haffkeine Institute, Bombay; ( e ) the Director, Deputy Director or Assistant Director of a Central Forensic Science Laboratory or a State Forensic Science Laboratory; ( f ) the Serologist to the Government; ( g ) any other scientific expert specified or certified, by notification, by the State Government or the Central Government for this purpose.
summary: Reports prepared by designated government scientific experts are admissible as evidence. The court may summon the expert, and if the expert cannot attend, a qualified officer may appear on his behalf. The section lists the categories of experts covered.
ingredients: Report by a Government scientific expert covered by this section
Report may be used as evidence without calling the expert as a witness
Court may summon and examine the expert on the subject-matter of the report
If expert cannot attend, a responsible officer may be deputed to depose
List of categories of experts to which the section applies
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 329, Page 11

description: ( 1 ) Where any document is filed before any Court by the prosecution or the accused, the particulars of every such document shall be included in a list and the prosecution or the accused or the advocate for the prosecution or the accused, if any, shall be called upon to admit or deny the genuineness of each such document soon after supply of such documents and in no case later than thirty days after such supply: Provided that the Court may, in its discretion, relax the time limit with reasons to be recorded in writing: Provided further that no expert shall be called to appear before the Court unless the report of such expert is disputed by any of the parties to the trial. ( 2 ) The list of documents shall be in such form as the State Government may, be rules, provide. ( 3 ) Where the genuineness of any document is not disputed, such document may be read in evidence in inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita without proof of the signature of the person by whom it purports to be signed: Provided that the Court may, in its discretion, require such signature to by proved.
summary: All documents filed by either party must be listed, and each party must admit or deny its genuineness within 30 days, unless the court extends the period. Uncontested documents can be read as evidence without signature proof, though the court may require it. Experts are only summoned if their reports are disputed.
ingredients: Preparation of a list of all documents filed by prosecution or accused
Parties must admit or deny genuineness of each document within 30 days
Court may extend the time limit at its discretion with recorded reasons
No expert summoned unless their report is disputed
Form of the list to be prescribed by State Government rules
Uncontested documents may be read as evidence without signature proof
Court may require signature proof at its discretion
exceptions: Provided that the Court may, in its discretion, relax the time limit with reasons to be recorded in writing:
Provided further that no expert shall be called to appear before the Court unless the report of such expert is disputed by any of the parties to the trial.
Provided that the Court may, in its discretion, require such signature to by proved.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 330, Page 11

description: The person in possession of the house trespassed upon.
summary: Identifies the possessor of a house that has been trespassed.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 330(c)

description: When any application is made to any Court in the course of any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, and allegations are made therein respecting any public servant, the applicant may give evidence of the facts alleged in the application by affidavit, and the Court may, if it thinks fit, order that evidence relating to such facts be so given.
summary: If an application during a proceeding contains allegations against a public servant, the applicant can support those allegations by affidavit, and the court may order such affidavit evidence to be admitted.
ingredients: Application made during inquiry, trial or other proceeding
Allegations in the application relate to a public servant
Applicant may provide evidence of those facts by affidavit
Court may order the affidavit evidence to be admitted
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 331, Page 11

description: ( 1 ) The evidence of any person whose evidence is of a formal character may be given by affidavit and may, subject to all just exceptions, be read in evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita. ( 2 ) The C
summary: Formal evidence may be presented by affidavit and read in evidence, subject to any justified exceptions.
ingredients: Evidence of a person of formal character
Evidence may be given by affidavit
Affidavit may be read in evidence subject to just exceptions
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 332, Page 11

title: Affidavits to be used before any Court
description: 333.  ( 1 ) Affidavits to be used before any Court under this Sanhita may be sworn or affirmed before— ( a ) any Judge or Judicial or Executive Magistrate; or ( b ) any Commissioner of Oaths appointed by a High Court or Court of Session; or ( c ) any notary appointed under the Notaries Act, 1952. ( 2 ) Affidavits shall be confined to, and shall state separately, such facts as the deponent is able to prove from his own knowledge and such facts as he has reasonable ground to believe to be true, and in the latter case, the deponent shall clearly state the grounds of such belief. ( 3 ) The Court may order any scandalous and irrelevant matter in the affidavit to be struck out or amended.
summary: This section prescribes who may administer oaths for affidavits and the content requirements. The deponent must state facts based on personal knowledge or belief with disclosed grounds. The court can strike out scandalous or irrelevant material.
ingredients: Affidavits may be sworn before a Judge, Judicial or Executive Magistrate, a Commissioner of Oaths appointed by a High Court or Court of Session, or a notary under the Notaries Act, 1952.
Affidavits must be limited to facts the deponent can prove from personal knowledge.
If the deponent includes facts believed to be true, the grounds for such belief must be clearly stated.
The court may strike out or amend any scandalous or irrelevant matter in the affidavit.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 333, Page 11

title: Proof of previous conviction or acquittal
description: 334.  In any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, a previous conviction or acquittal may be proved, in addition to any other mode provided by any law for the time being in force,— ( a ) by an extract certified under the hand of the officer having the custody of the records of the Court in which such conviction or acquittal was held, to be a copy of the sentence or order; or ( b ) in case of a conviction, either by a certificate signed by the officer in charge of the Jail in which the punishment or any part thereof was undergone, or by production of the warrant of commitment under which the punishment was suffered, together with, in each of such cases, evidence as to the identity of the accused person with the person so convicted or acquitted.
summary: The section allows a prior conviction or acquittal to be proved in current proceedings by certified extracts or certificates. It specifies the documents and identity evidence required for such proof.
ingredients: A certified extract of the sentence or order from the court where the prior conviction/acquittal was recorded may be used.
For a conviction, a certificate from the jail officer or the warrant of commitment may be produced.
In each case, evidence must be shown linking the accused in the current case with the person previously convicted or acquitted.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 334, Page 11

title: Deposition of witnesses in absence of accused
description: 335.  ( 1 )  If  it  is  proved  that  an  accused  person  has  absconded,  and  that  there is  no immediate prospect of arresting him, the Court competent to try, or commit for trial, such person for the offence complained of may, in his absence, examine the witnesses (if any) produced on behalf of the prosecution, and record their depositions and any such deposition may, on the arrest of such person, be given in evidence against him on the inquiry into, or trial for, the offence with which he is charged, if the deponent is dead or incapable of giving evidence  or  cannot  be  found  or  his  presence  cannot  be  procured  without  an  amount  of delay,  expense  or  inconvenience  which,  under  the  circumstances  of  the  case,  would  be unreasonable. ( 2 ) If it appears that an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life has been  committed  by  some  person  or  persons  unknown,  the  High  Court  or  the  Sessions Judge may direct that any Magistrate of the first class shall hold an inquiry and examine any witnesses  who  can  give  evidence  concerning  the offence  and  any  depositions  so  taken may be given in evidence against any person who is subsequently accused of the offence, if the deponent is dead or incapable of giving evidence or beyond the limits of India.
summary: When an accused is absconding and cannot be promptly arrested, the court may record witness depositions in his absence. The depositions become admissible once the accused is arrested, provided the witness is unavailable or incapacitated. For capital or life‑imprisonment offences, a magistrate may hold an inquiry and record such depositions.
ingredients: The accused must be proved to have absconded with no immediate prospect of arrest.
The competent court may examine prosecution witnesses and record their depositions in the accused's absence.

The recorded deposition may be used as evidence after the accused is arrested, if the witness is dead, incapable, cannot be found, or securing his presence would cause unreasonable delay, expense, or inconvenience.

For offences punishable with death or life imprisonment committed by unknown persons, the High Court or Sessions Judge may order a first‑class magistrate to hold an inquiry and record witness depositions.
Such depositions are admissible against a subsequently accused person if the witness is dead, incapable, or outside India.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 335, Page 11

title: Deposition of successor officer when original officer unavailable
description: 336.  Where any document or report prepared by a public servant, scientific expert, medical officer or investigating officer is purported to be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, and— ( i ) such public servant, expert or officer is either transferred, retired, or died; or ( ii )  such  public  servant,  expert  or  officer  cannot  be found  or  is  incapable  of giving deposition; or ( iii ) securing presence of such public servant, expert, or officer is likely to cause delay in holding the inquiry, trial or other proceeding, the  Court  shall  secure  presence  of  successor  officer  of  such  public servant,  expert,  or officer who is holding that post at the time of such deposition to give deposition on such document or report.
summary: If a public servant, expert, or officer who prepared a document cannot give evidence because of transfer, retirement, death, unavailability, or potential delay, the court must obtain a deposition from the successor officer holding the same post.
ingredients: The document or report must be intended for use as evidence in an inquiry, trial, or proceeding.
The original officer is unavailable due to transfer, retirement, death, inability to locate, incapacity, or likely delay.
The court must secure the presence of the successor officer currently holding the same post to give a deposition on the document or report.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 336, Page 11

title: Double jeopardy and related provisions
description: 338.
