( 2 ) An Additional Sessions Judge or a Chief Judicial Magistrate shall hear only such appeals as the Sessions Judge of the division may, by general or special order, make over to him or as the High Court may, by special order, direct him to hear.
summary: Section 422 outlines which judges may hear appeals to the Court of Session, including provisions for appeals against convictions by second class magistrates. It also limits the jurisdiction of Additional Sessions Judges and Chief Judicial Magistrates to appeals assigned by higher authority.
ingredients: Appeal to Court of Session or Sessions Judge shall be heard by the Sessions Judge or an Additional Sessions Judge.
Appeal against a conviction on a trial held by a Magistrate of the second class may be heard and disposed of by the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
An Additional Sessions Judge or a Chief Judicial Magistrate shall hear only appeals assigned by the Sessions Judge of the division or directed by the High Court.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 422, Page 14

description: Every appeal shall be made in the form of a petition in writing presented by the appellant or his advocate, and every such petition shall (unless the Court to which it is presented otherwise directs) be accompanied by a copy of the judgment or order appealed against.
summary: Section 423 requires that an appeal be filed as a written petition by the appellant or counsel, and that it be accompanied by the judgment or order being appealed, unless the court orders otherwise.
ingredients: Appeal must be a written petition presented by the appellant or his advocate.
Petition must be accompanied by a copy of the judgment or order appealed against unless the court directs otherwise.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 423, Page 14

description: If the appellant is in jail, he may present his petition of appeal and the copies accompanying the same to the officer in charge of the jail, who shall thereupon forward such petition and copies to the proper Appellate Court.
summary: Section 424 provides a procedure for an appellant who is incarcerated to submit his appeal petition and supporting documents to the jail officer, who must forward them to the appropriate appellate court.
ingredients: Incarcerated appellant may present the appeal petition and accompanying copies to the jail officer.
The jail officer shall forward the petition and copies to the proper Appellate Court.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 424, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) If upon examining the petition of appeal and copy of the judgment received under section 423 or section 424, the Appellate Court considers that there is no sufficient ground for interfering, it may dismiss the appeal summarily: Provided that— ( a ) no appeal presented under section 423 shall be dismissed unless the appellant or his advocate has had a reasonable opportunity of being heard in support of the same; ( b ) no appeal presented under section 424 shall be dismissed except after giving the appellant a reasonable opportunity of being heard in support of the same, unless the Appellate Court considers that the appeal is frivolous or that the production of the accused in custody before the Court would involve such inconvenience as would be disproportionate in the circumstances of the case; ( c ) no appeal presented under section 424 shall be dismissed summarily until the period allowed for preferring such appeal has expired. ( 2 ) Before dismissing an appeal under this section, the Court may call for the record of the case.
summary: Section 425 allows the appellate court to dismiss an appeal summarily if there is no sufficient ground, but imposes safeguards: a hearing opportunity for appeals under section 423, a hearing opportunity and checks for frivolousness for appeals under section 424, and a prohibition on summary dismissal of section 424 appeals before the appeal period expires. The court may also request the case record before dismissal.
ingredients: Appellate Court may dismiss an appeal summarily if there is no sufficient ground for interfering.
For appeals under section 423, dismissal requires that the appellant or advocate has had a reasonable opportunity to be heard.
For appeals under section 424, dismissal requires a reasonable opportunity to be heard unless the appeal is frivolous or producing the accused would cause disproportionate inconvenience.
Appeals under section 424 cannot be dismissed summarily until the period allowed for preferring the appeal has expired.
Before dismissing, the Court may call for the record of the case.
exceptions: no appeal presented under section 423 shall be dismissed unless the appellant or his advocate has had a reasonable opportunity of being heard in support of the same

no appeal presented under section 424 shall be dismissed except after giving the appellant a reasonable opportunity of being heard in support of the same, unless the Appellate Court considers that the appeal is frivolous or that the production of the accused in custody before the Court would involve such inconvenience as would be disproportionate in the circumstances of the case
no appeal presented under section 424 shall be dismissed summarily until the period allowed for preferring such appeal has expired
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 425, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) If the Appellate Court does not dismiss the appeal summarily, it shall cause notice of the time and place at which such appeal will be heard to be given— ( i ) to the appellant or his advocate; ( ii ) to such officer as the State Government may appoint in this behalf; ( iii ) if the appeal is from a judgment of conviction in a case instituted upon complaint, to the complainant; ( iv ) if the appeal is under section 419 or section 420, to the accused, and shall also furnish such officer, complainant and accused with a copy of the grounds of appeal. ( 2 ) The Appellate Court shall then send for the record of the case, if such record is not already available in that Court, and hear the parties: Provided that if the appeal is only as to the extent or the legality of the sentence, the Court may dispose of the appeal without sending for the record. ( 3 ) Where the only ground for appeal from a conviction is the alleged severity of the sentence, the appellant shall not, except with the leave of the Court, urge or be heard in support of any other ground.
summary: Section 426 mandates that, when an appeal is not dismissed summarily, the appellate court must give notice of the hearing to the appellant, appointed officer, complainant (if applicable), and accused (for appeals under sections 419 or 420), along with the grounds of appeal. The court must obtain the case record unless the appeal concerns only the sentence's extent or legality, in which case it may proceed without the record. If the sole ground is sentence severity, the appellant cannot raise other grounds without the court's permission.
ingredients: If appeal is not dismissed summarily, notice of time and place of hearing must be given to the appellant or advocate.
Notice must also be given to the officer appointed by the State Government.
If the appeal arises from a complaint‑initiated conviction, notice must be given to the complainant.
If the appeal is under section 419 or 420, notice must be given to the accused, and the officer, complainant, and accused must receive a copy of the grounds of appeal.
The court shall send for the case record if not already available and hear the parties, unless the appeal concerns only the extent or legality of the sentence.
When the only ground is alleged severity of the sentence, the appellant may not raise or be heard on any other ground without the court's leave.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 426, Page 14

description: After perusing such record and hearing the appellant or his advocate, if he appears, and the Public Prosecutor if he appears, and in case of an appeal under section 418 or section 419, the accused, if he appears, the Appellate Court may, if it considers that there is no sufficient ground for interfering, dismiss the appeal, or may— ( a ) in an appeal from an order or acquittal, reverse such order and direct that further inquiry be made, or that the accused be re-tried or committed for trial, as the case may be, or find him guilty and pass sentence on him according to law; ( b ) in an appeal from a conviction— ( i ) reverse the finding and sentence and acquit or discharge the accused, or order him to be re-tried by a Court of competent jurisdiction subordinate to such Appellate Court or committed for trial, or ( ii ) alter the finding, maintaining the sentence, or ( iii ) with or without altering the finding, alter the nature or the extent, or the nature and extent, of the sentence, but not so as to enhance the same; ( c ) in an appeal for enhancement of sentence— ( i ) reverse the finding and sentence and acquit or discharge the accused or order him to be re-tried by a Court competent to try the offence, or ( ii ) alter the finding maintaining the sentence, or ( iii ) with or without altering the finding, alter the nature or the extent, or, the nature and extent, of the sentence, so as to enhance or reduce the same; ( d ) in an appeal from any other order, alter or reverse such order; ( e ) make any amendment or any consequential or incidental order that may be just or proper:
summary: Section 427 gives the appellate court powers after reviewing the record and hearing parties: it may dismiss the appeal if no sufficient ground exists, or it may reverse, alter, or modify orders and sentences in various scenarios, including orders/acquittals, convictions, sentence enhancements, and any other orders, and may also make consequential amendments.
ingredients: Court may dismiss the appeal if there is no sufficient ground for interfering.
In appeals from an order or acquittal, the court may reverse the order and direct further inquiry, re‑trial, or convict and sentence the accused.
In appeals from a conviction, the court may reverse and acquit, order re‑trial, alter the finding while maintaining the sentence, or alter the nature or extent of the sentence without enhancing it.
In appeals for enhancement of sentence, the court may reverse and acquit, alter the finding while maintaining the sentence, or alter the nature or extent of the sentence to enhance or reduce it.
In appeals from any other order, the court may alter or reverse that order.
The court may make any amendment or consequential/incidental order that is just or proper.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 427, Page 14

description: The rules contained in Chapter XXVIII as to the judgment of a Criminal Court of original jurisdiction shall apply, so far as may be practicable, to the judgment in appeal of a Court of Session or Chief Judicial Magistrate: Provided that, unless the Appellate Court otherwise directs, the accused shall not be brought up, or required to attend, to hear judgment delivered.
summary: Section 428 extends the procedural rules of Chapter XXVIII on judgments of courts of original jurisdiction to appellate judgments of Sessions Courts or Chief Judicial Magistrates, with the proviso that the accused need not be present to hear the judgment unless the appellate court orders otherwise.
ingredients: Rules of Chapter XXVIII on judgments of original jurisdiction apply to appellate judgments of a Court of Session or Chief Judicial Magistrate as far as practicable.
Unless the Appellate Court directs otherwise, the accused shall not be brought up or required to attend to hear the judgment delivered.
exceptions: Provided that, unless the Appellate Court otherwise directs, the accused shall not be brought up, or required to attend, to hear judgment delivered.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 428, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) Whenever a case is decided on appeal by the High Court under this Chapter, it shall certify its judgment or order to the Court by which the finding, sentence or order appealed against was recorded or passed and if such Court is that of a Judicial Magistrate other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate, the High Court's judgment or order shall be sent through the Chief Judicial Magistrate, and if such Court is that of an Executive Magistrate, the High Court's judgment or order shall be sent through the District Magistrate. ( 2 ) The Court to which the High Court certifies its judgment or order shall thereupon make such orders as are conformable to the judgment or order of the High Court; and if necessary, the record shall be amended in accordance therewith.
summary: Section 429 requires the High Court, when deciding an appeal, to certify its judgment to the court that originally passed the judgment or order, routing the certification through the Chief Judicial Magistrate for judicial magistrate courts (other than the chief) and through the District Magistrate for executive magistrate courts. The receiving court must then implement orders conforming to the High Court's judgment and amend the record if needed.
ingredients: High Court must certify its judgment or order to the court that recorded or passed the appealed finding, sentence, or order.
If the original court is a Judicial Magistrate other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate, the certification is sent through the Chief Judicial Magistrate.
If the original court is an Executive Magistrate, the certification is sent through the District Magistrate.
The certified court must make orders conformable to the High Court's judgment or order.
If necessary, the court must amend the record in accordance with the High Court's judgment.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 429, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) Pending any appeal by a convicted person, the Appellate Court may, for reasons to be recorded by it in writing, order that the execution of the sentence or order appealed against be suspended and, also, if he is i
summary: Section 430 allows the appellate court, during the pendency of an appeal by a convicted person, to suspend the execution of the sentence or order appealed against, provided the reasons are recorded in writing.
ingredients: During a pending appeal by a convicted person, the Appellate Court may, with written reasons, order suspension of execution of the sentence or order appealed against.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 430, Page 14

description: When an appeal is presented under section 419, the High Court may issue a warrant directing that the accused be arrested and brought before it or any Subordinate Court, and the Court before which he is brought may commit him to prison pending the disposal of the appeal or admit him to bail. Judgments of Subordinate Appellate Court. Order of High Court on appeal to be certified to lower Court. Suspension of sentence pending the appeal; release of appellant on bail. Arrest of accused in appeal from acquittal.
summary: When an appeal under section 419 is filed, the High Court can issue a warrant for the accused's arrest and may order detention pending the appeal, or may admit the accused on bail.
ingredients: High Court may issue a warrant directing arrest of the accused and production before it or a Subordinate Court.
The Court before which the accused is brought may commit him to prison pending disposal of the appeal.
The Court may admit the accused to bail.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 431, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) In dealing with any appeal under this Chapter, the Appellate Court, if it thinks additional evidence to be necessary, shall record its reasons and may either take such evidence itself, or direct it to be taken by a Magistrate or, when the Appellate Court is a High Court, by a Court of Session or a Magistrate. ( 2 ) When the additional evidence is taken by the Court of Session or the Magistrate, it or he shall certify such evidence to the Appellate Court, and such Court shall thereupon proceed to dispose of the appeal. ( 3 ) The accused or his advocate shall have the right to be present when the additional evidence is taken. ( 4 ) The taking of evidence under this section shall be subject to the provisions of Chapter XXIV, as if it were an inquiry.
summary: The appellate court may order additional evidence in an appeal, either taking it itself or directing a magistrate or session court to do so; the evidence must be certified, and the accused has a right to be present.
ingredients: Appellate Court may deem additional evidence necessary.
It must record reasons for ordering additional evidence.
May take evidence itself or direct a Magistrate, or if High Court, a Court of Session or Magistrate to take it.
If taken by lower court, that court must certify the evidence to the Appellate Court.
Accused or advocate must be present when additional evidence is taken.
Taking of evidence is subject to provisions of Chapter XXIV as if it were an inquiry.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 432, Page 14

description: When an appeal under this Chapter is heard by a High Court before a Bench of Judges and they are divided in opinion, the appeal, with their opinions, shall be laid before another Judge of that Court, and that Judge, after such hearing as he thinks fit, shall deliver his opinion, and the judgment or order shall follow that opinion: Provided that if one of the Judges constituting the Bench, or, where the appeal is laid before another Judge under this section, that Judge, so requires, the appeal shall be re-heard and decided by a larger Bench of Judges.
summary: If a High Court bench is divided on an appeal, the matter is referred to another judge whose opinion becomes decisive; the appeal may be reheard by a larger bench if required.
ingredients: When the Bench is divided, the appeal with opinions is laid before another Judge of the Court.
That Judge hears as he thinks fit and delivers his opinion.
The judgment or order follows that opinion.
Provided that if any Judge (including the one to whom the appeal is laid) so requires, the appeal shall be reheard by a larger Bench.
exceptions: Provided that if one of the Judges constituting the Bench, or, where the appeal is laid before another Judge under this section, that Judge, so requires, the appeal shall be re-heard and decided by a larger Bench of Judges.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 433, Page 14

description: Judgments and orders passed by an Appellate Court upon an appeal shall be final, except in the cases provided for in section 418, section 419, sub-section ( 4 ) of section 425 or Chapter XXXII: Provided that notwithstanding the final disposal of an appeal against conviction in any case, the Appellate Court may hear and dispose of, on the merits,— ( a ) an appeal against acquittal under section 419, arising out of the same case, or ( b ) an appeal for the enhancement of sentence under section 418, arising out of the same case.
summary: Orders of an appellate court are final except for specific statutes; however, even after final disposal, the court may entertain appeals against acquittal under section 419 or sentence enhancement under section 418 arising from the same case.
ingredients: Judgments and orders of an Appellate Court on an appeal are final.
Exception: cases provided for in section 418, section 419, sub-section (4) of section 425, or Chapter XXXII.
Notwithstanding final disposal, the Appellate Court may hear on merits:
a) an appeal against acquittal under section 419 arising from the same case.
b) an appeal for enhancement of sentence under section 418 arising from the same case.
exceptions: except in the cases provided for in section 418, section 419, sub-section ( 4 ) of section 425 or Chapter XXXII
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 434, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) Every other appeal under section 418 or section 419 shall finally abate on the death of the accused. ( 2 ) Every other appeal under this Chapter (except an appeal from a sentence of fine) shall finally abate on the death of the appellant: Provided that where the appeal is against a conviction and sentence of death or of imprisonment, and the appellant dies during the pendency of the appeal, any of his near relatives may, within thirty days of the death of the appellant, apply to the Appellate Court for leave to continue the appeal; and if leave is granted, the appeal shall not abate. Explanation .—In this section, "near relative" means a parent, spouse, lineal descendant, brother or sister.
summary: Appeals under sections 418 or 419 abate upon death of the accused/appellant, but near relatives may apply within 30 days for leave to continue the appeal; if granted, the appeal proceeds.
ingredients: Every other appeal under section 418 or section 419 abates on death of the accused.
Every other appeal under this Chapter (except appeal from a sentence of fine) abates on death of the appellant.

Provided that if the appeal is against a conviction and sentence of death or imprisonment and the appellant dies during pendency, any near relative may apply within thirty days for leave to continue the appeal.
If leave is granted, the appeal does not abate.
exceptions: Provided that where the appeal is against a conviction and sentence of death or of imprisonment, and the appellant dies during the pendency of the appeal, any of his near relatives may, within thirty days of the death of the appellant, apply to the Appellate Court for leave to continue the appeal; and if leave is granted, the appeal shall not abate.
explanation: In this section, "near relative" means a parent, spouse, lineal descendant, brother or sister.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 435, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) Where any Court is satisfied that a case pending before it involves a question as to the validity of any Act, Ordinance or Regulation or of any provision contained in an Act, Ordinance or Regulation, the determination of which is necessary for the disposal of the case, and is of opinion that such Act, Ordinance, Regulation or provision is invalid or inoperative, but has not been so declared by the High Court to which that Court is Subordinate or by the Supreme Court, the Court shall state a case setting out its opinion and the reasons therefor, and refer the same for the decision of the High Court. Appellate Court may take further evidence or direct it to be taken. Procedure where Judges of Court of Appeal are equally divided. Finality of judgments and orders on appeal. Abatement of appeals. Reference to High Court. 5 10 15 20 25 30 35 40 45
summary: If a court believes a law involved in a case is invalid but not yet declared so by higher courts, it must state its opinion and refer the question to the High Court; the appellate court may seek further evidence.
ingredients: Court is satisfied that the case involves a question on validity of an Act, Ordinance, Regulation or provision.
Court opines that the law is invalid or inoperative and has not been declared so by the High Court or Supreme Court.
Court must state a case with its opinion and reasons.
Court refers the matter to the High Court for decision.
Appellate Court may take further evidence or direct it to be taken.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 436, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) When a question has been so referred, the High Court shall pass such order thereon as it thinks fit, and shall cause a copy of such order to be sent to the Court by which the reference was made, which shall dispose of the case conformably to the said order. ( 2 ) The High Court may direct by whom the costs of such reference shall be paid.
summary: Upon a reference, the High Court may pass any order it deems appropriate, must send a copy to the referring court which must act accordingly, and may decide who bears the costs.
ingredients: High Court passes an order as it thinks fit on the referred question.
High Court sends a copy of the order to the referring Court.
The referring Court disposes of the case in conformity with the High Court's order.
High Court may direct who shall pay the costs of the reference.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 437, Page 14

description: ( 1 ) The High Court or any Sessions Judge may call for and examine the record of any proceeding before any inferior Criminal Court situate within its or his local jurisdiction for the purpose of satisfying itself or himself as to the correctness, legality or propriety of any finding, sentence or order, recorded or passed, and as to the regularity of any proceedings of such inferior Court, and may, when calling, for such record, direct that the execution of any sentence or order be suspended, and if the accused is in confinement that he be released on bail or on his own bond pending the examination of the record. Explanation .—All Magistrates, whether Executive or Judicial, and whether exercising original or appellate jurisdiction, shall be deemed to be inferior to the Sessions Judge for the purposes of this sub-section and of section 439. ( 2 ) The powers of revision conferred by sub-section ( 1 ) shall not be exercised in relation to any interlocutory order passed in any appeal, inquiry, trial or other proceeding. ( 3 ) If an application u
summary: The High Court or Sessions Judge may call for records of inferior criminal courts to examine the correctness of orders, may suspend execution and release the accused on bail pending examination; revision powers do not extend to interlocutory orders.
ingredients: High Court or Sessions Judge may call for and examine the record of any proceeding before an inferior criminal court within its jurisdiction.
Purpose: to verify correctness, legality, propriety of findings, sentences, orders, and regularity of proceedings.
May direct suspension of execution of any sentence or order.
If the accused is in confinement, may release him on bail or his own bond pending examination.
Explanation defines all Magistrates as inferior to the Sessions Judge for this sub-section and section 439.
Sub-section (2) restricts revision powers from being exercised on interlocutory orders in any appeal, inquiry, trial or other proceeding.
Sub-section (3) is truncated in the source.
explanation: All Magistrates, whether Executive or Judicial, and whether exercising original or appellate jurisdiction, shall be deemed to be inferior to the Sessions Judge for the purposes of this sub-section and of section 439.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 438, Page 14

description: On examining any record under section 438 or otherwise, the High Court or the Sessions Judge may direct the Chief Judicial Magistrate by himself or by any of the Magistrates subordinate to him to make, and the Chief Judicial Magistrate may himself make or direct any subordinate Magistrate to make, further inquiry into any complaint which has been dismissed under section 226 or sub-section (4) of section 227, or into the case of any person accused of an offence who has been discharged: Provided that no Court shall make any direction under this section for inquiry into the case of any person who has been discharged unless such person has had an opportunity of showing cause why such direction should not be made.
summary: The High Court or Sessions Judge can order a Chief Judicial Magistrate to investigate complaints dismissed under section 226/227 or a discharged accused, but only after the person is given a chance to show cause against such inquiry.
ingredients: Examination of any record under section 438 or otherwise
Direction to the Chief Judicial Magistrate (or subordinate magistrate) to make further inquiry into a complaint dismissed under section 226 or sub‑section (4) of section 227
Direction to inquire into the case of a person accused of an offence who has been discharged
The discharged person must be given an opportunity to show cause why such direction should not be made
exceptions: Provided that no Court shall make any direction under this section for inquiry into the case of any person who has been discharged unless such person has had an opportunity of showing cause why such direction should not be made.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 439, Page 15

description: (1) In the case of any proceeding the record of which has been called for by himself, the Sessions Judge may exercise all or any of the powers which may be exercised by the High Court under sub-section (1) of section 442. (2) Where any proceeding by way of revision is commenced before a Sessions Judge under sub-section (1), the provisions of sub-sections (2), (3), (4) and (5) of section 442 shall, so far as may be, apply to such proceeding and references in the said sub-sections to the High Court shall be construed as references to the Sessions Judge. (3) Where any application for revision is made by or on behalf of any person before the Sessions Judge, the decision of the Sessions Judge thereon in relation to such person shall be final and no further proceeding by way of revision at the instance of such person shall be entertained by the High Court or any other Court.
summary: A Sessions Judge can exercise the same revision powers as the High Court for proceedings whose records are called for, and the provisions of section 442 apply as if the High Court were the revising authority; the Judge's decision on a revision application is final.
ingredients: If a proceeding's record is called for by the Sessions Judge, he may exercise all powers of the High Court under section 442(1)
When revision is commenced before the Sessions Judge, sections 442(2)-(5) apply, with references to the High Court read as references to the Sessions Judge
A decision of the Sessions Judge on a revision application is final; no further revision can be sought in any other court
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 440, Page 15

description: An Additional Sessions Judge shall have and may exercise all the powers of a Sessions Judge under this Chapter in respect of any case which may be transferred to him by or under any general or special order of the Sessions Judge. Disposal of case according to decision of High Court. Calling for records to exercise powers of revision. Power to order inquiry. Sessions Judge's powers of revision. Power of Additional Sessions Judge.
summary: An Additional Sessions Judge can exercise all the powers of a Sessions Judge for any case transferred to him by order of the Sessions Judge.
ingredients: A case may be transferred to an Additional Sessions Judge by a general or special order of the Sessions Judge
The Additional Sessions Judge may exercise all powers of a Sessions Judge under this Chapter
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 441, Page 15

description: (1) In the case of any proceeding the record of which has been called for by itself or which otherwise comes to its knowledge, the High Court may, in its discretion, exercise any of the powers conferred on a Court of Appeal by sections 427, 430, 431 and 432 or on a Court of Session by section 344, and, when the Judges composing the Court of Revision are equally divided in opinion, the case shall be disposed of in the manner provided by section 433. (2) No order under this section shall be made to the prejudice of the accused or other person unless he has had an opportunity of being heard either personally or by advocate in his own defence. (3) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to authorise a High Court to convert a finding of acquittal into one conviction. (4) Where under this Sanhita an appeal lies and no appeal is brought, no proceeding by way of revision shall be entertained at the instance of the party who could have appealed. (5) Where under this Sanhita an appeal lies but an application for revision has been made to the High Court by any person and the High Court is satisfied that such application was made under the erroneous belief that no appeal lies thereto and that it is necessary in the interests of justice so to do, the High Court may treat the application for revision as a petition of appeal and deal with the same accordingly.
summary: The High Court may exercise appellate powers for revision proceedings, must hear the accused before any order prejudicial to them, cannot turn an acquittal into a conviction, and may entertain revision only when an appeal exists or when a mistaken belief about the absence of appeal is corrected.
ingredients: If a proceeding's record is called for, the High Court may exercise any appellate powers under sections 427, 430, 431, 432 or section 344
If the Court of Revision is equally divided, disposal follows section 433
No order may prejudice the accused unless he has been given an opportunity to be heard personally or through counsel
The High Court cannot convert an acquittal into a conviction
If an appeal exists but is not filed, revision cannot be entertained by the party who could have appealed
If an application for revision is filed under a mistaken belief that no appeal exists, the High Court may treat it as an appeal and decide accordingly
exceptions: No order under this section shall be made to the prejudice of the accused or other person unless he has had an opportunity of being heard either personally or by advocate in his own defence.
Nothing in this section shall be deemed to authorise a High Court to convert a finding of acquittal into one conviction.
Where under this Sanhita an appeal lies and no appeal is brought, no proceeding by way of revision shall be entertained at the instance of the party who could have appealed.

Where under this Sanhita an appeal lies but an application for revision has been made to the High Court by any person and the High Court is satisfied that such application was made under the erroneous belief that no appeal lies thereto and that it is necessary in the interests of justice so to do, the High Court may treat the application for revision as a petition of appeal and deal with the same accordingly.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 442, Page 15

description: (1) Whenever one or more persons convicted at the same trial makes or make application to a High Court for revision and any other person convicted at the same trial makes an application to the Sessions Judge for revision, the High Court shall decide, having regard to the general convenience of the parties and the importance of the questions involved, which of the two Courts should finally dispose of the applications for revision and when the High Court decides that all the applications for revision should be disposed of by itself, the High Court shall direct that the applications for revision pending before the Sessions Judge be transferred to itself and where the High Court decides that it is not necessary for it to dispose of the applications for revision, it shall direct that the applications for revision made to it be transferred to the Sessions Judge. (2) Whenever any application for revision is transferred to the High Court, that Court shall deal with the same as if it were an application duly made before itself. (3) Whenever any application for revision is transferred to the Sessions Judge, that Judge shall deal with the same as if it were an application duly made before himself. (4) Where an application for revision is transferred by the High Court to the Sessions Judge, no further application for revision shall lie to the High Court or to any other Court at the instance of the person or persons whose applications for revision have been disposed of by the Sessions Judge.
summary: When convicted persons apply for revision in different courts, the High Court decides which court will finally hear the applications and can transfer them accordingly; once transferred, the receiving court treats the application as if originally made there, and no further revision can be sought elsewhere.
ingredients: If some convicted persons apply to the High Court and others to the Sessions Judge, the High Court decides which court will finally dispose of all applications based on convenience and importance
If the High Court decides to retain all applications, it orders transfer of those pending before the Sessions Judge to itself
If the High Court decides not to retain, it orders transfer of its own applications to the Sessions Judge
Transferred applications are to be dealt with as if originally made before the receiving court
After a transfer to the Sessions Judge, no further revision application can be made to the High Court or any other court by those persons
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 443, Page 15

description: Save as otherwise expressly provided by this Sanhita, no party has any right to be heard either personally or by an advocate before any Court exercising its powers of revision; but the Court may, if it thinks fit, when exercising such powers, hear any party either personally or by advocate.
summary: Generally, parties are not entitled to be heard before a court exercising revision powers, but the court may choose to hear them if it deems appropriate.
ingredients: No automatic right for a party to be heard before a revising court
The revising court may, at its discretion, hear the party personally or through counsel
exceptions: Save as otherwise expressly provided by this Sanhita, no party has any right to be heard either personally or by an advocate before any Court exercising its powers of revision; but the Court may, if it thinks fit, when exercising such powers, hear any party either personally or by advocate.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 444, Page 15

description: When the record of any trial held by a Magistrate is called for by the High Court or Court of Session under section 438, the Magistrate may submit with the record a statement setting forth the grounds of his decision or order and any facts which he thinks material to the issue, and that Court shall consider such statement before overruling or setting aside the said decision or order.
summary: If a magistrate's trial record is requisitioned for revision, the magistrate may attach a statement of reasons and material facts, which the higher court must consider before overturning the decision.
ingredients: When a trial record of a Magistrate is called for under section 438, the Magistrate may submit a statement of grounds and material facts with the record
The High Court or Court of Session must consider this statement before overruling or setting aside the magistrate's decision
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 445, Page 15

description: When a case is revised under this Chapter by the High Court or a Sessions Judge, it or he shall, in the manner provided by section 429, certify its decision or order to the Court by which the finding, sentence or order revised was recorded or passed, and the Court to which the decision or order is so certified shall thereupon make such orders as are conformable to the decision so certified, and, if necessary, the record shall be amended in accordance therewith.
summary: After a revision, the revising court must certify its decision to the lower court that made the original order, which then implements the certified decision and amends the record if required.
ingredients: The revising High Court or Sessions Judge must certify its decision to the court that originally recorded the finding, sentence, or order, following the procedure of section 429
The lower court must implement orders conformable to the certified decision
If needed, the case record shall be amended accordingly
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 446, Page 15

description: (1) Whenever it is made to appear to the Supreme Court that an order under this section is expedient for the ends of justice, it may direct that any particular case or appeal be transferred from one High Court to another High Court or from a Criminal Court subordinate to one High Court to another Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction subordinate to another High Court. (2) The Supreme Court may act under this section only on the application of the Attorney-General of India or of a party interested, and every such application shall be made by motion, which shall, except when the applicant is the Attorney-General of India or the Advocate-General of the State, be supported by affidavit or affirmation. (3) Where any application for the exercise of the powers conferred by this section is dismissed, the Supreme Court may, if it is of opinion that the application was frivolous or vexatious, order the applicant to pay by way of compensation to any person who has opposed the application such sum as it may consider appropriate in the circumstances of
summary: The Supreme Court can transfer cases or appeals between High Courts or subordinate criminal courts when justice requires, upon application by the Attorney-General or an interested party, and may award compensation if a dismissed application is found frivolous or vexatious.
ingredients: If the Supreme Court is convinced that transfer is necessary for justice, it may order transfer of a case or appeal between High Courts or between subordinate criminal courts of equal or superior jurisdiction

The application for transfer must be made by the Attorney-General of India or an interested party; if the applicant is not the Attorney-General or Advocate-General, the motion must be supported by affidavit or affirmation
If a transfer application is dismissed and deemed frivolous or vexatious, the Supreme Court may order the applicant to pay compensation to any person who opposed the application
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 447, Page 15

title: Power of High Court to transfer cases and appeals
description: (1) Whenever it is made to appear to the High Court— (a) that a fair and impartial inquiry or trial cannot be had in any Criminal Court subordinate thereto, or (b) that some question of law of unusual difficulty is likely to arise, or (c) that an order under this section is required by any provision of this Sanhita, or will tend to the general convenience of the parties or witnesses, or is expedient for the ends of justice, it may order— (i) that any offence be inquired into or tried by any Court not qualified under sections 197 to 205 (both inclusive), but in other respects competent to inquire into or try such offence; (ii) that any particular case or appeal, or class of cases or appeals, be transferred from a Criminal Court subordinate to its authority to any other such Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction; (iii) that any particular case be committed for trial to a Court of Session; or (iv) that any particular case or appeal be transferred to and tried before itself. (2) The High Court may act either on the report of the lower Court, or on the application of a party interested, or on its own initiative: Provided that no application shall lie to the High Court for transferring a case from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court in the same sessions division, unless an application for such transfer has been made to the Sessions Judge and rejected by him. (3) Every application for an order under sub-section (1) shall be made by motion, which shall, except when the applicant is the Advocate-General of the State, be supported by affidavit or affirmation. (4) When such application is made by an accused person, the High Court may direct him to execute a bond, with or without sureties, for the payment of any compensation which the High Court may award under sub-section (7). (5) Every accused person making such application shall give to the Public Prosecutor notice in writing of the application, together with a copy of the grounds on which it is made; and no order shall be made on the merits of the applications unless at least twenty-four hours have elapsed between the giving of such notice and the hearing of the application. (6) Where the application is for the transfer of a case or appeal from any Subordinate Court, the High Court may, if it is satisfied that it is necessary so to do in the interest of justice, order that, pending the disposal of the application the proceedings in the Subordinate Court shall be stayed, on such terms as the High Court may think fit to impose: Provided that such stay shall not affect the Subordinate Court's power of remand under section 346. (7) Where an application for an order under sub-section (1) is dismissed, the High Court may, if it is of opinion that the application was frivolous or vexatious, order the applicant to pay by way of compensation to any person who has opposed the application such sum as it may consider proper in the circumstances of the case. (8) When the High Court orders under sub-section (1) that a case be transferred from any Court for trial before itself, it shall observe in such trial the same procedure which that Court would have observed if the case had not been so transferred. (9) Nothing in this section shall be deemed to affect any order of Government under section 218.
summary: The High Court may transfer a criminal case or appeal to another court when fairness, legal difficulty, statutory requirement, convenience, or justice demands it. Applications must be made by motion, supported by affidavit (except for the Advocate-General), give notice to the Public Prosecutor, and observe a 24‑hour notice period. The Court may stay subordinate proceedings, impose compensation, and must follow the original court's procedure after transfer.
ingredients: Fair and impartial inquiry or trial cannot be had in the subordinate Criminal Court
A question of law of unusual difficulty is likely to arise
An order under this section is required by any provision of this Sanhita
The order will tend to the general convenience of parties or witnesses
The order is expedient for the ends of justice
Application must be made by motion
Application must be supported by affidavit or affirmation, except when the applicant is the Advocate‑General of the State
If the applicant is an accused, the High Court may require execution of a bond with or without sureties
Accused must give written notice of the application to the Public Prosecutor together with the grounds
At least twenty‑four hours must elapse between giving notice and hearing the application
If the application seeks transfer of a case or appeal from a Subordinate Court, the High Court may stay the subordinate proceedings, subject to the proviso on remand power
If an application is dismissed as frivolous or vexatious, the Court may order compensation to the opposing party
exceptions: Provided that no application shall lie to the High Court for transferring a case from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court in the same sessions division, unless an application for such transfer has been made to the Sessions Judge and rejected by him.
Provided that such stay shall not affect the Subordinate Court's power of remand under section 346.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 448, Page 15

title: Power of Sessions Judge to transfer cases and appeals
description: (1) Whenever it is made to appear to a Sessions Judge that an order under this sub-section is expedient for the ends of justice, he may order that any particular case be transferred from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court in his sessions division. (2) The Sessions Judge may act either on the report of the lower Court, or on the application of a party interested, or on his own initiative. (3) The provisions of sub-sections (3), (4), (5), (6), (7) and (9) of section 448 shall apply in relation to an application to the Sessions Judge for an order under sub-section (1) as they apply in relation to an application to the High Court for an order under sub-section (1) of section 448, except that sub-section (7) of that section shall so apply as if for the word "sum" occurring therein, the words "sum not exceeding ten thousand rupees" were substituted.
summary: A Sessions Judge can transfer a case within his division when justice requires it. The Judge may act on a report, an application, or on his own initiative, and the procedural rules of Section 448 (including notice, affidavit, and compensation provisions) apply, with the compensation cap limited to ten thousand rupees.
ingredients: It must appear to the Sessions Judge that the transfer is expedient for the ends of justice
Application may be made on the report of the lower Court, on an application of an interested party, or on the Judge's own initiative
Procedural requirements of Section 448 sub‑sections (3) to (9) apply, with the compensation amount limited to ten thousand rupees
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 449, Page 15

title: Withdrawal and recall of cases or appeals by Sessions Judge
description: (1) A Sessions Judge may withdraw any case or appeal from, or recall any case or appeal which he has made over to a Chief Judicial Magistrate subordinate to him. (2) At any time before the trial of the case or the hearing of the appeal has commenced before the Additional Sessions Judge, a Sessions Judge may recall any case or appeal which he has made over to any Additional Sessions Judge. (3) Where a Sessions Judge withdraws or recalls case or appeal under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2), he may either try the case in his own Court or hear the appeal himself, or make it over in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita to another Court for trial or hearing, as the case may be.
summary: A Sessions Judge has the authority to withdraw or recall a case or appeal that he has previously sent to a Chief Judicial Magistrate or an Additional Sessions Judge, provided the trial or hearing has not yet begun. After withdrawal or recall, the Judge may try the matter himself, hear the appeal, or re‑assign it to another appropriate court.
ingredients: The Sessions Judge may withdraw or recall a case/appeal sent to a Chief Judicial Magistrate subordinate to him
The Sessions Judge may recall a case/appeal sent to an Additional Sessions Judge before the trial or hearing commences
After withdrawal or recall, the Judge may try the case himself, hear the appeal, or re‑assign it to another court as per the Sanhita
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 450, Page 15

title: Withdrawal and recall of cases by Chief Judicial Magistrate
description: (1) Any Chief Judicial Magistrate may withdraw any case from, or recall any case which he has made over to, any Magistrate subordinate to him, and may inquire into or try such case himself, or refer it for inquiry or trial to any other such Magistrate competent to inquire into or try the same. (2) Any Judicial Magistrate may recall any case made over by him under sub-section (2) of section 213 to any other Magistrate and may inquire into or try such cases himself.
summary: A Chief Judicial Magistrate can withdraw or recall a case that he has sent to a subordinate magistrate and may either try the case himself or refer it to another competent magistrate. Similarly, a Judicial Magistrate may recall a case he previously transferred under Section 213 and may try it himself.
ingredients: Chief Judicial Magistrate may withdraw or recall a case sent to a subordinate magistrate
Chief Judicial Magistrate may inquire into or try the case himself, or refer it to another competent magistrate
Judicial Magistrate may recall a case he transferred under sub‑section (2) of section 213 to any other magistrate
Judicial Magistrate may inquire into or try the recalled case himself
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 451, Page 15

title: Transfer and withdrawal of cases by District or Sub‑Divisional Magistrate
description: Any District Magistrate or Sub-Divisional Magistrate may— (a) make over, for disposal, any proceeding which has been started before him, to any Magistrate subordinate to him; (b) withdraw any case from, or recall any case which he has made over to, any Magistrate subordinate to him, and dispose of such proceeding himself or refer it for disposal to any other Magistrate.
summary: A District or Sub‑Divisional Magistrate may either forward a proceeding to a subordinate magistrate, withdraw or recall a case already sent to a subordinate, and then either dispose of it himself or refer it to another magistrate for disposal.
ingredients: Make over any proceeding started before the District/Sub‑Divisional Magistrate to a subordinate magistrate for disposal
Withdraw or recall any case previously made over to a subordinate magistrate
After withdrawal or recall, either dispose of the proceeding himself or refer it to another magistrate
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 452, Page 15

title: Recording reasons for orders under sections 450, 451, 452 or 453
description: A Sessions Judge or Magistrate making an order under section 450, section 451, section 452 or section 453 shall record his reasons for making it.
summary: Whenever a Sessions Judge or any magistrate issues an order under Sections 450‑453, he is required to document the reasons for that order.
ingredients: When making an order under Section 450, 451, 452 or 453, the Judge or Magistrate must record the reasons for the order
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 453, Page 15

title: Execution of death sentence on receipt of High Court order
description: When in a case submitted to the High Court for the confirmation of a sentence of death, the Court of Session receives the order of confirmation or other order of the High Court thereon, it shall cause such order to be carried into effect by issuing a warrant or taking such other steps as may be necessary.
summary: If the High Court confirms a death sentence, the Court of Session must implement the order by issuing a warrant or taking any other necessary steps.
ingredients: Upon receipt of the High Court's confirmation or other order regarding a death sentence, the Court of Session must issue a warrant or take necessary steps to carry the order into effect
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 454, Page 15

title: Execution of death sentence passed by High Court in appeal or revision
description: When a sentence of death is passed by the High Court in appeal or in revision, the Court of Session shall, on receiving the order of the High Court, cause the sentence to be carried into effect by issuing a warrant.
summary: When the High Court imposes a death sentence in an appeal or revision, the Court of Session must, upon receiving the order, issue a warrant to execute the sentence.
ingredients: Upon receiving the High Court's order imposing a death sentence in appeal or revision, the Court of Session must issue a warrant to carry the sentence into effect
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 455, Page 15

title: Postponement of execution of death sentence
description: (1) Where a person is sentenced to death by the High Court and an appeal from its judgment lies to the Supreme Court under sub-clause (a) or sub-clause (b) of clause (1) of article 134 of the Constitution, the High Court shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed until the period allowed for preferring such appeal has expired, or if, an appeal is preferred within that period, until such appeal is disposed of. (2) Where a sentence of death is passed or confirmed by the High Court, and the person sentenced makes an application to the High Court for the grant of a certificate under article 132 or under sub-clause (c) of clause (1) of article 134 of the Constitution, the High Court shall order the execution of the sentence to be postponed until such application is disposed
summary: The High Court must defer the execution of a death sentence if an appeal to the Supreme Court is pending or if the convicted person has applied for a constitutional certificate; execution is postponed until the appeal is decided or the application is disposed.
ingredients: If an appeal to the Supreme Court under article 134 (sub‑clause a or b) is pending, postpone execution until the appeal period expires or the appeal is decided
If the convicted person applies to the High Court for a certificate under article 132 or article 134 (sub‑clause c), postpone execution until that application is disposed
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 456, Page 15

title: Postponement of capital sentence on pregnant woman
description: If a woman sentenced to death is found to be pregnant, the High Court shall commute the sentence to imprisonment for life.
summary: When a death‑sentenced woman is discovered to be pregnant, the High Court must commute her sentence to life imprisonment. This provision safeguards the unborn child.
ingredients: Determination that the sentenced woman is pregnant
High Court commutes death sentence to life imprisonment
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 457, Page 15

title: Power to appoint place of imprisonment
description: (1) Except when otherwise provided by any law for the time being in force, the State Government may direct in what place any person liable to be imprisoned or committed to custody under this Sanhita shall be confined. (2) If any person liable to be imprisoned or committed to custody under this Sanhita is in confinement in a civil jail, the Court or Magistrate ordering the imprisonment or committal may direct that the person be removed to a criminal jail. (3) When a person is removed to a criminal jail under sub-section (2), he shall, on being released therefrom, be sent back to the civil jail, unless either— (a) three years have elapsed since he was removed to the criminal jail, in which case he shall be deemed to have been released from the civil jail under section 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908; or (b) the Court which ordered his imprisonment in the civil jail has certified to the officer in charge of the criminal jail that he is entitled to be released under section 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. Reasons to be recorded.
summary: The State Government decides the place of imprisonment unless another law says otherwise. Courts can move a prisoner from a civil jail to a criminal jail and prescribe conditions for his return to the civil jail.
ingredients: State Government may direct the place of confinement
Court/Magistrate may order removal from civil to criminal jail
Return to civil jail after release unless (a) three years have passed or (b) civil jail court certifies release
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 458, Page 15

title: Forwarding of warrant and arrest for imprisonment
description: (1) Where the accused is sentenced to imprisonment for life or to imprisonment for a term in cases other than those provided for by section 455, the Court passing the sentence shall forthwith forward a warrant to the jail or other place in which he is, or is to be, confined, and, unless the accused is already confined in such jail or other place, shall forward him to such jail or other place, with the warrant: Provided that where the accused is sentenced to imprisonment till the rising of the Court, it shall not be necessary to prepare or forward a warrant to a jail, and the accused may be confined in such place as the Court may direct. (2) Where the accused is not present in Court when he is sentenced to such imprisonment as is mentioned in sub-section (1), the Court shall issue a warrant for his arrest for the purpose of forwarding him to the jail or other place in which he is to be confined; and in such case, the sentence shall commence on the date of his arrest.
summary: After sentencing, the court must immediately issue a warrant to the appropriate place of confinement, unless the sentence is until the court rises. If the accused is absent, a warrant for arrest is issued and the sentence starts from the arrest date.
ingredients: Court forwards warrant to jail/place of confinement immediately after sentencing
If sentenced till the rising of the Court, no warrant is required
If accused absent, court issues arrest warrant and sentence commences on arrest
exceptions: Provided that where the accused is sentenced to imprisonment till the rising of the Court, it shall not be necessary to prepare or forward a warrant to a jail, and the accused may be confined in such place as the Court may direct.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 459, Page 15

title: Direction of warrant for execution of sentence
description: Every warrant for the execution of a sentence of imprisonment shall be directed to the officer in charge of the jail or other place in which the prisoner is, or is to be, confined.
summary: All warrants for executing imprisonment sentences must be addressed to the officer in charge of the designated jail or place of confinement.
ingredients: Warrant must be addressed to the officer in charge of the jail or place of confinement
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 460, Page 15

title: Lodgement of warrant with jailor
description: When the prisoner is to be confined in a jail, the warrant shall be lodged with the jailor.
summary: If confinement is in a jail, the warrant must be lodged with the jailor.
ingredients: Warrant must be lodged with the jailor when confinement is in a jail
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 461, Page 15

title: Levy of fine
description: (1) When an offender has been sentenced to pay a fine, but no such payment has been made, the Court passing the sentence may take action for the recovery of the fine in either or both of the following ways, that is to say, it may— (a) issue a warrant for the levy of the amount by attachment and sale of any movable property belonging to the offender; (b) issue a warrant to the Collector of the district, authorising him to realise the amount as arrears of land revenue from the movable or immovable property, or both, of the defaulter: Provided that, if the sentence directs that in default of payment of the fine, the offender shall be imprisoned, and if such offender has undergone the whole of such imprisonment in default, no Court shall issue such warrant unless, for special reasons to be recorded in writing, it considers it necessary so to do, or unless it has made an order for the payment of expenses or compensation out of the fine under section 395. (2) The State Government may make rules regulating the manner in which warrants under clause (a) of sub-section (1) are to be executed, and for the summary determination of any claims made by any person other than the offender in respect of any property attached in execution of such warrant. (3) Where the Court issues a warrant to the Collector under clause (b) of sub-section (1), the Collector shall realise the amount in accordance with the law relating to recovery of arrears of land revenue, as if such warrant were a certificate issued under such law: Provided that no such warrant shall be executed by the arrest or detention in prison of the offender.
summary: If a fine remains unpaid, the court may issue a warrant to attach and sell the offender’s property or direct the Collector to recover the amount as land‑revenue arrears. Restrictions apply when the sentence includes imprisonment for default of fine payment.
ingredients: Court may issue warrant for attachment and sale of movable property (clause a)
Court may issue warrant to Collector to realise amount as land‑revenue arrears (clause b)
State Government may make rules for execution of clause (a) warrants
Collector must realise amount per land‑revenue law for clause (b) warrants
No warrant under clause (b) if offender has already served imprisonment for default unless special reasons are recorded or expenses/compensation order under section 395
exceptions: Provided that, if the sentence directs that in default of payment of the fine, the offender shall be imprisoned, and if such offender has undergone the whole of such imprisonment in default, no Court shall issue such warrant unless, for special reasons to be recorded in writing, it considers it necessary so to do, or unless it has made an order for the payment of expenses or compensation out of the fine under section 395.
Provided that no such warrant shall be executed by the arrest or detention in prison of the offender.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 462, Page 15

title: Execution of warrant under clause (a) of section 462
description: A warrant issued under clause (a) of sub-section (1) of section 462 by any Court may be executed within the local jurisdiction of such Court, and it shall authorise the attachment and sale of any such property outside such jurisdiction, when it is endorsed by the District Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction such property is found.
summary: A warrant for attachment and sale of property can be executed within the issuing court’s jurisdiction and, with District Magistrate endorsement, can also be executed outside that jurisdiction.
ingredients: Warrant may be executed within the local jurisdiction of the issuing court
Attachment and sale outside jurisdiction allowed if endorsed by the District Magistrate of the area where property is located
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 463, Page 15

title: Warrant issued by Court in territory not covered by this Sanhita
description: Notwithstanding anything contained in this Sanhita or in any other law for the time being in force, when an offender has been sentenced to pay a fine by a Criminal Court in any territory to which this Sanhita does not extend and the Court passing the sentence issues a warrant to the Collector of a district in the territories to which this Sanhita extends, authorising him to realise the amount as if it were an arrear of land revenue, such warrant shall be deemed to be a warrant issued under clause (b) of sub-section (1) of section 462 by
summary: A fine‑recovery warrant issued by a court outside the jurisdiction of this Sanhita is treated as if it were issued under clause (b) of section 462, provided it is directed to a Collector in a territory where the Sanhita applies.
ingredients: Offender sentenced to fine by a court in a non‑Sanhita territory
Court issues warrant to Collector in a Sanhita‑applicable territory
Warrant is deemed to be issued under clause (b) of section 462
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 464, Page 15

title: Fine only and imprisonment in default of payment
description: (1) When an offender has been sentenced to fine only and to imprisonment in default of payment of the fine, and the fine is not paid forthwith, the Court may— (a) order that the fine shall be payable either in full on or before a date not more than thirty days from the date of the order, or in two or three installments, of which the first shall be payable on or before a date not more than thirty days from the date of the order and the other or others at an interval or at intervals, as the case may be, of not more than thirty days; (b) suspend the execution of the sentence of imprisonment and release the offender, on the execution by the offender of a bond, with or without sureties, as the Court thinks fit, conditioned for his appearance before the Court on the date or dates on or before which payment of the fine or the installments thereof, as the case may be, is to be made; and if the amount of the fine or of any installment, as the case may be, is not realised on or before the latest date on which it is payable under the order, the Court may direct the sentence of imprisonment to be carried into execution at once. (2) The provisions of sub-section (1) shall be applicable also in any case in which an order for the payment of money has been made on non-recovery of which imprisonment
summary: If a fine remains unpaid, the court can set a payment schedule or suspend imprisonment upon bond. If payment is not made by the stipulated date, imprisonment may be executed immediately. The same rules apply when imprisonment is ordered for non‑recovery of money.
ingredients: Court may order fine payable in full within 30 days or in 2‑3 installments each not exceeding 30 days interval
Court may suspend imprisonment and release offender upon execution of a bond conditioned on appearance for fine payment
If fine or any installment is not paid by the latest due date, court may direct immediate execution of imprisonment
Sub‑section (1) also applies where imprisonment is ordered for non‑recovery of money
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 465, Page 15

description: See section 465 ( 1 ) ( b )
summary: Section 465(1)(b) is referenced in Form No. 44A relating to a bond for appearance of an offender released pending realization of fine.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 465(1)(b)

title: General provisions regarding execution
description: Every warrant for the execution of a sentence may be issued either by the Judge or Magistrate who passed the sentence, or by his successor-in-office.
summary: A warrant to execute a sentence can be issued by the judge or magistrate who sentenced the offender, or by their successor. This ensures continuity in the execution process.
ingredients: Warrant may be issued by the Judge who passed the sentence
Warrant may be issued by the Magistrate who passed the sentence
Warrant may be issued by the successor-in-office of the Judge or Magistrate
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 466, Page 15

title: Sentence on escaped convict when to take effect
description: ( 1 ) When a sentence of death, imprisonment for life or fine is passed under this Sanhita on an escaped convict, such sentence shall, subject to the provisions hereinbefore contained, take effect immediately. ( 2 ) When a sentence of imprisonment for a term is passed under this Sanhita on an escaped convict,— ( a ) if such sentence is severer in kind than the sentence which such convict was undergoing when he escaped, the new sentence shall take effect immediately; ( b ) if such sentence is not severer in kind than the sentence which such convict was undergoing when he escaped, the new sentence shall take effect after he has suffered imprisonment for a further period equal to that which, at the time of his escape, remained unexpired of his former sentence. ( 3 ) For the purposes of sub-section ( 2 ), a sentence of rigorous imprisonment shall be deemed to be severer in kind than a sentence of simple imprisonment.
summary: If an escaped convict receives a death sentence, life imprisonment, or fine, it becomes effective immediately. For term imprisonment, the new sentence takes effect immediately if it is harsher; otherwise, it takes effect after the remaining period of the original sentence is served. Rigorous imprisonment is considered harsher than simple imprisonment.
ingredients: Death, life imprisonment or fine on escaped convict takes effect immediately
If new term imprisonment is severer than the original, it takes effect immediately
If new term imprisonment is not severer, it takes effect after serving the unexpired portion of the original sentence
Rigorous imprisonment is deemed severer than simple imprisonment
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 467, Page 15

title: Sentence on offender already sentenced for another offence
description: ( 1 ) When a person already undergoing a sentence of imprisonment is sentenced on a subsequent conviction to imprisonment or imprisonment for life, such imprisonment or imprisonment for life shall commence at the expiration of the imprisonment to which he has been previously sentenced, unless the Court directs that the subsequent sentence shall run concurrently with such previous sentence: Provided that where a person who has been sentenced to imprisonment by an order under section 141 in default of furnishing security is, whilst undergoing such sentence, sentenced to imprisonment for an offence committed prior to the making of such order, the latter sentence shall commence immediately. ( 2 ) When a person already undergoing a sentence of imprisonment for life is sentenced on a subsequent conviction to imprisonment for a term or imprisonment for life, the subsequent sentence shall run concurrently with such previous sentence.
summary: A subsequent imprisonment normally starts after the earlier sentence ends, unless the court orders concurrent running. An exception applies when the earlier sentence was under section 141 for default security; the later sentence then starts immediately. For life sentences, any further sentence runs concurrently.
ingredients: Subsequent imprisonment commences after expiration of the earlier sentence unless the court orders concurrency
If earlier sentence is under section 141 for default of furnishing security, the later sentence commences immediately
When already serving life imprisonment, any subsequent sentence runs concurrently
exceptions: Provided that where a person who has been sentenced to imprisonment by an order under section 141 in default of furnishing security is, whilst undergoing such sentence, sentenced to imprisonment for an offence committed prior to the making of such order, the latter sentence shall commence immediately.
sourceReference: Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 (No. 46 of 2023), Gazette of India Extraordinary Part II Sec. 1, 25th December 2023, Section 468, Page 15

title: Set off of period of detention against sentence
description: Where an accused person has, on conviction, been sentenced to imprisonment for a term, not being imprisonment in default of payment of fine, the period of detention, if any, undergone by him during the investigation, inquiry or trial of the same case and before the date of such conviction, shall be set off against the term of imprisonment imposed on him on such conviction, and the liability of such person to undergo imprisonment on such conviction shall be restricted to the remainder, if any, of the term of imprisonment imposed on him: Provided that in cases referred to in section 476, such period of detention shall be set off against the period of fourteen years referred to in that section.
summary: Detention time spent during investigation, inquiry, or trial is deducted from the term of imprisonment awarded on conviction, reducing the remaining time to be served. In cases covered by section 476, the detention is set off against a fourteen‑year period specified therein.
ingredients: Detention during investigation, inquiry or trial is set off against the imposed term of imprisonment
Remaining liability is limited to the unserved portion of the sentence
In cases referred to in section 476, detention is set off against the fourteen‑year period mentioned in that section
exceptions: Provided that in cases referred to in section 476, such period of detention shall be set off against the period of fourteen years referred to in that section.
